theory-1.name: Hierarchical Episodic-Prospective Memory Theory (HEPMT)
theory-1.description: Agents that play text games using language models are best served by a hierarchical memory architecture that separates (1) detailed episodic traces (raw transcripts, recent observations, exact action sequences), (2) compressed schemas/semantic summaries (location types, object affordances, quest templates), and (3) prospective (intention/subgoal) memory holding short conditional plans. The hierarchy trades off recoverable detail for generalization and supports efficient retrieval, long-horizon credit assignment, and reuse of strategies under partial observability and limited retrieval bandwidth.
theory-1.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: Agents that play text games using language models are best served by a hierarchical memory architecture that separates (1) detailed episodic traces (raw transcripts, recent observations, exact action sequences), (2) compressed schemas/semantic summaries (location types, object affordances, quest templates), and (3) prospective (intention/subgoal) memory holding short conditional plans. The hierarchy trades off recoverable detail for generalization and supports efficient retrieval, long-horizon credit assignment, and reuse of strategies under partial observability and limited retrieval bandwidth.

theory_statements.theory_statement: For LM-based agents in text games with constrained memory-retrieval bandwidth, organizing memory into at least two levels — detailed episodic traces E and compressed schemas S — yields higher task performance P for a fixed total token/storage budget than a flat memory representation. Performance is approximately a monotonic function of an effective-memory utility M_eff = α|S| + β|E| where α > β > 0 (α and β are per-unit utility weights reflecting schema generality vs episodic specificity). When tasks demand long-horizon reasoning and recurring structures, marginal utility per unit of S exceeds that of E.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to agents whose controller is an autoregressive language model (transformer-family or similar) that (a) generates actions as text tokens, (b) experiences partially observable, language-described states (interactive fiction / text-adventure / TextWorld / Jericho-style environments), (c) operates under bounded per-step context/token budgets (limited retrieval/conditioning tokens), and (d) faces tasks requiring multi-step strategies across tens-to-hundreds of steps with recurring substructures.
special_cases: Environments with effectively no recurrence or shared structure (high-entropy stochastic descriptions) reduce the value of S; α ≈ β.
Extremely short episodes (≤ ~5 steps) or single-shot tasks where no generalization is useful make hierarchical indexing overhead detrimental.
When memory indexing/retrieval is near-perfect (very high precision), raw episodic capacity E can approach the utility of compressed schemas.

supporting_evidence.text: Memory-augmented neural architectures (Memory Networks, DNC) separate stored traces and retrieved content, improving QA and sequential tasks.
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and nearest-neighbor LMs show large early gains from a small, well-indexed retrieval set rather than a large unindexed cache.
Text game benchmarks (TextWorld, Jericho) and analyses show recurring room types, object affordances, and repeatable quest/recipe patterns that are amenable to schema abstraction.
Hierarchical RL (options, skills) demonstrates that abstractions enabling reuse improve long-horizon performance and sample efficiency.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Episodic vs semantic memory distinctions, retrieval-augmented LMs, and hierarchical RL are established; prior work shows retrieval and abstraction both help sequential tasks.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: HEPMT explicitly prescribes an LM-centered hierarchical memory with a quantitative effective-utility decomposition M_eff = α|S| + β|E| and the operational claim α > β for long-horizon text-game tasks under bounded retrieval budgets.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement synthesizes known components into a specific utility-based decomposition for LM agents in text games; the pieces exist but the combined, prescriptive law and domain focus are novel.
references: Sukhbaatar et al. (2015) End-to-End Memory Networks [episodic-style retrieval for language tasks]
Graves et al. (2016) Differentiable Neural Computer [content-addressable memory architectures]
Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) [retrieval improves LM outputs]
Côté et al. (2018) TextWorld: A Learning Environment for Text-based Games [benchmark and recurring structure]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Explicit storage of short prospective plans (conditional subgoal sequences of length L ≈ 2–8, with attached verification hooks) reduces expected re-planning cost CR roughly proportional to the fraction φ of repeated subgoals successfully retained and reused: CR ∝ (1 - φ). Storing prospective plans improves average-step efficiency and reduces repeated exploratory backtracking when plans are reliably verified upon retrieval.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies when agents encounter recurring multi-step subroutines (e.g., fetch-assemble-use workflows, door-unlock sequences) in text games, and when plan execution can be interrupted or requires recovery after exploration; assumes plans are stored with contextual triggers (state fingerprints) enabling targeted retrieval.
special_cases: If plan outcomes are highly non-deterministic or environment dynamics change between attempts, retained plans may mislead and increase wasted actions.
Adversarial paraphrasing or deliberate state obfuscation without robust state fingerprints reduces utility of stored plans.
Very long prospective plans (L≫8) become brittle and costly to maintain; effectiveness concentrates on short chunks.

supporting_evidence.text: Hierarchical RL/options literature shows reusable subpolicies reduce planning overhead; episodic control caches successful trajectories for faster reuse.
Human prospective memory research demonstrates that storing intentions reduces planning load and improves task completion rates in interrupted tasks.
Practical text-game agents that cache short successful action sequences or recipes empirically show efficiency gains on recurring quests.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Options and subpolicy reuse are standard in RL; simple action-sequence caching has been used in text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Formalizing prospective memory as a first-class memory partition for LM agents with a compact predictive relationship for re-planning cost (CR ∝ (1 - φ)) and prescribing verification hooks and conditional triggers tied to language fingerprints.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This extends existing hierarchical/subgoal reuse ideas into a memory engineering principle for LM-controller agents interacting through text.
references: Sutton et al. (1999) Between MDPs and Semi-MDPs: Options [subgoal reuse]
Blundell et al. (2016) Model-Free Episodic Control [trajectory caching]
Bower & Morrow (1990) Prospective memory studies [human evidence for intention storage]

theory_statements.theory_statement: When per-step retrieval tokens/time are constrained and retrieval precision does not improve with storage, marginal performance gain ΔP from increasing raw episodic memory |E| follows diminishing returns approximated by ΔP/Δ|E| ∝ 1/|E| (i.e., P ∝ log |E|) beyond an initial regime; allocating added capacity to compressed schemas S yields larger early returns when environment structure repeats.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to transformer-based LM agents with (a) hard per-decision context token limits and (b) retrieval systems whose precision (hit-rate for relevant entries) is approximately constant as |E| grows; environments must contain repeated structure so that compressed schemas capture recurring information.
special_cases: If indexing/retrieval precision improves with capacity (learned keys, hierarchical indices), returns can exceed logarithmic behavior.
If episodic entries are highly redundant, effective |E| is smaller and the law overestimates marginal benefits.
If retrieval budget is effectively unlimited, diminishing-returns constraint is relaxed.

supporting_evidence.text: Empirical RAG and nearest-neighbor cache studies show large early gains from small, well-chosen retrieval sets with tapering improvements as raw cache grows without better indexing.
General scaling/diminishing-return patterns for models and datasets (Kaplan et al.) and intuition from information theory about marginal information in random additional transcripts.
Practical constraints in deployed LMs: token windows, latency budgets, and cost-per-token which limit effective retrieval per step.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Scaling laws and caching performance curves are well-documented; retrieval literature notes tapering gains.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying a specific logarithmic-style diminishing-returns guideline to episodic memory for LM agents in bounded-retrieval text-game settings and deriving an actionable recommendation to prioritize compressed schema capacity.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an application/extension of existing scaling concepts into memory-augmentation engineering for LM-driven interactive agents.
references: Kaplan et al. (2020) Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models [diminishing returns in model/data scaling]
Khandelwal et al. (2020) Nearest Neighbor Language Models [cache/retrieval performance patterns]
Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) [benefit from small retrieved contexts]

new_predictions_likely: An LM agent equipped with a two-level memory (episodic transcripts + compressed schemas) will reach solutions in benchmark TextWorld quests with fewer environment interactions than a flat-memory LM with equal total token capacity.
Storing and verifying short prospective plan chunks (2–6 actions) will reduce average steps-to-completion and backtracking in multi-step fetch/sequence quests.
Given a fixed token budget per decision, allocating a majority of that budget to concise schemas rather than raw transcripts yields larger early performance gains on games with recurring structures.
new_predictions_unknown: A learned compression routine (e.g., VAE/contrastive compressor over episodes) that optimizes α and β in M_eff will generalize across diverse unseen text games to produce human-comparable schema abstractions.

Scheduling offline consolidation (periodic compression of episodic traces into schemas, mimicking sleep-like consolidation) will substantially improve cross-game transfer if scheduled at task-appropriate intervals.
HEPMT combined with chain-of-thought style internal LM planning will produce emergent multi-stage strategies that match or outperform symbolic planners on many text-game tasks.
negative_experiments: Ablate the schema layer: if a flat memory of equal token capacity consistently outperforms the hierarchical system across diverse text games, HEPMT's central utility decomposition is undermined.

Disable verification hooks on prospective plans: if plan retention no longer reduces re-planning cost or increases wasted actions, the Prospective Memory Principle's operational utility is questioned.
Apply adversarial paraphrasing to break similarity-based compression: if hierarchical summaries fail catastrophically while flat memory remains robust, the claim α > β is not generally valid.

unaccounted_for.text: A concrete, reliable, and general method for forming compressed schemas S from language-only interactions (no ground-truth state) that works across arbitrary text-game styles is unresolved.

Precise latency/token-tradeoff analyses for integrating HEPMT with very large LMs in real-time simulators (how many retrieval tokens per decision vs achievable throughput) require empirical calibration per deployment.

Mechanisms to maintain schema robustness under heavy paraphrasing/linguistic variability (e.g., adversarial phrasing) are not fully specified by HEPMT and need complementary paraphrase-invariant embeddings or alignment methods.

conflicting_evidence.text: Empirical RL studies have observed that in extremely high-novelty domains, learned abstractions can hurt performance (abstraction bias). In such domains compressed schemas may be unhelpful or detrimental.
If retrieval indexing is extremely poor or retrieval noise is high, adding a schema layer that depends on similarity/hits may not produce benefits and can add overhead.

special_cases: Domains with high, irreducible stochasticity where past episodes carry little predictive power for future outcomes.
Games whose descriptions systematically obfuscate or randomize surface wording (dynamic paraphrasing) unless robust invariant embeddings are used.
Situations with effectively unlimited per-decision context/compute budgets (research-only setups) where full-episode replay is feasible and compressed schemas have less relative benefit.

references: Sukhbaatar et al. (2015) End-to-End Memory Networks [episodic retrieval]
Graves et al. (2016) Differentiable Neural Computer [content-based memory]
Sutton et al. (1999) Between MDPs and Semi-MDPs: Options [hierarchical subgoals]
Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) [retrieval for LMs]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Separate strands: episodic memory models, retrieval-augmented LMs, and hierarchical RL each demonstrate parts of the claimed benefits.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: HEPMT integrates these into an LM-centric hierarchical memory design for text games and introduces the explicit effective-memory weighting (M_eff) and prospective-memory partition with verification hooks as prescriptive components.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Because HEPMT composes existing mechanisms into a domain-specific, quantitative recommendation set for LM agents in text games, it is closely related to prior work but extends it into a focused, novel engineering/theoretical proposal.

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

theory-2.name: Latent World-Model Memory Theory (LWMMT)
theory-2.description: Language-model agents can and should construct a compact latent model of the text-game world inside memory by distilling episodic traces into a generative/simulatable representation (latent states and transition dynamics). This internal world model, stored and updated in memory, enables cheap internal rollouts, counterfactual planning, and sample-efficient policy improvement even when environment interaction is expensive.
theory-2.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: Language-model agents can and should construct a compact latent model of the text-game world inside memory by distilling episodic traces into a generative/simulatable representation (latent states and transition dynamics). This internal world model, stored and updated in memory, enables cheap internal rollouts, counterfactual planning, and sample-efficient policy improvement even when environment interaction is expensive.

theory_statements.theory_statement: From a set of episodic transcripts and their outcomes, an LM-driven agent can learn a compact latent state-space Z and transition operator T_z such that predictive fidelity of textual next-observation distributions improves monotonically with the mutual information I(Z; history) up to representational capacity limits. Planning success S_planning correlates with predictive fidelity: S_planning ∝ F(T_z), where F is a calibrated fidelity metric (e.g., log-likelihood of held-out transitions under the latent model).
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to LM agents interacting with text-game environments where observations and actions are discrete language strings; assumes access to sufficient episodic data to estimate compact latent transitions and that internal simulation (rollouts) can be executed within the LM's compute budget.
special_cases: If the environment has unobservable latent variables that cannot be inferred from text (hidden RNG), predictive fidelity is fundamentally limited.
When language descriptions encode irreversible information (one-time secrets), latent models must encode non-Markovian histories or perform state augmentation to be predictive.

supporting_evidence.text: Model-based RL (world models, Dreamer, Ha & Schmidhuber) shows compact latent models enable efficient planning and policy learning in visual domains.
LMs can be fine-tuned to generate plausible continuations given latent conditioning; RAG/Retrieval approaches provide mechanisms to inject episodic content into model conditioning.
Text-game solution strategies often follow rules and templates that a compact latent world model can capture (object states, door open/closed, inventory), enabling accurate short-horizon simulation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: World models and internal simulation are established in model-based RL; LMs have been used with retrieval and conditioning to approximate latent states.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying compact latent world-model construction explicitly as a memory-layer that LM agents build and use for internal rollouts in text-only interactive settings, with a fidelity→planning-success proportionality claim.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an extension of model-based RL ideas into the LM/text-game domain, focusing on memory as the persistent storage of the latent model and linking fidelity to planning success.

theory_statements.theory_statement: The utility of episodic memory for planning scales with (a) the accuracy of retrieval of context-relevant episodes and (b) the ease of mapping retrieved episodic content into latent states for simulation. Formally, effective planning throughput T_eff ≈ R_prec × M_map × C_roll where R_prec is retrieval precision for relevant episodes, M_map is the mapping fidelity to latent states, and C_roll is per-rollout compute budget; low R_prec or M_map collapses the benefit of stored episodes regardless of storage size.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies where episodic retrieval is used as the primary input to construct or update latent states for internal planning, in LM agents with retrieval and simulation capacities constrained by token/compute budgets.
special_cases: If a strong learned encoder maps raw text to latent states without retrieval (direct compression), retrieval precision is less critical.
When retrieval is perfect but mapping to latent states is highly lossy, stored episodes remain of little planning value.

supporting_evidence.text: RAG and dense retrieval literature demonstrates that retrieval precision strongly determines downstream generation quality.
World model approaches rely on high-fidelity encoders from observations to latent states; mapping loss degrades control performance.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: The importance of retrieval precision and latent encoding is recognized in retrieval-augmented NLP and model-based RL.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Explicit multiplicative coupling formulation (T_eff ≈ R_prec × M_map × C_roll) contextualized for LM-driven planning using memory-built latent models in text games.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This expresses known dependencies in a compact, actionable multiplicative form tailored to LM agents that use memory for internal simulation.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Periodic offline consolidation (batched compression of episodic traces into latent/world-model updates and schemas) improves sample efficiency and transfer. The marginal benefit of consolidation is greatest when performed after encountering clusters of related episodic evidence (high local mutual information) and diminishes if done too frequently (noisy compressions) or too rarely (lost opportunities for early reuse).
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies when agents can perform offline compute between episodes or during low-interaction intervals and when the environment contains recurring patterns that benefit from aggregation into latent model parameters or schemas.
special_cases: If the agent has continuous, unlimited online compute with streaming updates matching environment nonstationarity, explicit offline consolidation provides little extra benefit.
If episodic data is extremely noisy or adversarial, consolidation may overfit and harm performance.

supporting_evidence.text: Biological consolidation (sleep) and machine learning practices (mini-batch training, experience replay) show offline aggregation improves generalization and transfer.
World model training and replay buffers in RL benefit from periodic model updates and offline training for stability.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Experience replay and periodic model updates are standard in RL; biological consolidation analogies are frequently invoked.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Framing consolidation as a scheduled memory-compression operation for LM agents in text games with a predictive claim about clustering-by-mutual-information maximizing marginal benefit.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This adapts consolidation concepts to a specific LM-memory-world-model workflow and prescribes conditions under which consolidation is maximally useful.

new_predictions_likely: An LM agent that learns a compact latent world model from episodic traces and uses internal rollouts will require fewer environment interactions to solve new quests than a purely model-free LM agent with equivalent compute.
Improving retrieval precision (R_prec) for episodes that inform latent-state updates will produce multiplicative gains in planning throughput and success rates.
Periodic offline consolidation into latent model parameters after encountering clustered evidence will increase transfer to similar quests encountered later in a training stream.
new_predictions_unknown: A universal latent representation learned on a sufficiently large and diverse set of text games will transfer out-of-the-box to new text-adventure categories, allowing zero-shot internal simulation that achieves high planning fidelity.
Internal rollouts using LMs conditioned on latent states will discover emergent strategies (multi-step subroutines) that human designers did not anticipate, rivaling handcrafted symbolic planners.

Direct end-to-end training of LM controllers plus a differentiable latent world-model memory (with backprop across simulated rollouts) will outperform modular pipelines combining separate retrieval, compression, and planning modules.
negative_experiments: If an agent that constructs latent models from episodic traces fails to outperform a non-model-based baseline across a wide variety of text games (with matched compute), the central claim that latent-model memory aids planning is undermined.
If improving retrieval precision for episodes produces no measurable improvement in planning throughput S_planning, the multiplicative coupling hypothesis is false in practice.
If offline consolidation consistently reduces transfer (overfitting), the scheduling principle is invalid or poorly specified.

unaccounted_for.text: Practical methods for learning latent spaces Z from text-only interactions that reliably capture non-trivial state (inventory, dynamic object states) without environment meta-signals remain an engineering challenge.
Trade-offs between fidelity of latent model and computational cost of internal rollouts (how many rollouts are useful under real token/latency budgets) need empirical calibration per game class.

conflicting_evidence.text: In highly stochastic or partially observable text games, compact Markovian latent models may fail to represent necessary information, and model-free approaches or richer memory encodings may outperform latent-model planners.

special_cases: Games with unobservable mechanics or strong hidden RNG bias that cannot be inferred from text render latent modeling ineffective.
Scenarios where token/latency budgets preclude useful internal rollouts even if a good latent model exists.

references: Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) Recurrent World Models Facilitate Policy Learning [world models enabling cheap internal rollouts]
Hafner et al. (2019/2020) Dreamer / PlaNet [latent-model-based planning]
Lewis et al. (2020) RAG [retrieval used to condition generation]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Model-based RL with learned latent world models is well established in continuous/visual domains; retrieval-augmented LMs show retrieval can provide episodic grounding.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: LWMMT adapts latent-model construction explicitly into the memory layer of LM agents in text-only interactive domains, and links predictive fidelity to measurable planning success with deployment-aware constraints.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: LWMMT repurposes world-model ideas into the LM-memory-text-game stack; while conceptually related to prior work, the domain transfer and memory-as-latent-model framing are distinctive.

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

theory-3.name: Uncertainty-Driven Differentiable Memory Control (UDDMC) — REVISED
theory-3.description: A unified, differentiable control policy for episodic key-value memory in language-model (LM) agents playing text games. UDDMC gates writes based on probabilistic surprise multiplied by an estimator of expected future utility, modulated by an explicit storage cost; it controls retrieval concentration via a learned mapping from epistemic uncertainty to softmax temperature; and it implements end-to-end differentiable reads/writes so reward gradients backpropagate to memory-addressing and content. The result is sparse, high-utility memory contents, focused retrieval when the agent is uncertain, and improved long-horizon credit assignment for textual decision sequences.
theory-3.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: A unified, differentiable control policy for episodic key-value memory in language-model (LM) agents playing text games. UDDMC gates writes based on probabilistic surprise multiplied by an estimator of expected future utility, modulated by an explicit storage cost; it controls retrieval concentration via a learned mapping from epistemic uncertainty to softmax temperature; and it implements end-to-end differentiable reads/writes so reward gradients backpropagate to memory-addressing and content. The result is sparse, high-utility memory contents, focused retrieval when the agent is uncertain, and improved long-horizon credit assignment for textual decision sequences.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Write o_t to episodic key-value memory iff Surprise(o_t) * EstimatedExpectedUtilityGain(o_t) > lambda * StorageCost, where Surprise(o_t) = -log p_model(o_t | context); EstimatedExpectedUtilityGain is a learned (or bootstrapped) estimate of the marginal increase in future return enabled by retaining o_t; lambda is a resource-cost scalar. The write operation is implemented as a differentiable gating scalar in [0,1] (soft write) so gradients from downstream rewards can update both content encodings and the utility estimator.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to transformer-based LM agents interacting with text-based interactive environments (e.g., TextWorld/Jericho-style games) where (1) observations are token sequences, (2) memory capacity or per-step retrieval token budget is constrained, and (3) the agent is trained with gradient-based methods (supervised fine-tuning and/or policy gradients). Not intended for purely symbolic planners without learnable likelihood models, nor for settings where observation likelihoods are unavailable or meaningless (e.g., adversarial token streams without a trainable model).
special_cases: Purely random environments: Surprise is high but EstimatedExpectedUtilityGain is near-zero; the multiplicative rule prevents wasteful writes.
Zero or negligible storage cost (lambda -> 0): gating collapses toward frequent writes (degenerates to write-every-step policy).
When the utility estimator is poorly trained early in learning, use curriculum (bootstrapped TD targets or auxiliary supervised labels) or conservative thresholds to avoid pathological writes.
Discrete write constraints (hard capacity) can be approximated by soft gates + periodic hard pruning based on learned importance.

supporting_evidence.text: Neuroscience: novelty/surprise signals bias hippocampal encoding and long-term consolidation (novelty-triggered memory formation).
Reinforcement learning: prioritized experience replay stores and samples transitions by priority (e.g., TD-error) to focus learning on high-utility experiences (Schaul et al. 2015).
Episodic control: model-free episodic caches store high-return episodes for rapid retrieval (Blundell et al. 2016).
Likelihood-based novelty measures are commonly available from LMs (next-token probabilities) and have been used to detect surprising inputs.
Complementary learning systems theory: consolidation transfers episodic traces into more stable representations when they are behaviorally significant (McClelland et al. 1995).
Practical LM + memory systems (RAG, RETRO) demonstrate gains from selective retrieval and storage of relevant text chunks, suggesting write selection matters for performance under retrieval budgets.
Temporal-difference/value estimation provides standard mechanisms to estimate marginal utility of stored experiences (Sutton & Barto).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Surprise/novelty-driven encoding and priority-based storage policies exist in neuroscience and RL (novelty bonuses, prioritized replay, episodic control). LMs provide next-token likelihoods usable as surprise proxies.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The explicit multiplicative gate combining probabilistic surprise with a learned ExpectedUtilityGain, scaled by an explicit storage-cost lambda, implemented as a differentiable soft-write gate tightly integrated with transformer-based LM agents in text-game settings.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Synthesis of existing components into a differentiable gate that is directly trainable end-to-end with LM logits and reward gradients—applies known concepts but introduces a concrete, trainable rule and implementation constraints for language agents.
references: Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [priority-based replay]
Blundell et al. (2016) Model-Free Episodic Control [episodic caching]
McClelland et al. (1995) Why there are complementary learning systems in the hippocampus and neocortex [consolidation]
Lewis et al. (2020) RAG: Retrieval-Augmented Generation [selective retrieval in LMs]
Borgeaud et al. (2022) RETRO: Cutting Transformer Retrieval [retrieval-augmented LM]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Memory retrieval attention scores use softmax(key·query / tau(u)), where tau(u) is a learned, differentiable temperature function of epistemic uncertainty u about the current state; tau(u) monotonically decreases with u, producing sharper (more concentrated) retrieval distributions when the agent is more uncertain, and broader retrieval (higher tau) when the agent is confident to expose richer context.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies where (1) memory retrieval is implemented by differentiable attention over key-value stores, (2) per-step token budget limits the number of retrieved tokens that can condition the LM, and (3) an epistemic uncertainty estimate is available (e.g., ensemble variance, MC-dropout variance, logits-entropy calibrated by augmentations). Not applicable when no credible uncertainty estimate can be computed or when retrieval must be hard/one-hot by design.
special_cases: Poor or adversarial uncertainty estimates may cause overconfident or underconfident retrieval; calibration procedures or robust estimators (ensembles, deep ensembles) are recommended.
Very low tau (near-zero) can cause brittle single-memory reliance; add entropy regularization or floor temperature to avoid collapse.
When retrieval budget is large (effectively unlimited), temperature control is less impactful and can be neutralized.

supporting_evidence.text: Softmax temperature controls distribution concentration; used widely in attention and sampling.

Uncertainty-aware attention and selection have precedent in active learning and some memory/attention works; LM uncertainty signals (ensembles, MC-dropout, deep ensembles) are practical and informative for calibration.
Retrieval-augmented LMs (RAG, RETRO) show the importance of selecting high-quality contexts — concentration on few high-value contexts is beneficial when retrieval is costly.
Calibration and ensemble techniques (Lakshminarayanan et al. 2017; Gal & Ghahramani 2016) provide operational uncertainty metrics suitable for tau(u).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Temperature scaling and uncertainty estimation are established; attention mechanisms and retrieval-augmented LMs use scoring functions.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A continuous differentiable mapping from LM epistemic uncertainty to retrieval temperature tau, used as an operational knob to trade off concentration vs. breadth under token budgets for text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The components exist separately; coupling uncertainty to retrieval temperature as a learned differentiable module targeted to token-limited gameplay is novel and actionable.
references: Gal & Ghahramani (2016) Dropout as a Bayesian Approximation [MC-dropout uncertainty]
Lakshminarayanan et al. (2017) Deep Ensembles [practical uncertainty]
Lewis et al. (2020) RAG [retrieval selection]
Borgeaud et al. (2022) RETRO [retrieval-augmented LM]

theory_statements.statement_name: Differentiable Credit Assignment Through Memory Replay
theory_statements.theory_statement: Making write and read operations soft/differentiable allows gradients from delayed textual rewards to flow back through retrieval attention weights and write gates to the original stored encodings; this lowers variance of long-horizon credit signals and enables the model to learn which observations causally influenced future returns, improving sample-efficiency over purely heuristic, non-differentiable replay scheduling.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to agents trained with gradient-based learning (supervised fine-tuning, actor-critic/policy gradients with reparameterization where possible) and using soft attention addressing in memory modules (e.g., Neural Turing Machine, DNC, Memory Networks). Not directly applicable to hard-coded episodic buffers with discrete addresses unless relaxed differentiably.
special_cases: On-policy RL with non-differentiable sampling (e.g., discrete action sampling without reparameterization) still requires variance reduction techniques (baselines/advantage).
Very long horizons can make end-to-end differentiation computationally heavy; truncated backpropagation through time or episodic credit proxies may be necessary.
Where reward sparsity is extreme, gradients through memory alone may be insufficient without additional auxiliary losses (behavior cloning, value prediction).

supporting_evidence.text: Differentiable memory architectures (NTM, DNC, End-to-end Memory Networks) allow gradient flow through read/write addressing and improve tasks requiring long-term dependencies.

Non-differentiable prioritized replay requires heuristics to assign importance and cannot directly receive gradient signals from future returns; prioritized replay still effective but limited for direct credit assignment.

Empirical RL literature shows that structured memory and attention mechanisms can reduce variance and improve learning on long-horizon tasks when integrated with gradients (Graves et al. 2016; Kaiser et al. works).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Differentiable memory architectures and prioritized replay are established; applying gradient-based credit assignment through attention/read-write mechanisms is known in sequence modeling.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Framing differentiable read/write gating specifically as the mechanism for long-horizon credit-assignment in LM agents for text games, combined with the uncertainty-driven write/retrieval controls of UDDMC.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Integrates known differentiable-memory advantages into a reward-driven LM context and prescribes concrete mechanisms for credit flow tied to surprise and utility gating.
references: Graves et al. (2016) Differentiable Neural Computer [differentiable memory]
Kaiser et al. (2017) Learning to remember rare events [memory and rare events]
Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [non-differentiable prioritization]

new_predictions_likely: Under realistic token-retrieval budgets, UDDMC will store a subset of observations <30% of timesteps while matching or exceeding task performance of write-every-step baselines in procedurally generated text games (e.g., TextWorld).

Agents using uncertainty-to-temperature retrieval will recover from ambiguous descriptions faster (fewer failed attempts) than fixed-temperature retrieval, because sharper retrieval under uncertainty surfaces decisive episodic evidence.

End-to-end differentiable gating will reduce variance in policy-gradient estimates for long-horizon quests, improving sample efficiency relative to agents relying solely on prioritized non-differentiable replay.
new_predictions_unknown: A learned ExpectedUtilityGain estimator can be reliably bootstrapped from sparse episodic rewards across diverse games using auxiliary losses (value prediction, hindsight objectives) and will consistently outperform simple heuristics across curricula.
Combining UDDMC with symbolic short-term state trackers (explicit inventory/location variables) will approach near-optimal sample efficiency on large classes of procedurally-generated text games.

Differentiable gates can be trained to compress episodic traces into compact textual programs/macros that the LM can later 'execute' internally; if true, this could enable bridging symbolic planning with language-model reasoning.
negative_experiments: If Surprise * EstimatedExpectedUtilityGain gating fails to reduce memory size without harming performance across many games compared to write-every-step baselines, the utility of the multiplicative write rule is undermined.

If uncertainty-based temperature control produces more failures or no benefit vs. fixed-temperature attention under realistic uncertainty estimators, the retrieval-control law is called into question.

If gradients through memory operations do not lower policy-gradient variance or improve long-horizon credit assignment compared to strong prioritized replay baselines, the claimed advantage of differentiable credit-assignment would be weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: Practical, low-variance algorithms for estimating ExpectedExpectedUtilityGain from single or few episodes in extremely sparse-reward text games are not fully specified; reliable off-policy estimators or auxiliary supervision are likely required.

Interactions between differentiable memory gating and discrete decoding constraints of large frozen LMs (when LM weights are not fully fine-tunable) are implementation-dependent and need more concrete methods (e.g., prefix-tuning, adapter layers).
Scaling to very large episodic memories (millions of key-value slots) requires efficient approximate nearest-neighbor/key hashing — UDDMC specifies control but not those engineering choices.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some empirical reports show prioritized non-differentiable replay or tabular episodic caches outperform differentiable memory architectures in noisy RL tasks due to robustness and computational simplicity.
Differentiable memory modules sometimes struggle to scale to very long horizons or to generalize out-of-distribution compared to explicit symbolic memory or graph-based state trackers.

special_cases: Domains where surprise correlates poorly with downstream task relevance (e.g., stylistic, irrelevant novelty) require additional filters such as reward-conditioned surprise (surprise weighted by reward-relevance) or learned relevance classifiers.
When compute limits forbid backpropagating through memory (e.g., frozen production LMs), hybrid approaches that train gating separately or via actor-critic critics may be needed.
In multi-agent or adversarial text games where agents may inject misleading but surprising observations, reliability of surprise signals is compromised and adversarial-robust gating is required.

references: Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [priority-based writes]
Graves et al. (2016) Differentiable Neural Computer [differentiable read/write]
Blundell et al. (2016) Model-Free Episodic Control [episodic caching of high-value trajectories]
Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) [selective retrieval in LMs]
Borgeaud et al. (2022) RETRO: Retrieval-Enhanced Transformer [retrieval-augmented LMs]
McClelland et al. (1995) Complementary Learning Systems [consolidation and novelty]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Elements exist: novelty/surprise-driven encoding, prioritized replay, episodic control, differentiable memory architectures, and retrieval-augmented LMs.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The UDDMC formalizes a differentiable write gate combining surprise and learned ExpectedUtilityGain with an explicit cost scalar, plus a principled mapping from epistemic uncertainty to retrieval temperature for token-budgeted retrieval—packaged specifically for LM agents in text-game settings.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: UDDMC synthesizes and extends existing mechanisms into a coherent, trainable control policy for memory-augmented LM agents; components are known but their integration and differentiable gating targeted to LMs and text games is novel.

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

theory-4.name: Hierarchical Episodic-Semantic Memory (HESM) for Language-Model Agents
theory-4.description: A structured memory architecture that separates short-term working memory (recent tokens and actions), an episodic store of sparse, time-stamped experiences, and a semantic graph (grounded facts and abstractions). HESM prescribes consolidation rules (when and how episodic traces are abstracted into semantic graph nodes), retrieval arbitration across levels based on task-context and utility, and grounded alignment between textual episodes and semantic nodes so that LMs can generalize past episodic outcomes to novel but structurally similar quests.
theory-4.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_name: Hierarchical Episodic-Semantic Memory (HESM) for Language-Model Agents
components.theory_description: A structured memory architecture that separates short-term working memory (recent tokens and actions), an episodic store of sparse, time-stamped experiences, and a semantic graph (grounded facts and abstractions). HESM prescribes consolidation rules (when and how episodic traces are abstracted into semantic graph nodes), retrieval arbitration across levels based on task-context and utility, and grounded alignment between textual episodes and semantic nodes so that LMs can generalize past episodic outcomes to novel but structurally similar quests.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Memory is organized into (1) Working Memory (WM): high-bandwidth short-lived token buffer for immediate context (seconds/steps), (2) Episodic Store (ES): compressed key-value traces of salient experiences with timestamps and metadata (minutes/episodes), and (3) Semantic Graph (SG): durable, schema-like abstractions (facts, affordances, rules) created by consolidating repeated or high-utility episodic patterns. Each tier uses different encoding fidelity and access patterns: WM preserves full token context, ES stores compressed encodings and provenance, SG stores symbolic/graphified summaries.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to LM agents solving sequential text-based tasks requiring both immediate context (short-term effects) and long-term generalization across episodes (procedurally generated quests), particularly when the environment exhibits repeated structure and reusable affordances. Not required for single-step question-answering tasks.
special_cases: In purely one-shot tasks with no repeated structure, semantic consolidation yields little benefit and can be de-emphasized.
If storage is extremely constrained, ES may be pruned aggressively and SG built only for the most frequent/valuable abstractions.

supporting_evidence.text: Cognitive neuroscience posits complementary roles for working memory, episodic memory, and semantic memory with consolidation processes (McClelland et al. 1995; Tulving).
Memory hierarchies in ML (short-term caches, episodic replay, learned embeddings/knowledge graphs) improve both fast adaptation and long-term generalization.

LM systems augmented with external knowledge graphs or symbolic memories can perform structured reasoning and generalize across contexts beyond single episodic recall (various knowledge-enhanced LM works).
Text-game environments often contain recurring object affordances and rule sets that benefit from abstracted semantic facts (e.g., 'the key opens the chest' archetypes).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Memory hierarchies are a long-standing idea in neuroscience and ML; hybrid symbolic-connectionist memory work exists.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Explicit operationalization for LM agents in text games that prescribes consolidation triggers, metadata-rich episodic encodings for provenance, and retrieval arbitration policies across tiers based on utility and context.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Builds on known hierarchical-memory ideas but provides a concrete, actionable architecture and rules targeted at LM agents engaging in procedurally generated textual environments.
references: McClelland et al. (1995) Complementary Learning Systems [consolidation]
Sukhbaatar et al. (2015) End-to-end Memory Networks [episodic memory for LMs]
Lewis et al. (2020) RAG [external retrieval architectures]

theory_statements.theory_statement: An episodic trace e is consolidated into a semantic node if (A) its observed utility across episodes exceeds U_thresh (average episodic return contribution), and (B) its structural form recurs across k >= k_min distinct episodes or can be abstracted via schema induction; consolidation is accompanied by a linking mechanism storing provenance pointers to representative episodic traces.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies during offline or low-activity phases (analogous to sleep/replay) or during explicit consolidation steps between episodes. Requires episodic metadata (counts, return estimates) and a mechanism for structural abstraction (clustering, pattern mining).
special_cases: Single highly valuable one-off episodes may be preserved in ES but may not be consolidated if recurrence is absent; however, an override can consolidate if estimated utility is extremely high.
Noisy environments with spurious recurrence can create misleading semantic nodes; require statistical significance filtering or human-in-the-loop validation.

supporting_evidence.text: Neuroscience evidence of offline replay and selective consolidation of memories with behavioral relevance.
ML approaches that mine frequent patterns or cluster episodic traces can produce reusable subroutines/abstractions improving generalization.
Experience replay and offline RL use aggregated statistics across experiences to update global policies and value functions—consolidation mirrors this aggregation for memory abstraction.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Consolidation ideas (replay, clustering frequent patterns) exist in neuroscience and ML.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Explicit dual-threshold (utility + recurrence) consolidation rule with provenance linking for LM agents to ensure traceability and replay-based reification of semantic nodes.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Translates abstract consolidation concepts into actionable rules for LM-based agents operating on textual episodic traces.
references: McClelland et al. (1995) Complementary Learning Systems [consolidation/replay]
Sutton & Barto (2018) Reinforcement Learning [aggregating experiences to learn value]
Ravula et al. (2021) Schema induction works [pattern abstraction]

theory_statements.theory_statement: At query-time, retrieval arbitration scores candidate items from each tier using a utility-weighted combination: Score = alpha(context_relevance) * Beta_tier * Importance, where Beta_tier encodes preferred tier given task horizon (e.g., WM high for immediate next-token prediction; ES preferred for episodic recall; SG favored for generalized planning); a learned gating network chooses alpha and Beta_tier conditioned on LM state and uncertainty.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies when multiple tiers are present and retrieval budgets require choosing which tier to draw from. The arbitration mechanism must be differentiable when end-to-end training is used.
special_cases: When WM contains sufficient tokens, arbitration can defer to WM and ignore ES/SG to save retrieval tokens.
When SG contains conflicting or outdated facts, arbitration must prefer recent ES traces or use provenance for disambiguation.

supporting_evidence.text: Hybrid memory systems in AI often require a controller to decide which memory to consult based on context and cost (e.g., gating in hierarchical caches).
In human cognition, strategic retrieval draws from different memory systems depending on task demands (episodic recall vs. semantic knowledge).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Memory arbitration/controllers are known in systems architectures and cognitive models.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete parameterization (Beta_tier and learned alpha) tailored to LM agent state and uncertainty, designed specifically to manage token-budgeted retrieval across WM/ES/SG in text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Implements known arbitration within the specific constraints of LMs and text games, providing an operational approach to tier selection.
references: Sukhbaatar et al. (2015) Memory Networks [memory controllers]
McClelland et al. (1995) Complementary Learning Systems [systems interaction]

new_predictions_likely: Agents using HESM will generalize faster to new game levels that reuse object affordances (e.g., keys/chests) because semantic graph abstractions capture reusable rules.
Consolidation-by-Repetition-and-Utility will reduce episodic memory footprint while preserving task performance: only recurring, high-utility episodes are converted into semantic nodes.
Cross-tier arbitration will reduce unnecessary retrieval from large episodic stores, improving latency while maintaining accuracy by preferring WM/SG as appropriate.
new_predictions_unknown: Semantic graph induction from noisy textual episodic traces can be automated robustly across diverse game grammars and will enable zero-shot transfer to novel quest structures.
Linking provenance pointers from SG nodes back to representative episodic traces will allow targeted replay that accelerates fine-tuning on new tasks more than generic replay.
A hybrid HESM combined with programmatic compression (see PEC theory) will enable agents to discover reusable macros that rival hand-coded strategies.
negative_experiments: If forced separation into WM/ES/SG yields no long-term generalization benefits over a flat episodic store for procedurally-generated text games, the hierarchical separation is unnecessary.
If SG nodes induced by consolidation regularly produce incorrect generalizations that harm performance, the consolidation rule is invalidated or requires stronger constraints.
If cross-tier arbitration cannot be reliably learned and defaults to always querying the largest store (ES) without benefit, the arbitration law lacks practical value.

unaccounted_for.text: Practical algorithms for robust semantic induction from noisy natural-language episodic traces across many game grammars are not fully specified.
How to maintain/refresh SG nodes when environment rules change or games are non-stationary requires further mechanisms (decay, re-evaluation).

conflicting_evidence.text: Some ML systems demonstrate that flat large-scale retrieval stores (dense vector indexes) combined with end-to-end fine-tuning can match or exceed more structured memory hierarchies in certain tasks.

special_cases: Environments with extremely high variability and little reuse make SG construction either impossible or counterproductive.
Domains with adversarially poisoned episodic traces (e.g., multi-agent adversarial settings) require provenance verification before consolidation.

references: McClelland et al. (1995) Complementary Learning Systems [neuroscience hierarchy]
Sukhbaatar et al. (2015) End-to-end Memory Networks [episodic memory for language tasks]
Lewis et al. (2020) RAG [retrieval augmentation and external knowledge]
Borgeaud et al. (2022) RETRO [retrieval-augmented LM architectures]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Hierarchical memory concepts exist in neuroscience and computing; retrieval-augmented models show benefits for external memories.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: HESM operationalizes consolidation thresholds, provenance linking, and a learnable cross-tier arbitration mechanism explicitly designed for LM agents in interactive text environments.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: HESM refines existing conceptual hierarchies into an architecture and set of rules tailored to LM agents with token budgets and episodic/textual provenance needs.

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

theory-5.name: Predictive World-Model Memory (PWM)
theory-5.description: Agents augment transformer language models with a compact, learned predictive transition model stored in memory (a world-model memory). This memory stores generalized transition primitives (compressed state-action -> predicted next-observation / latent_delta) that an LM can query to simulate outcomes, enabling multi-step lookahead, counterfactual reasoning, and sample-efficient planning in text games without full environment interaction.
theory-5.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: Agents augment transformer language models with a compact, learned predictive transition model stored in memory (a world-model memory). This memory stores generalized transition primitives (compressed state-action -> predicted next-observation / latent_delta) that an LM can query to simulate outcomes, enabling multi-step lookahead, counterfactual reasoning, and sample-efficient planning in text games without full environment interaction.

theory_statements.theory_statement: A compact predictive memory M that maps (compressed_observation, canonicalized_action) -> predicted_observation_delta or next_observation allows an LM to approximate multi-step rollouts with cumulative error that grows sublinearly with rollout length if M is trained on representative trajectories and the environment has moderate determinism; querying M reduces required environment interactions for planning by factor proportional to the predictive accuracy A (higher A => larger reduction).
theory_statements.domain_scope: Text-based interactive fiction and parser/text-adventure games where observations and action effects can be represented as symbolic/textual observations and where environment stochasticity is low-to-moderate; training data includes agent trajectories with paired (obs,action,next_obs) transitions or self-play rollouts.
special_cases: Highly stochastic environments or ones with hidden adversarial dynamics degrade predictive model accuracy and thus reduce utility.
When observations are extremely long, compression into compact representations is necessary; if compression discards essential state, model predictions fail.

supporting_evidence.text: World-model approaches in RL (e.g., latent world models) let agents plan with fewer environment interactions by learning compact transition dynamics.
Transformer LMs can predict next-token / next-sentence distributions and thus are capable of learning text transition dynamics given enough data.
Text-game frameworks commonly provide repeated canonical action effects (e.g., 'open door' usually yields 'the door is open'), which can be learned as transitions.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: World-models in RL (Ha & Schmidhuber 2018 and follow-ups) compress transitions to allow planning; language models perform next-token prediction and have been used to model environment dynamics in language spaces.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Explicitly storing learned text-transition primitives in a memory module that an LM queries for simulated rollouts in text games, together with the claim of sublinear error growth and quantitative reduction in environment interactions proportional to model predictive accuracy.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Builds on model-based RL and LM prediction capabilities but packages them into a retrieval-accessible world-model memory for LMs acting in text-game environments.
references: Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) 'World Models' [latent predictive models for planning]
Côté et al. (2018) 'TextWorld' [text-game benchmarks where canonical transitions exist]
Khandelwal et al. (2020) 'Nearest Neighbor Language Models' [cache/retrieval for language prediction]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Given predictive memory M with per-step prediction error e (expected distance between predicted and true next observation in an observation-similarity metric), the expected error of an n-step simulated rollout grows approximately as E_rollout(n) = O(n^p * e) with p in [0.5,1] in typical structured text games (p<1 because symbolic constraints and canonical outcomes correct cumulative drift); the agent's effective planning depth is the largest n such that E_rollout(n) remains below a task-dependent planning-threshold T_plan.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Text games with structural constraints (objects, canonical verbs, deterministic or low-entropy outcomes) where an observation-similarity metric (e.g., semantic embedding distance or token-overlap) meaningfully correlates with downstream decision quality.
special_cases: If the game contains branching hidden state not recoverable from text outputs, error may grow linearly or faster (p≈1).
If memory M includes symbolic constraints or invariants (e.g., object-ownership algebra), p can be <0.5 for some environments.

supporting_evidence.text: Empirical work on learned simulators shows cumulative model error grows with rollout length; however, structural priors and symbolic constraints can mitigate drift.
Many text games enforce invariants (e.g., an object cannot be in two places simultaneously), which can be exploited by a learned model to reduce error growth.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Model-based RL analyses of compounding model error in rollouts are established; world-model work formalizes error accumulation.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying an explicit approximate scaling law for rollout error in text-game language-space and connecting it to an LM's effective planning depth via a task threshold T_plan.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Extends existing model-error analyses into the textual / symbolic domain and makes testable predictions about planning depth.
references: Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) 'World Models' [model-based rollouts, error accumulation]
Côté et al. (2018) 'TextWorld' [structured text-game mechanics]

new_predictions_likely: An LM augmented with a learned predictive memory will require 30–70% fewer environment interactions to reach a fixed performance threshold on deterministic TextWorld tasks than a comparable model-free LM agent.

Short multi-step rollouts (2–5 steps) using the predictive memory will improve decision quality (measured by success rate per episode) more than single-step caching strategies in environments with predictable intermediate consequences.
new_predictions_unknown: A predictive memory learned in one family of text games (e.g., adventure puzzles) will permit zero-shot planning transfer to a different game family (e.g., mystery exploration) if both share deep structural invariants represented in memory primitives.
Combining predictive memory rollouts with symbolic constraint solvers extracted from memory will permit reliable long-horizon planning (tens of steps) in some previously intractable text games.
negative_experiments: If augmenting an LM with a predictive memory trained on large trajectories does not reduce required environment interactions or planning quality on deterministic TextWorld tasks, the PWM assumptions about learnable transition primitives are invalid.
If simulated rollouts of length 2 produce worse decisions than single-step cached outcomes in most tasks, compounding error or poor memory retrieval is undermining the approach.

unaccounted_for.text: How to automatically choose the compression granularity for observations to optimize tradeoff between predictive accuracy and memory compactness.

conflicting_evidence.text: Environments with substantial hidden state not revealed in text outputs (e.g., internal flags) can make learned rollouts misleading and reduce PWM utility.

special_cases: Games that intentionally obfuscate state via stochastic responses or adversarial phrasing reduce predictive memory utility.
When training data lacks coverage of rare but critical transitions, memory-based rollouts can miss important failure modes.

references: Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) 'World Models' [latent predictive models for planning]
Côté et al. (2018) 'TextWorld' [text-game structure and benchmarks]
Khandelwal et al. (2020) 'Nearest Neighbor Language Models' [retrieval to improve LM prediction]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Latent world-models and retrieval-augmented LMs are established; model-based planning reduces sample complexity in RL.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Framing a small, queryable predictive-memory specifically for transformer LMs acting in text-game language-space, with explicit scaling relations for rollout error and planning depth.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: The theory synthesizes model-based RL and retrieval-augmented language modeling into a specific module (PWM) for text-game planning.

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

theory-6.name: Hierarchical Episodic-Semantic Memory (HESM)
theory-6.description: LM agents should maintain a structured memory with two cooperative layers: (1) episodic traces (detailed transcripts of recent interactions) and (2) compressed semantic abstractions (generalized facts, rules, object affordances). Retrieval policies mediate between them: episodic retrieval for exact replays and recovery, semantic retrieval for generalization and planning. Together these layers enable both one-shot recovery from surprises and transfer across similar games.
theory-6.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: LM agents should maintain a structured memory with two cooperative layers: (1) episodic traces (detailed transcripts of recent interactions) and (2) compressed semantic abstractions (generalized facts, rules, object affordances). Retrieval policies mediate between them: episodic retrieval for exact replays and recovery, semantic retrieval for generalization and planning. Together these layers enable both one-shot recovery from surprises and transfer across similar games.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Frequent co-occurrences across episodic traces are compressed into semantic facts (e.g., 'turning on lamp yields light in room') via an abstraction operator A that maps sets of aligned transcripts to canonicalized rules; the rate of successful compression depends on repetition frequency f and semantic-salience s such that probability of abstraction P_abstract ≈ 1 - exp(-k * f * s) for some k>0.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Text games and interactive text environments where repeated patterns occur across episodes (multiple runs or different rooms/scenarios) and where transcripts can be aligned into templated event structures.
special_cases: Singleton events with high importance (rare but critical) may be missed by frequency-based compression and require dedicated attention mechanisms.
Spurious co-occurrences due to coincidental language patterns may produce incorrect abstractions if not validated.

supporting_evidence.text: Human memory research distinguishes episodic traces from semantic knowledge; repeated episodic experiences are known to form semantic memory.
Engineering systems (e.g., learning from demonstrations and pattern mining) successfully extract rules from repeated trajectories.
In text games, many local rules recur across contexts (e.g., light sources enable inspection), enabling abstraction.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Cognitive theories of episodic-to-semantic consolidation and ML work on rule extraction from trajectories exist.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A quantitative, operational compression law P_abstract ≈ 1 - exp(-k*f*s) applied to LM-agent memory consolidation in text games, connecting repetition and salience to abstraction probability.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Applies cognitive consolidation intuition to LM memory design with a simple testable functional form.
references: Tulving (1972) 'Episodic and semantic memory' [cognitive foundation]
Blundell et al. (2016) 'Neural Episodic Control' [episodic storage for RL]
Graves et al. (2016) 'Differentiable Neural Computer' [structured memory systems]

theory_statements.theory_statement: At decision time, the agent chooses retrieval from episodic memory when the uncertainty over current state U_state (measured by entropy over plausible states) exceeds a threshold U_e; otherwise it prefers semantic memory retrieval. This balances exact recovery (episodic) and generalization (semantic) and minimizes expected decision-time error.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to transformer LM agents with access to both detailed transcripts and compressed semantic facts, where a computational budget for retrieval is constrained and uncertainty estimates are available (e.g., via LM calibration or ensemble spreads).
special_cases: If uncertainty estimates are unreliable, retrieval decisions may misroute, causing failures.
When episodic memory is too large to search efficiently, a pre-filtering or indexing step is required.

supporting_evidence.text: Episodic memory improves recovery of rare events; semantic memory supports generalization. Hybrid retrieval strategies have been effective in other agent contexts.
Uncertainty-based retrieval (querying detailed logs when uncertain) is an established heuristic in decision systems and active learning.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hybrid memory architectures and uncertainty-driven querying exist in RL and question-answering systems.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Formalizing a decision rule that switches between episodic and semantic memory retrieval based on a measurable current-state uncertainty metric for LM agents in text games.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Synthesis of existing memory ideas into a clear retrieval policy suited for LMs acting in textual environments.
references: Blundell et al. (2016) 'Model-Free Episodic Control' [episodic value storage]
Graves et al. (2016) 'Differentiable Neural Computer' [memories accessed by controller]

new_predictions_likely: Agents that consolidate repeated episodic sequences into semantic rules will solve new levels faster (fewer exploration steps) than agents that only store raw transcripts.
A retrieval policy that routes high-uncertainty situations to episodic recall and low-uncertainty to semantic facts will outperform fixed-weighted memory mixtures on median task success.
new_predictions_unknown: A single compression operator A trained on many games can learn cross-game semantic primitives that enable transfer to games with different surface language but shared mechanics.

Automated salience estimation (for determining s in the compression law) can be learned end-to-end from downstream reward signals, allowing the system to focus semantic consolidation on task-relevant events.
negative_experiments: If semantic consolidation consistently removes critical rare-event details and performance drops, the compression rule is inadequate and HESM's consolidation assumptions fail.
If uncertainty-based retrieval routing does not outperform uniform retrieval across many tasks, the proposed decision rule may be invalid or hard to implement robustly.

unaccounted_for.text: Precise mechanisms for aligning and canonicalizing free-form natural language transcripts into templates for abstraction without manual supervision.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some text games intentionally avoid repeated patterns and require one-off creative reasoning, which limits semantic consolidation utility.

special_cases: Games with intentionally varied phrasing for identical mechanics (paraphrase obfuscation) can make naive frequency-based abstractions incorrect.
Memory budget constraints force selective retention policies that must be learned.

references: Tulving (1972) 'Episodic and semantic memory' [foundation for layered memory]
Blundell et al. (2016) 'Model-Free Episodic Control' [episodic memory in RL]
Graves et al. (2016) 'Differentiable Neural Computer' [structured memory architectures]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Layered memory concepts and episodic/semantic separation have cognitive and ML precedents.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Operationalizing a hierarchical episodic-semantic memory specifically for transformer LMs in text games and proposing a quantitative compression law and uncertainty-based retrieval routing.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Builds on cognitive and ML memory literature but gives specific, testable constructs for LM agents in text environments.

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

theory-7.name: Hierarchical Episodic-Context Memory (HECM)
theory-7.description: LM agents playing text games maintain a two-tier memory: a short-term, token-level working context (sliding window + attention cache) and a compressed episodic-context memory that stores structured summaries of past episodes (summaries include key locations, persistent object states, failed/successful action sequences, and discovered subgoals). The agent dynamically promotes frequently-relevant episodic entries into a mid-term deliberation buffer used for planning and retrieval, and demotes or consolidates infrequent entries. This hierarchy balances the LM's limited attention with long-term task-relevant memory, enabling multi-episode strategic behavior, subgoal chaining, and efficient reuse of past solutions.
theory-7.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: LM agents playing text games maintain a two-tier memory: a short-term, token-level working context (sliding window + attention cache) and a compressed episodic-context memory that stores structured summaries of past episodes (summaries include key locations, persistent object states, failed/successful action sequences, and discovered subgoals). The agent dynamically promotes frequently-relevant episodic entries into a mid-term deliberation buffer used for planning and retrieval, and demotes or consolidates infrequent entries. This hierarchy balances the LM's limited attention with long-term task-relevant memory, enabling multi-episode strategic behavior, subgoal chaining, and efficient reuse of past solutions.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Maintain (1) a working context W as the LM's immediate attention span (token-limited sliding window plus persistent attention cache) for fluent language-grounded decision-making, and (2) an episodic-context memory E that stores compact structured summaries S_k of past episodes k. Retrieval for decision d_t is an attention-weighted soft combination Retrieval(d_t) = alpha(t) * read_W(d_t) + (1 - alpha(t)) * read_E(d_t), where alpha(t) adapts based on novelty and recency signals (alpha in [0,1]).
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to language-model-based agents interacting in partially-observable, sequential text environments (interactive fiction/text games) where episodic experiences across episodes or long horizons contain reusable information and LM attention is limited (< ~8k tokens).
special_cases: In extremely short-horizon tasks (< W length) E provides little benefit and alpha ~ 1.
When the environment is adversarially stochastic, over-reliance on E can mislead; alpha should adapt toward W.
If summaries S_k are lossy or misaligned with decision needs, retrieval harms performance.

supporting_evidence.text: Transformer LMs have limited effective context windows; augmenting with episodic memory improves long-horizon tasks (general literature on memory-augmented neural networks and Transformer-XL style recurrence).
Human episodic memory is hierarchical (working vs. long-term) and supports planning via retrieval of past episodes; this is an inspiration for architectural division.
Agents that summarize and reuse past trajectories (experience replay, episodic control) improve sample efficiency in RL and sequential tasks.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Memory-augmented networks and experience replay separate short- and long-term information; transformer recurrence and retrieval-augmented generation use similar read mechanisms.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Explicit alpha-weighted, novelty-modulated interpolation between working context reads and structured episodic summary reads tailored for LM agents in text games, plus the operationalization of promotion/demotion consolidation rules for episodic entries.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: HECM synthesizes existing memory and retrieval ideas but provides a task-specific, operational hierarchy and an adaptive interpolation mechanism for LM decision-making in text games.
references: Graves et al. (2014) Neural Turing Machines [memory-augmented networks]
Dai et al. (2019) Transformer-XL [extended context via recurrence]
Weston et al. (2014) Memory Networks [episodic-style retrieval]
Côté et al. (2018) TextWorld [text-game benchmark and motivation]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Represent episodic summaries S_k with meta-features (frequency f_k, recency r_k, utility u_k measured by subsequent success-rate improvement) and consolidate memory by promoting entries where utility*u_weight + frequency*f_weight + recency*r_weight > threshold T_promote into a mid-term deliberation buffer D; demote or compress entries with utility < T_demote. Properly tuned weights produce sublinear growth of active deliberation set |D| relative to total episodes N (|D| = O(log N) under naturalistic tasks with repetitive structure).
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to agents that (a) generate compact structured summaries for episodes, (b) can track simple statistics (f_k, r_k, utility u_k) over episodes, and (c) operate in domains with recurring structure (e.g., locations/items/mechanics recurring across episodes).
special_cases: In procedurally-generated games with low recurrence, |D| may grow near-linearly and consolidation thresholds must adapt.
Utility estimation can be noisy early in training; bootstrap safeguards are required to avoid discarding rare-but-critical episodes.

supporting_evidence.text: Experience replay and prioritized replay in RL use priority signals (TD error, success) to select experiences; consolidation improves sample efficiency.
Biological memory consolidation prioritizes salient, frequently reactivated episodes; inspiration for promotion/demotion heuristics.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prioritized experience replay and memory consolidation heuristics exist in RL and neuroscience-inspired ML.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying a compact, parametric promotion-demotion law to LM-generated episodic summaries with explicit predicted sublinear active-set scaling in text-game environments.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This law is an adaptation of prioritized replay ideas into the episodic-summary domain with an explicit predicted complexity bound.
references: Schaul et al. (2016) Prioritized Experience Replay [prioritization of replayed experiences]
Graves et al. (2014) Neural Turing Machines [memory operations]
Côté et al. (2018) TextWorld [motivating text-game memory needs]

new_predictions_likely: An LM agent with HECM will solve multi-episode quests (where solution requires reuse of facts discovered in prior episodes) with significantly fewer environment restarts than an LM without episodic-context memory.
The mid-term deliberation buffer D size will stabilize (sublinear growth) in games with recurring rooms and persistent objects when promotion thresholds are tuned to task statistics.
Adaptive alpha(t) that shifts toward E when novelty signals are low yields higher planning depth and success rates on puzzles requiring multi-step chaining.
new_predictions_unknown: HECM consolidation rules learned end-to-end (via differentiable objectives) will match or exceed heuristic promotion/demotion performance across diverse game families.
HECM summaries learned in one genre of text games will transfer to other genres with minimal fine-tuning, enabling near-zero-shot task acceleration.
negative_experiments: If an HECM implementation leads to no improvement or degraded performance compared to a large-context LM (with same compute) on long-horizon text games, the two-tier benefit claim is challenged.
If D grows linearly despite promotion thresholds and recurring structures, the predicted sublinear scaling is invalid for realistic text-game families.

unaccounted_for.text: Precise algorithms for compressing arbitrary natural-language episode transcripts into structured summaries S_k that reliably capture planning-relevant content remain an open engineering problem.

conflicting_evidence.text: In some tasks, retrieval-augmented LMs fail to benefit from extra memory due to retrieval noise or misalignment, suggesting HECM must manage retrieval precision carefully.
