special_cases: Tasks with extremely high stochasticity or hidden state may render episodic summaries misleading unless summaries include probabilistic beliefs.
When game mechanics change across episodes (non-stationarity), older summaries must be deprecated or reweighted.

references: Graves et al. (2014) Neural Turing Machines [memory-augmented networks inspiration]
Dai et al. (2019) Transformer-XL [limited-context remedy via recurrence]
Schaul et al. (2016) Prioritized Experience Replay [priority-based replay]
Côté et al. (2018) TextWorld [task motivations]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Memory augmentation, retrieval-augmented generation, and prioritized experience replay are established; transformer extensions address limited context.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: HECM integrates an adaptive interpolation mechanism between short-term context and structured episodic summaries with explicit promotion/demotion laws tuned for LM agents in text games.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: HECM repurposes existing memory/replay ideas into a domain-specific hierarchical system with adaptive control and explicit scaling predictions.

theory-8.name: Action-Conditioned Retrieval & Synthesis (ACRS)
theory-8.description: LM agents should condition memory retrieval on candidate actions: when evaluating an action a, retrieve only those episodic summaries and substructures that are action-relevant (e.g., contain same objects, locations, verbs). Retrieved contexts are then synthesized via the LM into an imagined successor observation distribution. Action-conditioned retrieval reduces noise, improves model-based rollouts, and enables counterfactual evaluation of candidate action sequences without full environment execution.
theory-8.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: LM agents should condition memory retrieval on candidate actions: when evaluating an action a, retrieve only those episodic summaries and substructures that are action-relevant (e.g., contain same objects, locations, verbs). Retrieved contexts are then synthesized via the LM into an imagined successor observation distribution. Action-conditioned retrieval reduces noise, improves model-based rollouts, and enables counterfactual evaluation of candidate action sequences without full environment execution.

theory_statements.theory_statement: For candidate action a_t, compute a relevance score R(S_k, a_t) between action and episodic summary using a learned scorer (embedding dot-product or LM-scored relevance). Retrieve top-K summaries with highest R and use them as additional context for action evaluation. This targeted retrieval yields higher precision of recalled actionable facts than blind retrieval of top-K by topical similarity.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to LM agents that can (a) enumerate candidate actions or action templates, (b) embed actions and summaries into a common relevance space, and (c) perform LM-conditioned synthesis with retrieved contexts.
special_cases: If candidate action set is extremely large, scoring cost becomes a bottleneck; hierarchical candidate filtering is required.
If relevance scorer is trained on biased data, retrieval may systematically ignore rare but correct summaries.

supporting_evidence.text: Retrieval-augmented generation improves downstream tasks when retrieval is relevant; conditional retrieval (query-aware) outperforms unguided retrieval in many NLP tasks.
Action-conditioned world models in RL (action-conditional prediction) provide better rollouts than action-agnostic models.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Retrieval-augmented LMs and action-conditional world models are known independently.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Combining action-conditioned retrieval of episodic summaries with LM-based synthesis for counterfactual rollouts specifically in text-game agents, and claiming precision gains versus topical retrieval.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: ACRS integrates action-conditioned retrieval into LM agent rollouts; this is a targeted adaptation of retrieval-augmented generation and action-conditional world models.
references: Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation [RAG style retrieval]
Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) World Models [action-conditional rollouts]
Weston et al. (2014) Memory Networks [conditional read]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Let p_hat be the LM-conditioned predicted success probability per simulated step for candidate rollouts using retrieved contexts, and c_sim be cost (compute) per simulated step. If p_hat > p_env * (c_env / c_sim) where p_env is baseline empirical success-per-step in environment and c_env is environment interaction cost, then counterfactual evaluation reduces expected real-environment interactions required to find successful plans.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to scenarios where simulation cost is meaningfully cheaper than environment interactions, and the LM's synthesized successor distributions produce informative predictions (p_hat reasonably correlated with real outcomes).
special_cases: If synthesized predictions are uncorrelated with environment outcomes, p_hat is unreliable and the inequality fails; counterfactual rollouts then mislead.
For extremely cheap environment steps (e.g., deterministic local simulator), simulation advantage disappears.

supporting_evidence.text: Model-based RL theory shows benefit when model predictions are accurate relative to environment costs; LM-based synthesis acts as a learned model.
Empirical work on planning with learned models shows reduction in environment interactions when model-step accuracy is sufficient.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Model-based RL provides cost-benefit analyses for planning vs. executing; retrieval-augmented LMs have been used for simulation-like tasks.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying a concrete inequality binding predicted per-step success probability, simulation vs. environment costs, and LM-action-conditioned retrieval for text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement formalizes when LM-based counterfactual rollouts help; it adapts established model-based cost bounds to LM-retrieval synthesis in text games.
references: Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) World Models [model-based planning]
Sutton (1991) Dyna Architecture [planning with learned models]
Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation [retrieval improves generation]

new_predictions_likely: Action-conditioned retrieval will reduce the proportion of irrelevant retrieved summaries by >30% versus topical retrieval baselines, improving planning precision in text games.
LM agents using ACRS counterfactual rollouts will find multi-step puzzle solutions with fewer environment trials when simulation cost is lower than environment reset cost.
new_predictions_unknown: Learning the relevance scorer end-to-end jointly with LM policy will generalize across game genres, enabling transfer of retrieval policies.
ACRS combined with symbolic constraint checking on retrieved summaries will solve puzzles requiring logical consistency beyond LM capacity alone.
negative_experiments: If action-conditioned retrieval shows no precision improvement over simple topical retrieval in multiple game families, the claimed advantage is refuted.
If counterfactual rollouts with ACRS consistently misrank actions (i.e., p_hat uncorrelated with true success), the cost-benefit bound fails in practice.

unaccounted_for.text: Designing compact summary representations that encode action-relevant structure (objects, affordances) without supervised labels is a challenging open problem.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some retrieval-augmented LMs are brittle to retrieval noise; if retrieval errors align with action-conditioned retrieval failures, ACRS benefits may be limited.

special_cases: When candidate action spaces are continuous or enormous, action-conditioned scoring requires approximate or hierarchical techniques.
If the environment enforces hidden counters/time-based events, retrieved summaries need temporal annotations to remain useful.

references: Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation [retrieval conditioning improves generation]
Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) World Models [action-conditional prediction]
Sutton (1991) Dyna Architecture [planning with learned models]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Retrieval-augmented generation and action-conditioned learned models are established.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: ACRS specifically prescribes action-conditioned retrieval of episodic summaries for LM-based counterfactual rollouts in text games and gives a quantitative cost-benefit bound.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: ACRS connects retrieval and model-based planning in an LM agent context with an operational retrieval policy and utility criterion.

theory-9.name: Sparse Episodic-Indexing Dominance
theory-9.description: For long-horizon, low-stochasticity text-adventure games requiring deep planning, a sparse key-value episodic memory with constrained capacity and narrow retrieval (top-k small) yields higher task success than extending the transformer's dense attention context beyond practical limits. The mechanism is reduced attention noise and preserved salient episodic structure through compact indexed retrieval.
theory-9.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: For long-horizon, low-stochasticity text-adventure games requiring deep planning, a sparse key-value episodic memory with constrained capacity and narrow retrieval (top-k small) yields higher task success than extending the transformer's dense attention context beyond practical limits. The mechanism is reduced attention noise and preserved salient episodic structure through compact indexed retrieval.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For deterministic or low-stochasticity text games with required planning depth T_planning >= 500 steps, an episodic-indexing memory (key-value store capacity C in [64,512], top-k retrieval k = 8) will outperform extending the LM dense attention context window beyond W = 2048 tokens. Numeric thresholds: T_planning >= 500, W_threshold = 2048 tokens, episodic capacity C ∈ [64,512], top-k k = 8. Negative prediction: Increasing the dense attention window to W >= 8192 tokens will NOT increase mean success rate relative to the episodic-indexing agent and will reduce success if episodic capacity C < 64 or top-k k > 32.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Deterministic or low-stochasticity (per-step transition entropy < 1.0 nat) single-player text-adventure games with required planning depth >= 500 steps, branching factor B ∈ [2,6], running on transformer LMs with baseline context capacity 2048 tokens and parameter count between 100M and 10B.
special_cases: Observations whose single-step tokenized length > 2048 tokens (single-observation huge blobs) — episodic keys that summarize such blobs could lose necessary detail and fail.
Environments with per-step transition entropy >= 2.0 nats (high stochasticity) where past episodic traces poorly predict future transitions.
Tasks that require remembering continuous numeric values or counters with fine precision across >10k steps — compact embedding keys may quantize away required precision.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Dense self-attention cost and interference scale with token count; retrieving a small set of salient items from a key-value index avoids quadratic attention to all tokens.

inference: For planning horizons >> context window length, repeated retrieval of temporally-distant salient states via compact indices preserves decision-relevant information without linear growth in attention computation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Existing work proposes both extending transformer context and using external memory; prior comparisons are qualitative or lack concrete numeric cross-over thresholds.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Concrete numeric cross-over claim (planning depth >=500 favors episodic-indexing; context window W>=8192 will not beat episodic-indexing under stated capacity/top-k bounds) and an explicit negative prediction about large context windows degrading performance under capacity constraints.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Extends prior memory-vs-context work by committing to numeric thresholds and a falsifiable comparative experiment.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Take a fixed pretrained 1B-parameter transformer LM (no architecture change to model internals). Build two agents: (A) Episodic-indexing with capacity C=256 entries, key dim 128, top-k=8 retrieval, write-on-event novelty policy; (B) Extended-context with no external memory but transformer context window W=8192 tokens (all other training/data equal). Evaluate both on 100 procedurally-generated deterministic text games with average required planning depth >=500 and branching factor ≈3. Metric: mean success rate across games; run 30 random seeds per game. Compare means with paired test.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: Agent (B) with extended context W >= 8192 achieves an absolute >10 percentage-point higher mean success rate than agent (A) across the evaluation suite (p < 0.05 by paired test).

new_predictions_likely: On the stated domain, episodic-indexing agents with capacity C ∈ [128,512] and top-k = 8 will show >= 15 percentage-point absolute higher success rate than a baseline LM limited to W = 2048 tokens.
When episodic capacity C is increased from 64 to 256, average retrieval hit-rate (relevant retrieved entries per retrieval) will increase by >= 10 percentage points and plateau beyond C = 512.
For the domain, increasing top-k from 8 to 32 will decrease mean success rate by >= 5 percentage points due to increased retrieval noise.
new_predictions_unknown: At model parameter counts > 50B, the cross-over planning depth where extended context equals episodic indexing will fall below 500; the exact cross-over is unknown.

Hybrid designs that store raw tokens inside episodic entries (instead of compact embeddings) could outperform both pure episodic indexing and pure extended context in games where single observations exceed 2048 tokens.
If retrieval uses learned approximate nearest-neighbor indices with amortized constant-time retrieval, the optimal top-k may shift below 8.
negative_experiments: Fork test: Repeat the discriminating experiment but with extended context set to W = 8192. If the extended-context agent wins by >10 percentage points in mean success rate, the theory is decisively refuted.
Ablation: Reduce episodic capacity C to 16 while keeping top-k=8. If episodic-indexing still outperforms extended context significantly, the capacity lower bound [64,512] is invalid.

unaccounted_for.text: background_principle: Human episodic memory uses multimodal and richly structured cues; the key-value index model here omits multimodal integration.
inference: Structured, task-specific memories (maps, object graphs) could outperform generic key-value episodic stores in navigation-heavy games; this theory does not predict that outcome.

special_cases: Highly stochastic games (per-step entropy >= 2.0 nats).
Single observations longer than the LM context window (single-step token length > 2048).

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Memory-augmented agents, retrieval-augmented generation, and extending transformer contexts are proposed approaches.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Explicit numeric thresholds (T_planning >=500, W=2048/8192, capacity and top-k bounds) and a concrete negative prediction comparing very-large context windows to episodic stores.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Builds on existing concepts but makes specific, testable numeric claims.

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

theory-10.name: Compressed Semantic Memory for Goal Conditioning
theory-10.description: When language-model agents play long, highly-branching text games under tight memory-bytes budgets, storing compressed semantic summaries (fixed-size learned embeddings per event) and maintaining them with reservoir sampling plus periodic compression yields higher goal-directed success than storing raw token sequences. The compression removes irrelevant lexical variation and increases retrieval precision for goal-conditioned policies when compression exceeds a specific ratio.
theory-10.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_name: Compressed Semantic Memory for Goal Conditioning
components.theory_description: When language-model agents play long, highly-branching text games under tight memory-bytes budgets, storing compressed semantic summaries (fixed-size learned embeddings per event) and maintaining them with reservoir sampling plus periodic compression yields higher goal-directed success than storing raw token sequences. The compression removes irrelevant lexical variation and increases retrieval precision for goal-conditioned policies when compression exceeds a specific ratio.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For text-adventure environments with branching factor B >= 5 and mean episode length L_avg >= 200, an embedding-based compressed memory (embedding dimension D = 64) with effective compression ratio R >= 16 (stored memory bytes <= 1/16 of raw-token bytes) outperforms raw-token episodic replay under the same memory-byte budget; numeric thresholds: B = 5, L_avg = 200, D = 64, R = 16; negative prediction: under these conditions raw-token replay will NOT outperform the compressed embedding memory and, if compression ratio is reduced below R = 4, compressed-memory performance will degrade relative to the R >= 16 case; discriminating experiment: build a suite of procedurally-generated English-like text games with B = 6 and L_avg = 400, fix memory budget to 1 MB, and compare (A) an agent that stores D = 64 embeddings sampled by reservoir sampling and periodically compresses to maintain R >= 16, against (B) an agent that stores raw tokens until the same budget is exhausted; measure mean success rate across 50 games × 20 seeds and retrieval-precision (human-annotated relevance of retrieved items); falsifier: if agent B (raw-token) attains >=5 percentage points higher mean success rate than agent A across the suite (p < 0.05), this statement is falsified.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Procedurally-generated English-like text-adventure games with branching factor >= 5, mean episode length >= 200 steps, memory budget in the range 0.5–5 MB, and decision-making by a language model that conditions on retrieved memories.
special_cases: Games that require exact string reproduction (single-use passphrases, exact codes) where an embedding cannot recover the exact token sequence; compressed-memory may fail.
Very low-branching environments (B <= 2) where raw-token storage retains high signal and compression provides no advantage.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Fixed-size semantic embeddings (e.g., 64-d) cluster semantically similar states and remove lexical noise, improving nearest-neighbor retrieval precision under constrained storage.

inference: As branching factor increases, the relevance-signal-to-noise ratio of stored raw-token contexts falls faster than the compressible semantic content does; therefore high compression that preserves semantics raises effective retrieval SNR when bytes are limited.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Embedding-based memory and reservoir sampling have been proposed for language agents.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Concrete numeric thresholds (B >= 5, L_avg >= 200, D = 64, R >= 16), an explicit negative prediction against raw-token replay under equal memory-bytes, and a concrete falsifier bound (>=5% advantage to raw-token refutes).
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Extends existing memory-compression ideas with specific, testable regime bounds and a discriminating experiment.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Create 50 procedurally-generated text games with branching factor 6 and average length 400. Fix agent LM policy architecture and memory-byte budget at 1 MB. Agent A: store D = 64 embeddings with reservoir sampling and periodic PCA/quantization to maintain storage <= 1/16 of raw bytes. Agent B: store raw tokens until 1 MB. Use identical retrieval mechanism and decision-conditioning. Run 20 random seeds per game. Report mean success rate, retrieval precision (human-annotated), and median episode reward.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: Raw-token agent B obtains >=5 percentage points higher mean success rate than compressed-embedding agent A across the suite (p < 0.05).

new_predictions_likely: With memory budget <= 2 MB, embedding-based compressed memory (D = 64, R >= 16) will yield >= 12 percentage-point higher retrieval precision than raw-token storage on the specified game suite.
With the above settings, mean success rate will improve by >= 8 percentage points versus raw-token storage for at least 70% of the games.
Embedding-based memory will reduce the agent's retrieval latency (average retrieved items examined before action) by >= 20% because of higher precision under the same budget.
new_predictions_unknown: If embeddings are trained end-to-end with the LM policy using a contrastive objective tied to goals, the required embedding dimension D for parity with raw tokens may fall below 32; the exact threshold is unknown.

A hybrid scheme that stores compressed embeddings plus a tiny set of raw-token anchors may outperform pure compression in environments with occasional exact-string requirements; the optimal anchor fraction is unknown.
negative_experiments: Fork test: Run the discriminating experiment (B = 6, L_avg = 400, budget = 1 MB). If the raw-token agent beats the compressed agent by >=5% mean success, the compression-dominance statement is refuted.

Ablation: Reduce compression ratio to R = 4 under the same conditions. If compressed-agent then loses >5% success relative to its R >= 16 performance, the specified R >= 16 threshold is validated; if performance remains unchanged, the numeric threshold is incorrect.

unaccounted_for.text: background_principle: Tasks that demand exact string output (passwords, exact names) are not solved by semantic compression alone.

inference: Misalignment between the embedding space and the downstream LM policy (e.g., embeddings not predictive of action-relevant distinctions) could negate the advantage; this interaction is not modeled in the statement.

special_cases: Passphrase/one-time-token games requiring exact textual reproduction.
Highly morphologically compact languages where token embeddings may conflate distinct meanings.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Techniques exist for embedding-based memory, reservoir sampling, and compressed retrieval for language agents.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Explicit numeric regime where compression strictly dominates raw replay and a concrete falsifier.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Builds on known ideas but supplies precise, testable thresholds tied to branching and episode length.

theory-11.name: Schema-based Macro-Action Memory (Hierarchical Chunks)
theory-11.description: When text games contain repeated multi-step subroutines, inducing and storing 'schemas' (macro-actions) that replace sequences of primitive actions with single macro tokens yields superior long-horizon planning compared to flat episodic or compressed-memory approaches, provided macro induction captures >= 20% of trajectory steps and macro length k >= 4.
theory-11.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: When text games contain repeated multi-step subroutines, inducing and storing 'schemas' (macro-actions) that replace sequences of primitive actions with single macro tokens yields superior long-horizon planning compared to flat episodic or compressed-memory approaches, provided macro induction captures >= 20% of trajectory steps and macro length k >= 4.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For procedurally-generated text games with branching factor B >= 8 and average episode length L_avg >= 400 that contain repeated subroutines, a schema-based memory that identifies and stores macros of median length k >= 4 and that compresses >= 20% of trajectory tokens into macros will outperform both compressed-embedding memory and raw-token episodic replay when schema vocabulary size V_schema <= 500 and memory budget <= 5 MB; numeric thresholds: B = 8, L_avg = 400, k = 4, compress >= 20%, V_schema = 500, budget = 5 MB; negative prediction: under these conditions neither compressed embeddings nor raw-token replay will achieve higher mean success; discriminating experiment: generate games with engineered repeated subroutines making up exactly 25% of steps, run three agents with identical LM decision policies and memory budgets (5 MB): (A) schema-agent that discovers macros online (merge-subsequence heuristic + frequency threshold) and stores macros as single tokens, (B) compressed-embedding agent (D = 128, R = 8), (C) raw-token replay agent; measure mean success rate and planning depth; falsifier: if either agent B or C attains >=7 percentage points higher mean success than schema-agent A across the suite, the statement is falsified.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Procedurally-generated English-like text-adventure games that include repeated multi-step subroutines (deterministic or stochastic), branching factor >= 8, mean episode length >= 400, memory budgets up to 5 MB, and language-model decision policies capable of conditioning on macro-tokens.
special_cases: Games where repeated subsequences are semantically brittle (small lexical changes alter outcomes) and so macros cause harmful overgeneralization.
Games where the cost of discovering macros online exceeds the compute/time budget and so macros cannot be reliably induced.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Hierarchical reinforcement learning (options, macro-actions) reduces effective horizon and improves planning in environments with repeated subroutines.

inference: Many procedurally-generated text games include repeated 'fetch' or 'unlock' subroutines; compressing repeated subsequences into single tokens reduces branching at the planning level and increases effective sample efficiency when compression covers >= 20% of steps.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Options/skills and macro-actions in RL; chunking in sequence models.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Numeric thresholds for branching, episode length, required compression fraction (>= 20%), macro length (k >= 4), schema vocabulary size, and a direct comparison to compressed embeddings under equal memory budgets.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Specializes hierarchical-memory ideas to text-game agents with explicit falsifiers and experiment design.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Construct a suite of 40 procedurally-generated games where repeated subroutines occupy 25% of steps and branching = 8. Fix memory budget = 5 MB and LM policy. Agent A: online macro induction (frequency threshold 0.02, merge if substitute reduces average token length by >=4), store macros as single tokens with schema vocab cap V_schema = 500. Agent B: compressed embeddings D = 128, R = 8. Agent C: raw-token replay. Run 30 seeds per game. Compare mean success rate, planning depth (average macro uses per episode), and failure modes.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: Either compressed-embedding agent B or raw-token agent C achieves >=7 percentage points higher mean success rate than schema-agent A (p < 0.05) across the suite.

new_predictions_likely: When the schema-agent captures >= 20% of steps into macros, mean success will increase by >= 10 percentage points relative to compressed-embedding agents on the specified suite.
Schema-based agents will exhibit >= 30% reduction in effective planning depth (number of decisions) compared to primitive-action agents when macros of k >= 4 are used.
When V_schema <= 500 and macros cover >= 20% of steps, sample efficiency (episodes to reach 80% peak success) will improve by >= 25% versus flat-memory agents.
new_predictions_unknown: If macros are learned with semantic-aware merging (aligning lexical variants), the minimum compression fraction required for advantage might drop below 15%; this is discriminative and untested.

In adversarial text games that intentionally vary lexical surface forms, schema induction that uses abstracted predicate templates may outperform literal-sequence macros; the crossover point is unknown.
negative_experiments: Fork test: Run the discriminating experiment (B = 8, L_avg = 400, repeated-subroutines = 25%, budget = 5 MB). If compressed-embedding or raw-token agents beat the schema-agent by >=7% success, the schema-dominance statement is refuted.

Ablation: Limit schema vocabulary to V_schema = 50. If schema-agent then fails to improve and is outperformed by compressed embeddings, then the V_schema <= 500 bound is necessary; if not, the numeric cap is incorrect.

unaccounted_for.text: inference: The theory does not model the compute cost of discovering macros online; in low-compute regimes macro discovery may be impractical.
background_principle: Macros can overgeneralize when surface variations alter outcomes; the theory assumes stable subroutine semantics for the macro to be useful.

special_cases: Games with intentionally adversarial lexical variation that breaks literal subsequence matching.
Environments where repeated subroutines are extremely rare (<10% of steps).

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Hierarchical RL (options), chunking, and macro-actions in sequential decision-making.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Numeric thresholds tying branching, episode length, macro length, compression fraction, and schema vocabulary to advantages specifically in text-game LM agents.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Applies hierarchical-memory ideas to text games with explicit falsifiers and regimes where macros must compress a fixed fraction of steps.

theory-12.name: Hierarchical Slot-based Memory Prioritization (HSPM)
theory-12.description: Agents should allocate a fixed number of high-priority memory 'goal slots' separate from a larger episodic store; above a concrete slot-count threshold these slots enable chaining of subgoals and reduce interference, improving long-horizon quest completion at the cost of some short-term reactivity.
theory-12.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: Agents should allocate a fixed number of high-priority memory 'goal slots' separate from a larger episodic store; above a concrete slot-count threshold these slots enable chaining of subgoals and reduce interference, improving long-horizon quest completion at the cost of some short-term reactivity.

theory_statements.statement_name: Slot-count fork: prioritized slots vs recency-only
theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For text-game agents with total memory capacity M_total and episodic event-level entries, configuring K prioritized goal slots with K >= 128 improves long-horizon quest-completion rate compared to K <= 32; numeric threshold: K = 128 slots; negative prediction: setting K <= 32 will NOT improve long-horizon quest-completion (it will reduce completion rate by >=10% relative to K=128) for games with required plan-chain length >=3 and episode length >=300; discriminating_experiment: keep M_total constant, implement identical retrieval and scoring mechanisms, vary K in {16,32,128,512} and evaluate on 100 procedurally generated quests requiring plan-chain length 3-7 and episode length 300-800; measure quest-completion rate and average subgoal chaining length; falsifies_if: if K <= 32 yields >=10% higher quest-completion rate than K=128 across the evaluation suite (p<0.05).
theory_statements.domain_scope: Text-adventure games where (a) events are discrete steps (actions/observations) stored as entries, (b) tasks require chaining >=3 subgoals, and (c) total memory capacity M_total is fixed between 8k and 64k entries.
special_cases: Purely reactive micro-games with plan-chain length = 0 where prioritized slots are unnecessary.
Extremely long-world-knowledge tasks where relevant facts are immutable and should be stored in a separate persistent KB rather than goal slots.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Fixed dedicated high-priority slots reduce interference between temporally local episodic traces and stable task-critical goals by creating a separate address space for high-utility items.

inference: When K is too small relative to required concurrent subgoals, goal slot eviction causes loss of intermediate targets and breaks chaining; increasing K above a threshold reinstates chaining capability.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Memory prioritization and dedicated buffers are used in some agents and cognitive models, but fixed numeric slot-count thresholds are rarely specified.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: An explicit fork committing that K >= 128 is necessary (vs K <= 32) for improved long-horizon chaining under constrained total capacity, producing a falsifiable numeric bound and prediction of a >=10% effect.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Goes beyond general prioritization by prescribing slot-count and a discriminating experiment.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Implement an LM-based agent with identical scoring and retrieval; set total store size to 16k event entries. Create four variants with K=16,32,128,512 dedicated goal slots. Generate 100 procedural quests requiring 3-7 chained subgoals and episode lengths 300-800. Evaluate quest completion and average intact subgoal chains.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: Any K <= 32 yields >=10% higher quest-completion rate than K=128 across the test suite (p<0.05).

new_predictions_likely: Numeric: In the domain specified, K=128 will produce at least 12 percentage points higher quest-completion rate than K=32 on average.
Agents with K >=128 will exhibit 2x fewer broken subgoal linkages (measured as interrupted required subgoals per successful completion) than K<=32.
Retrieval precision for planning-related queries will increase by >=15% when K is raised from 32 to 128, holding M_total constant.
new_predictions_unknown: If the LM has an internal emergent gating that replicates prioritized slots, the external K threshold may shift downward; magnitude unknown.
For tasks where subgoal depth correlates with sparse reward frequency, the optimal K may scale sublinearly with required chain length; direction uncertain.
negative_experiments: Fork test: Run the discriminating_experiment; if K<=32 outperforms K=128 by >=10% on quest-completion, theory is refuted.
Ablation: Remove dedicated slots (all memory in unified recency-indexed store); if performance matches K=128 within 5% on chaining tasks, the dedicated-slot hypothesis fails.

unaccounted_for.text: background_principle: How to set scoring function for slot admission (utility predictor) is not specified; different admission rules may change the numeric threshold.

special_cases: Tasks where subgoals are strictly hierarchical and can be encoded as single composite tokens — K may be much smaller.
Environments with continual external hints making subgoal retention trivial; dedicated slots unnecessary.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Prior work uses prioritized memory buffers and episodic stores; hierarchical planning with working memory buffers is discussed in cognitive theory.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Numeric slot-count threshold (K=128) as a hard fork against low-K configurations and an explicit falsifiable performance delta.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Extends existing ideas with concrete quantitative commitments and discriminating experiments.

theory-13.name: Sparse Landmark Anchoring and Low-frequency Replay (SLARF)
theory-13.description: Store only high-surprise 'landmark' events (top P percentile) and perform low-frequency scheduled replay; this sparse-anchor + low-replay regime enables efficient long-horizon planning by preserving salient connectors while avoiding retrieval noise from dense stores.
theory-13.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_name: Sparse Landmark Anchoring and Low-frequency Replay (SLARF)
components.theory_description: Store only high-surprise 'landmark' events (top P percentile) and perform low-frequency scheduled replay; this sparse-anchor + low-replay regime enables efficient long-horizon planning by preserving salient connectors while avoiding retrieval noise from dense stores.

theory_statements.statement_name: Sparsity-replay fork: landmarks with low-frequency replay vs dense high-frequency replay
theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For text-game agents solving long-horizon tasks (episode length >=500) the regime 'store only top P=5% surprise events + replay frequency F = 0.02 per step' yields higher planning efficiency than 'store P >=20% + replay F >= 0.2 per step'; numeric thresholds: P=5% and F=0.02; negative prediction: increasing storage density to P >=20% and replay frequency to F >=0.2 will NOT increase planning efficiency and will reduce planning success per unit compute by >=12% relative to the sparse regime; discriminating_experiment: run agents with storage percentile P in {1%,5%,20%,50%} and replay frequency F in {0.00,0.02,0.2,0.5}; evaluate planning success, average retrieval latency, and planning-success-per-compute on 80 long-horizon games (episode length 500-1200) requiring bridging sparse connectors; falsifies_if: if any dense-high-replay configuration (P>=20% and F>=0.2) yields >=10% higher planning-success-per-compute than P=5%,F=0.02, the statement is falsified.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Text-adventure games with sparse salient connectors (critical events occur <5% of time), episode lengths >=500 steps, and agents constrained by limited retrieval compute budget per step.
special_cases: Games with uniformly informative steps where 'surprise' distribution is flat; landmark selection fails.
Environments where replay is free (no compute budget), making high-frequency replay viable.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Storing and replaying only high-surprise events reduces noise and focuses learning/planning on connectors between sparse rewards.
inference: High-frequency replay over dense stores amplifies spurious correlations and increases retrieval cost per useful planning step, lowering planning efficiency.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Experience replay and prioritized replay are known in RL; sparse episodic landmarks are discussed in cognitive literature.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Concrete sparse threshold P=5% combined with low-frequency replay F=0.02 and an explicit performance penalty for denser/higher-frequency regimes, forming a falsifiable fork.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Combines percentile-based sparsity and absolute replay-frequency thresholds to create a discriminable experimental claim.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Implement an LM-agent with identical surprise metric. Sweep P in {1,5,20,50}% and F in {0,0.02,0.2,0.5}. Run on 80 long-horizon procedurally generated games (500-1200 steps) needing sparse connectors. Measure planning-success-per-compute and retrieval latency.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: Any configuration with P>=20% and F>=0.2 achieves >=10% higher planning-success-per-compute than P=5%,F=0.02 across the suite (p<0.05).

new_predictions_likely: Numeric: P=5% and F=0.02 will yield >=15% improvement in planning-success-per-compute over P=20%,F=0.2 in the specified domain.
Average retrieval latency per planning step will be >=3x lower in the sparse regime than in dense-high-replay regime for equivalent total stored events.
Sparse-landmark agents will exhibit fewer spurious plan branches (measured as explored but irrelevant action sequences) by >=20%.
new_predictions_unknown: If the surprise metric is poorly correlated with downstream planning utility, the sparse regime may fail; the threshold P required will depend on surprise-utility correlation unknown a priori.
Learned adaptive replay schedules might outperform fixed low-frequency replay; whether they converge to F~0.02 is unknown.
negative_experiments: Fork test: Run the discriminating_experiment; if dense-high-replay (P>=20%,F>=0.2) outperforms sparse-low-replay by >=12% planning-success-per-compute, theory is refuted.
Ablation: Replace surprise-based filtering with random sampling at same P=5%; if performance matches surprise-based P=5% within 5%, the landmark hypothesis fails.

unaccounted_for.text: inference: How to compute 'surprise' in text-game streams (token-level vs event-level) is unspecified and may alter P's effective meaning.

special_cases: Worlds with frequent meaningful events (>20% informative) where P=5% would miss many relevant items.
Scenarios with near-zero retrieval cost where high-frequency replay is cheap and the sparse advantage disappears.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Prioritized replay and landmark-based episodic memory in cognitive models and RL.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Concrete numeric pair (P=5%, F=0.02) and a falsifiable comparative claim vs dense-high-replay with explicit performance delta.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Transforms prior qualitative ideas into a specific, testable operational regime.

theory-14.name: Hierarchical-Graph Memory for High-Branching Planning
theory-14.description: When branching factor is large, compressing episodic traces into hierarchical node summaries and connecting them into an explicit directed exploration graph yields superior planning ability over flat episodic key-value stores. The mechanism: graph topology preserves branching structure enabling targeted retrieval of alternative futures without exhaustive per-token attention.
theory-14.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: When branching factor is large, compressing episodic traces into hierarchical node summaries and connecting them into an explicit directed exploration graph yields superior planning ability over flat episodic key-value stores. The mechanism: graph topology preserves branching structure enabling targeted retrieval of alternative futures without exhaustive per-token attention.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For text games with branching factor B >= 4 and planning depth T between 100 and 2000 steps, a hierarchical summarization into graph nodes of max node-token-size N = 128 tokens with graph degree limit D = 8 (edges per node) and summarization cadence S = 10 steps will outperform a flat episodic key-value store with capacity C = 512 and top-k = 16. Numeric thresholds: branching factor B >= 4, node size N = 128 tokens, degree D <= 8, summarization cadence S = 10 steps, flat memory capacity C = 512, top-k = 16. Negative prediction: Increasing node-token-size to N >= 1024 will NOT increase mean success and will reduce performance by introducing noisy, high-variance node embeddings.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Single-player text-adventure games where branching factor B >= 4, planning depth T ∈ [100,2000], observation token lengths per step <= 512 tokens, and transformer LMs with baseline context 2048 tokens.
special_cases: Games where branching is shallow (B <= 2) — flat episodic memory or extended context may outperform graph memory.
Tasks where inter-node relationships are non-relational (pure sequence) rather than graph-like — graph structure adds overhead with no benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Explicit graph representations preserve topological branching relationships and allow targeted search algorithms (e.g., graph search) that do not require re-attending to entire token histories.
inference: Compressing contiguous segments into short node summaries (N=128 tokens) preserves sufficient decision-relevant detail for branching decisions while limiting attention and retrieval noise.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Some work stores episodic memory as summaries and others propose learned map- or graph-like memories, but numeric node-size, degree, and cadence thresholds are not standard.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Specific numeric design: node-token-size N=128, degree cap D=8, summarization cadence S=10 steps and explicit negative prediction about N >= 1024 harming performance.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Combines hierarchical summarization with an explicit graph topology and numeric bounds as a falsifiable design claim.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Implement two agents on a suite of 80 procedurally-generated high-branching (B >= 4) text games with average depth T ≈ 500: (A) Graph-memory agent: summarize every S=10 steps into node summaries ≤128 tokens, link nodes with edges when transition observed, limit node out-degree to D=8, retrieve neighbors via targeted graph search; (B) Flat episodic agent: key-value store capacity C=512, top-k=16 retrieval, same LM backbone. Train/fine-tune equally. Evaluate mean success rate, mean number of irrelevant retrievals per retrieval, and planner call counts. 30 seeds per game.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: Flat episodic agent (B) achieves an absolute >10 percentage-point higher mean success rate than graph-memory agent (A) across the suite (p < 0.05), or graph-memory agent shows no statistically significant reduction in irrelevant retrievals.

new_predictions_likely: On games with B >= 4 and T ≈ 500, graph-memory agents as specified will achieve >= 12 percentage-point higher success rate than flat episodic agents with C = 512, top-k = 16.
When node-token-size increases from N = 128 to N = 1024, mean success rate will drop by >= 8 percentage points due to embedding noise.
Average retrievals per decision for graph-memory agents will be <= 0.5× the retrievals per decision of the flat episodic agent under identical retrieval budgets.
new_predictions_unknown: If LM backbone is scaled >50B parameters, learned summarization quality might allow node-size N to increase without degradation; the cross-over is unknown.
For games with semantic locality (local clusters of decisions), graph pruning heuristics could make degree cap D <= 4 preferable.
If edges are augmented with transition probabilities learned online, the best summarization cadence S could drop below 10 steps.
negative_experiments: Fork test: Repeat discriminating experiment but restrict branching factor to B <= 2. If graph-memory agent still significantly outperforms flat episodic memory by >10 percentage points, the proposed branching-dependence claim is falsified.
Ablation: Increase node-token-size to N = 1024 and measure performance. If performance increases or stays within ±2 percentage points, the negative prediction about large nodes is falsified.

unaccounted_for.text: background_principle: Some games require exact token-level recall of long passages rather than abstracted summaries; the graph summary approach may lose information necessary for such tasks.
inference: Constructing and maintaining explicit graph topology imposes computational overhead that could be limiting for real-time applications; the theory does not quantify runtime cost tradeoffs.

special_cases: Shallow-branching games (B <= 2).
Single-step huge observations (>1024 tokens) where node summary truncation loses critical detail.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Prior proposals include summary-based memories, map-like memories, and graph representations for navigation; however, precise numeric node-size/degree/cadence bounds are novel.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Specific numeric bounds (N=128, D=8, S=10) and a concrete negative prediction that large node sizes (N>=1024) will harm performance.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Integrates existing ideas into an explicit, falsifiable design with numeric thresholds.

theory-15.name: Event-triggered Write Policy Advantage
theory-15.description: When environment-change events (inventory change, location change, new NPC encounter) occur sparsely relative to steps, restricting writes to event-triggered snapshots yields higher retrieval precision and downstream performance than continuous per-step writes for the same memory budget. The claim sets a concrete event-rate threshold below which event-triggered write strictly dominates continuous write.
theory-15.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: When environment-change events (inventory change, location change, new NPC encounter) occur sparsely relative to steps, restricting writes to event-triggered snapshots yields higher retrieval precision and downstream performance than continuous per-step writes for the same memory budget. The claim sets a concrete event-rate threshold below which event-triggered write strictly dominates continuous write.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For text games where environment-change event rate E_rate <= 0.25 (i.e., <=25% of steps are environment-change events) and average episode length L >= 100, an event-triggered write policy (write only on environment-change events) with deduplication will outperform continuous per-step write under equal memory budget; numeric thresholds: E_rate = 0.25, L = 100 steps; negative prediction: continuous per-step write will NOT outperform event-triggered write under these conditions and will reduce retrieval precision by >=10 percentage points; discriminating experiment: run two agents identical except write policy on a suite of games engineered to have E_rate=0.1 and L=200: (A) event-triggered write plus simple deduplication; (B) continuous per-step write until budget exhausted. Measure retrieval precision and success rate; falsifier: if continuous-write yields >=7% higher retrieval precision or success rate than event-write, the statement is falsified.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Text-adventure games where environment-change events are clearly defined and occur in <=25% of steps, average episode length >=100, and memory budgets constrained to small megabytes.
special_cases: Games with noisy or high-frequency event signals where event detection is unreliable; event-triggered policy may drop relevant micro-events.
Tasks where relevant temporal dynamics occur within non-event steps (e.g., a hidden timer that matters even without state-change events).

supporting_evidence.text: Background principle: Writing only salient events reduces redundant storage and focuses retrieval capacity on informative entries under constrained budgets.
Inference: When event_rate is low, continuous writes mostly add redundant near-duplicate states; deduplicated event writes increase effective information per stored entry.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Event-triggered and priority write policies have been suggested; continuous per-step write is a baseline.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The concrete numeric event-rate threshold (E_rate <= 0.25), explicit deduplication requirement, and falsifiable negative prediction about continuous-write performance.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Specifies operational thresholds where event-triggered write strictly dominates.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Construct games with event_rate controlled at 0.1 and L=200. Compare two agents with identical LM and memory budget: (A) event-triggered writes on inventory/location/NPC change + deduplication, (B) continuous per-step writes until budget. Evaluate retrieval precision (human-annotated relevance) and success rate across 80 games with 20 seeds each.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: Continuous-write agent (B) attains >=7% higher retrieval precision or >=7% higher success rate than event-triggered agent (A) across the evaluation (p<0.05).

new_predictions_likely: On games with E_rate=0.1 and memory budget 1MB, event-triggered write will yield retrieval precision at least 15 percentage points higher than continuous-write.
Numeric: Event-write agents will need <=60% of the stored entries of continuous-write agents to reach similar coverage of environment-change events.
Event-triggered write reduces memory churn and leads to 20–40% fewer irrelevant retrievals per episode.
new_predictions_unknown: If event detector is learned end-to-end and misclassifies some environment-change events, continuous-write may regain advantage; the break-even misclassification rate is unknown.
In agents that can summarize sequences in-memory automatically, continuous-write combined with strong compression may outperform event-write even at low E_rate; thresholding for this is unknown.
negative_experiments: Fork test: Run discriminating experiment; if continuous-write beats event-write by >=7% retrieval precision or success, theory is refuted.
Ablation: Turn off deduplication in event-write. If event-write still outperforms continuous-write, deduplication is not necessary and theory's mechanism is incomplete.

unaccounted_for.text: Inference: The cost of reliable event detection (compute and errors) is not fully accounted for; event-triggered systems require an event detector whose failures can negate benefits.

special_cases: Games where important information appears in steps without explicit environment-change events (e.g., time-based puzzles).
Environments where event_rate fluctuates widely within episodes.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Write-priority policies and event-triggered logging have been proposed in memory systems.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Numeric event-rate threshold E_rate <=0.25, deduplication requirement, and concrete falsifiable tests.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Refines existing ideas into a testable operational claim for text-game agents.

theory-16.name: Layer-limited Linear Arithmetic (LLLA)
theory-16.description: Transformers implement exact, compositional integer arithmetic by learning low-dimensional linear place-value transforms applied iteratively across layers. There exists a sharp layer-depth-to-digit threshold: when model depth exceeds a numeric multiple of operand digit-length the model uses an algorithmic linear projection + carry propagation mechanism; when depth is below that multiple the model defaults to memorized mapping and fails algorithmic generalization.
theory-16.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can perform arithmetic.

components.theory_description: Transformers implement exact, compositional integer arithmetic by learning low-dimensional linear place-value transforms applied iteratively across layers. There exists a sharp layer-depth-to-digit threshold: when model depth exceeds a numeric multiple of operand digit-length the model uses an algorithmic linear projection + carry propagation mechanism; when depth is below that multiple the model defaults to memorized mapping and fails algorithmic generalization.

theory_statements.statement_name: Layer-threshold fork for compositional linear arithmetic
theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For base-10 integer addition of two D-digit operands, if transformer depth L satisfies L >= 4 * D then Mechanism A (compositional linear arithmetic via learned linear projections and iterative carry propagation across layers) dominates; if L < 4 * D then Mechanism B (sparse memorized mapping of frequent input-output pairs) dominates. Numeric threshold: L = 4 * D. Negative prediction: For L >= 4 * D, held-out exact-addition accuracy on unseen D-digit pairs will NOT fall below 90% after standard training (see domain_scope); for L < 4 * D, held-out exact-addition accuracy will NOT exceed 60% without explicit example memorization of >50% of the input space. Discriminating experiment: For D in {2,4,6}, train transformer models (fixed width 1024, 16 heads) with depths L in {D,2D,3D,4D,5D} on a dataset containing 15% uniformly sampled D-digit addition pairs. Evaluate (1) exact-match accuracy on the held-out 85% of pairs, (2) exact-match accuracy on inputs with digit-place permutation (place-values shuffled), and (3) effect of replacing the final learned linear output projection with a random fixed linear map at inference. If models with L >= 4*D obtain >90% held-out exact accuracy, >85% on permuted-place tests, and drop >50 percentage points when final projection is randomized, Mechanism A is supported; otherwise the fork is false. Falsifier: Observing any model with L >= 4 * D that (a) after adequate training (converged training loss, >100k gradient steps for D>=4) has held-out exact-addition accuracy <= 90% on unseen D-digit pairs, or (b) shows <30 percentage-point accuracy drop when the final learned projection is randomized (indicating lookup-like output encoding), refutes this statement.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Decoder-only or encoder-decoder transformer architectures trained from scratch on exact base-10 integer addition of two equal-length operands. Operands are tokenized as single decimal digits (one token per digit), positional encodings present, model width in [512,4096], float16/float32 numerics, training dataset limited to <=20% of the D-digit input space so full memorization is infeasible. No chain-of-thought or explicit carry supervision provided during training. Applies to inference without stepwise prompting.
special_cases: If explicit stepwise/carry supervision is provided during training, the numeric multiplier (4) can reduce by up to 2x (i.e., L >= 2*D suffices).
If tokenization groups k digits per token, effective D is ceil(D/k) and the threshold uses that effective digit-length (see TPVE theory).

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Transformer depth enables multiple sequential transformations of position-wise embeddings; iterative computations can be implemented by repeated layer transforms.

inference: A minimal procedural decomposition of digit-wise addition requires at least four coarse pipeline steps per digit (read digit pair, compute partial sum, propagate carry, write output) motivating the approximately 4*D depth scaling.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior analyses show transformers can implement iterative computations and that depth correlates with algorithmic capacity; work exists on emergent arithmetic-like behaviors without an explicit numeric layer-to-digit rule.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete fork with a precise numeric threshold L = 4 * D, a negative prediction about held-out accuracy, and a single concrete ablation (final linear projection randomization) as a discriminating test between algorithmic and memorized solutions.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Builds on existing qualitative ideas but commits to a falsifiable numeric boundary and concrete experiments.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Train transformers (width 1024, 16 heads) on D-digit addition with D in {2,4,6} using 15% uniform coverage of the input space. For each D train models with depths L={D,2D,3D,4D,5D} to convergence. Evaluate exact-match on unseen 85% holdout, exact-match on place-value-permuted inputs, and accuracy after replacing the final learned linear output projection with a fixed random matrix. Support is: L>=4D models have >90% holdout accuracy, >85% permutation accuracy, and >50-point drop after projection randomization. Otherwise falsified.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: Any trained model with L >= 4 * D that (a) has held-out exact-match accuracy <= 90% given sufficient training, or (b) shows <30 percentage-point drop in exact-match accuracy when the final learned output projection is randomized, falsifies this statement.

new_predictions_likely: For D=5, a transformer with L >= 20 layers (width 1024, 15% data coverage) will reach >90% exact-addition accuracy on held-out 5-digit pairs after convergence; a model with L <= 16 will remain <=60% exact accuracy.

Randomizing the final linear output projection in models with L >= 4*D will reduce exact-addition accuracy by >50 percentage points, while shallow models (L < 4*D) will change by <15 percentage points.
Increasing width by 4x while holding depth L < 4*D will not raise held-out exact-addition accuracy above 70% on D-digit tasks sampled with 15% coverage.
new_predictions_unknown: Whether the numeric multiplier '4' is universal across bases (binary, hex) or changes predictably as 4 * log_base(10) scaling; empirical measurement required.
Whether pretraining on large language corpora containing numeric patterns shifts the threshold L = 4*D downward by an additive constant or multiplicative factor.
Whether more expressive attention variants (sparse or linearized attention) alter the multiplier from 4 to a different integer.
negative_experiments: Train a model with L >= 4*D and observe held-out exact accuracy <=60% despite convergence; if so, this refutes LLLA (fork goes the other way).
Train models with L < 4*D that nonetheless achieve >90% held-out exact accuracy and robust permutation invariance; this also falsifies LLLA.

unaccounted_for.text: Pretrained language models that learned numeric patterns in context may use external statistical heuristics and cached phrase-level patterns; LLLA does not predict pretraining transfer magnitudes.

special_cases: Chain-of-thought / explicit carry supervision reduces the required depth by up to 2x.
Tokenizations that group digits change effective D and thus the layer requirement.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Work showing transformers can learn iterative algorithms and that depth correlates with ability to perform algorithmic tasks.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Explicit multiplicative layer-to-digit threshold (L = 4 * D) and concrete ablation discriminators.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Extends qualitative algorithmic-learning claims with a crisp numeric boundary and falsifiable predictions.

theory-17.name: Sparse-Memory Token Lookup (SMTL)
theory-17.description: When training coverage of the mapping from inputs to outputs exceeds a coverage threshold, language models preferentially implement sparse memorized lookup encodings rather than algorithmic compositional arithmetic. This leads to near-perfect accuracy on seen pairs and sharply degraded generalization on unseen pairs; the switch point is a training-coverage numeric threshold independent of depth in the high-capacity regime.
theory-17.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can perform arithmetic.

components.theory_description: When training coverage of the mapping from inputs to outputs exceeds a coverage threshold, language models preferentially implement sparse memorized lookup encodings rather than algorithmic compositional arithmetic. This leads to near-perfect accuracy on seen pairs and sharply degraded generalization on unseen pairs; the switch point is a training-coverage numeric threshold independent of depth in the high-capacity regime.

theory_statements.statement_name: Coverage fork for memorization vs algorithmic arithmetic
theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For D-digit base-10 addition, let training coverage C be the fraction of all possible operand pairs seen during training. If C >= 0.25 then Mechanism B (sparse-memory lookup tables stored in embedding/output layers) dominates; if C < 0.25 then Mechanism A (algorithmic compositional arithmetic that generalizes to unseen pairs) dominates. Numeric threshold: C = 0.25 (25%). Negative prediction: For C >= 0.25, held-out exact-addition accuracy on unseen pairs will NOT exceed 55% despite near-perfect accuracy on seen pairs; for C < 0.25, held-out exact-addition accuracy on unseen pairs will NOT be below 85% for models meeting algorithmic capacity (e.g., depth >= 4*D per LLLA). Discriminating experiment: Train identical transformer models (width 1024, 16 heads, depth fixed at 4*D) on D-digit addition with datasets sampled at coverages C ∈ {0.05,0.15,0.25,0.4}. For each C measure: (1) exact-match accuracy on seen training pairs, (2) exact-match accuracy on the held-out unseen pairs, and (3) singular-value spectrum of the final token-output embedding matrix (to detect memorized lookup structure). If C >= 0.25 gives >98% seen accuracy but unseen accuracy <=55% and shows a low-rank plus sparse singular-value pattern consistent with memorized lookup, SMTL is supported. Falsifier: If models trained with C >= 0.25 routinely obtain >85% exact-match accuracy on unseen pairs without signs of sparse lookup embeddings (no large sparse singular values), or if models with C < 0.25 fail to reach >85% unseen accuracy given algorithmic capacity, this statement is falsified.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Decoder-only transformers trained from scratch on base-10 D-digit addition, tokenized one digit per token, model depth set at 4*D to ensure algorithmic capacity per LLLA; training data coverage C measured as fraction of all ordered operand pairs seen during training; width between 512 and 4096; no chain-of-thought supervisory signals.
special_cases: If training includes heavy repetition and curriculum that emphasizes rare pairs, memorization threshold C can shift downward (i.e., lower coverage suffices for lookup).
When the model capacity (parameters) is orders of magnitude larger than the mapping cardinality, memorization can occur at lower C.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Models with high parameter capacity can store many input-output associations in embedding and final-layer linear maps when those pairs are observed frequently.

inference: A sparse-memory implementation produces a characteristic embedding/output matrix with a few large singular values corresponding to memorized clusters and many small singular values, unlike distributed algorithmic implementations which produce smoother spectra.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Work on memorization in neural networks and dataset coverage influencing memorization vs generalization exists; prior descriptions are qualitative or capacity-based without a crisp 25% coverage fork for arithmetic.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A specific numeric coverage threshold (25%) that predicts a sharp switch to lookup-based arithmetic and a concrete spectral discriminator for memorization.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Translates general memorization principles into an explicit, falsifiable policy-relevant threshold and measurement.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Train transformers (depth fixed at 4*D) on D-digit addition with coverages C={0.05,0.15,0.25,0.4}. Measure seen vs unseen exact-match, and compute SVD of final output embedding matrix to detect sparse-large singular values. Confirm memorization if C>=0.25 yields unseen <=55% and SVD shows sparse dominant singular values. Otherwise falsified.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: If models trained with C >= 0.25 achieve >85% exact-match on unseen pairs without spectral signatures of memorization, or if models with C < 0.25 fail to exceed 85% unseen accuracy when depth=4*D, this statement is falsified.

new_predictions_likely: At D=4 with depth L=16, a model trained with C=0.4 will show >99% accuracy on seen pairs and <=50% accuracy on unseen pairs; its final output embedding will show 2–5 singular values >10x the next singular values.
At D=4 with C=0.15 and L=16, the same architecture will achieve >85% accuracy on unseen pairs, consistent with algorithmic generalization.
For fixed parameter count, increasing C from 0.15 to 0.3 will produce a discontinuous drop in unseen accuracy (from >85% down to <60%) once C crosses ≈0.25.
new_predictions_unknown: Whether the 25% threshold shifts significantly for different bases (binary vs decimal) or for asymmetric tasks (e.g., multiplication), and whether the threshold is sensitive to loss function choices.
Whether pretraining on large corpora dramatically increases the model's tendency to memorize numeric mappings at lower C.
Whether curriculum learning that prioritizes rare pairs prevents the switch to memorization even at C>=0.25.
negative_experiments: Train at C=0.3 and find unseen accuracy >85% with no spectral memorization signature; this refutes SMTL.
Train at C=0.1 and observe unseen accuracy <=55% despite depth=4*D; this refutes SMTL.

unaccounted_for.text: Effects of pretraining and natural-language co-occurrence statistics on numeric memorization are not modeled here.

special_cases: Very large parameter counts relative to mapping size can enable memorization at lower coverage C.
Curriculum or targeted sampling can shift threshold.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Literature on memorization and dataset coverage in deep networks; studies showing lookup-like behavior when mappings are heavily sampled.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Numeric 25% coverage fork and spectral singular-value discriminator for memorization vs algorithmic arithmetic.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Applies memorization principles to arithmetic tasks with a crisp, testable coverage threshold.

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can perform arithmetic.

theory-18.name: Carry-sweep Attention Mechanism (CSAM)
theory-18.description: Addition is implemented by repeated localized attention 'sweeps' across digit positions that sequentially propagate carries. Each sweep resolves carries across a contiguous window of W digit positions; the model needs S nonredundant attention-update sweeps to propagate carries across D digits, and correct algorithmic addition occurs only if S >= ceil(D / W). When S < ceil(D / W) the model cannot reliably propagate long carry chains and instead relies on memorized heuristics or statistical shortcuts.
theory-18.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can perform arithmetic.

components.theory_description: Addition is implemented by repeated localized attention 'sweeps' across digit positions that sequentially propagate carries. Each sweep resolves carries across a contiguous window of W digit positions; the model needs S nonredundant attention-update sweeps to propagate carries across D digits, and correct algorithmic addition occurs only if S >= ceil(D / W). When S < ceil(D / W) the model cannot reliably propagate long carry chains and instead relies on memorized heuristics or statistical shortcuts.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For exact addition of two D-digit base-10 integers tokenized at single digits, if the model provides S attention-update sweeps and attention locality window W (digits) such that S >= ceil(D / W) then carry propagation proceeds algorithmically by localized sweeps (Mechanism A dominates); if S < ceil(D / W) then carry propagation fails systematically and the model relies on memorized or statistical heuristics (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: S = ceil(D / W). Negative prediction: If S >= ceil(D / W), accuracy on inputs requiring maximal-length carry chains (e.g., 999... + 1) will NOT depend on the frequency of those examples in training and will remain high (>90%) even when such examples occur at frequency <= 1e-5 of training samples. Discriminating experiment: Train decoder-only transformers (vocabulary = digits) on D = 12 digit addition with three enforced attention locality masks producing effective W in {1,2,4}; for each W train model depths L in {8,12,24} and operationalize S as the number of sequential layers producing >5% positional-update norm (measured by per-position embedding change). Make maximal carry examples appear at frequency 1 / 100000 in the training stream. Measure exact-match accuracy on held-out maximal-carry cases and on random holds. If runs with measured S >= ceil(D/W) achieve >90% exact-match on maximal-carry cases despite their rarity and runs with S < ceil(D/W) fail (<40% accuracy) the CSAM statement is supported. Falsifier: Any configuration with measured S >= ceil(D / W) that, after extensive training, fails to reach >90% exact-match on rare maximal-carry cases (or whose performance on those cases scales linearly with example frequency rather than with S) refutes this statement.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Decoder-only transformers trained for token-level (single-digit) exact integer addition in base-10, where attention locality can be enforced by sparse-masking; S is measured as the count of layers producing nontrivial cross-position update (operationalized via embedding-change norm). Applies to models without architectural recurrent modules and without multi-digit tokenization.
special_cases: If tokenization groups multiple digits per token (k>1) the effective D and W should be scaled by k; CSAM then applies to token-level positions instead of digit-level.
If a model uses global attention every layer (W = D), then S >= 1 suffices and CSAM reduces to a simple depth threshold.
If residual paths are surgically altered (e.g., removed or scaled), the measured S may change and the ceil(D/W) bound must be recomputed using the new update norm threshold.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Hardware ripple-carry adders propagate carries sequentially across adjacent bits/digits; an analogous algorithmic requirement exists for any architecture constrained to local communication per update step.

inference: Attention implements the local communication channel in transformers; restricting attention locality W increases the number of sequential update steps required to move information across D positions, yielding the bound S >= ceil(D/W).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Analogy between ripple-carry adders and sequential neural propagation, and prior work connecting sparse/local attention to algorithmic computation.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: An explicit, falsifiable quantitative sweep-count bound S >= ceil(D/W), a concrete negative prediction about robustness to rare maximal-carry examples, and a precise discriminating experiment manipulating W and S.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Translates hardware adder intuition into an explicit transformer-attention bound that is directly testable and numerically specified.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Train transformer decoders (embedding dim 512) on 12-digit addition with W enforced via sparse masks {1,2,4}. For each mask, train depths L in {8,12,24}. Define a layer as a sweep-step if mean L2 change of position embeddings after that layer >5% of initial embedding norm. Compute S as count of such layers. Insert maximal-carry examples at frequency 1/100k. Evaluate exact-match on maximal-carry held-out set and on random sums. Compare accuracy vs S and vs example frequency. Success pattern: accuracy >90% when S >= ceil(12/W) and <40% otherwise. Alternative patterns falsify CSAM.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: Measured S >= ceil(D/W) but model achieves <90% accuracy on rare maximal-carry cases after extensive training, or performance correlates strongly with example frequency and not S.

new_predictions_likely: Numeric: For D=12 and enforced W=2, models with measured S >= 6 will achieve >90% exact-match on 12-digit maximal-carry examples that appear at frequency 1/100k in training; models with S <=5 will be <40% accurate on those examples.
Architectural: Imposing strict locality (small W) increases required depth approximately proportional to W to reach equivalent carry performance compared to dense-attention models.
Data-efficiency: In the S >= ceil(D/W) regime, increasing the frequency of maximal-carry examples by 10x will produce <2% absolute improvement in exact-match (diminishing returns).
new_predictions_unknown: Whether learned attention patterns will automatically widen effective W during training to avoid the sweep requirement (i.e., whether models can bypass enforced masks by learning alternative routing).
How large-scale pretraining on natural text with implicit numeric patterns alters effective S or W for digit-addition tasks.
Whether the sweep-count bound holds in mixed-radix or floating-point addition representations without modification.
negative_experiments: Fork test: Enforce W=1 and train a very deep model such that measured S >> ceil(D/W); if the model still fails to compute maximal-carry cases reliably despite S >= ceil(D/W), CSAM is decisively refuted.

Fork test: Keep S < ceil(D/W) but massively increase frequency of maximal-carry examples (to 1/100 of training); if the model then reliably computes maximal-carry examples, this supports memorization-over-sweep; if not, that outcome also discriminates mechanisms.

unaccounted_for.text: Mechanisms that exploit tokenization shortcuts (multi-digit tokens) or positional embeddings that directly encode magnitude may bypass sweep dynamics; CSAM does not model those bypasses explicitly.

special_cases: When W = D (global attention), S >= 1 suffices and CSAM collapses to a depth-threshold condition.
When architectures include explicit recurrence, S should count recurrent steps as sweeps; the ceil(D/W) mapping still applies with S counting recurrent steps.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Sparse/local attention and analogies to ripple-carry algorithms have been discussed; prior work has not given this precise sweep-count bound or the negative prediction about rare-case robustness.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Explicit quantitative bound S >= ceil(D/W), the prediction about independence from example frequency in the algorithmic regime, and a concrete experimental protocol to discriminate sweep-based vs memorization behaviors.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Provides a testable numeric criterion translating digit-distance, attention locality, and effective depth into a carry-propagation requirement.
