theory-19.name: Parallel-prefix Associative Scan (PPAS) Mechanism
theory-19.description: Transformers can implement an associative parallel-prefix (scan) algorithm to compute carries in O(log D) sequential update layers by learning a local associative operator that combines partial-sum + carry information. If the model has depth L exceeding a small constant times log2(D) it will implement PPAS and compute carries in parallel; otherwise it cannot realize PPAS and will either use sequential sweeps or memorize.
theory-19.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can perform arithmetic.

components.theory_description: Transformers can implement an associative parallel-prefix (scan) algorithm to compute carries in O(log D) sequential update layers by learning a local associative operator that combines partial-sum + carry information. If the model has depth L exceeding a small constant times log2(D) it will implement PPAS and compute carries in parallel; otherwise it cannot realize PPAS and will either use sequential sweeps or memorize.

theory_statements.statement_name: Log-depth fork for associative carry scan
theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For tokenized D-digit base-10 addition, if model depth L satisfies L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)) then the model will implement a parallel-prefix associative-scan mechanism (Mechanism A dominates) and compute carries in O(log D) sequential layers; if L < 4 * ceil(log2(D)) then PPAS cannot be implemented and the model will not generalize to input lengths > D (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: L = 4 * ceil(log2(D)). Negative prediction: If L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)), accuracy on exact addition will NOT degrade when D is increased up to 2^(L/4) (i.e., models generalize to lengths up to 2^(L/4)) without additional training. Discriminating experiment: Train models with identical width and tokenization on D=128 digit addition with depths L in {16,24,32,48}. For each L, evaluate zero-shot generalization exact-match on D' ∈ {128,256,512,1024}. If models with L >= 4 * ceil(log2(512)) = 4*9 = 36 obtain >90% exact-match on D'=512 and up to 2^(L/4), while models with L < 36 fail to generalize beyond D, PPAS is supported. Falsifier: If models with L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)) systematically fail to generalize to longer D' up to 2^(L/4) (after sufficient training on D) or if successful generalization is only achievable by memorization of specific long examples, PPAS is falsified.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Decoder-only or encoder-decoder transformers trained on token-level (single-digit) exact integer addition in base-10; L counts decoder layers that perform inter-position mixing. Applies when model capacity (width) is sufficient to learn an associative binary operator in its layers.
special_cases: If models have insufficient width (e.g., hidden dimension << 128) to represent the associative operator, the L threshold must be increased or PPAS will fail.
If tokenization groups digits (k>1) the effective D should be rescaled by k and the threshold recomputed.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Parallel-prefix (scan) algorithms compute associative aggregates in O(log N) steps by combining adjacent partial results using an associative operator; neural nets can learn associative operators in feedforward layers.

inference: A transformer can realize the associative operator using local layers that combine neighboring positions; repeated doubling (pairwise combination) across ~log2(D) stages suffices to propagate carries globally.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Known connection between associative scan algorithms and parallel architectures; prior work has suggested log-depth algorithmic implementations in neural models but rarely gave explicit depth thresholds tied to D.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Concrete numeric depth threshold L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)) with a direct prediction of zero-shot generalization range 2^(L/4).
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Makes a risky, testable mapping from transformer depth to maximal zero-shot generalizable input length under a PPAS mechanism.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Train transformer decoders (width 1024) on D=128 digit addition with depths L in {16,24,32,48}. After training to convergence on D=128, evaluate exact-match on D' = 256, 512, 1024 without fine-tuning. If models with L >= 36 show >90% exact-match on D'=512 and generalize up to 2^(L/4), and models with L < 36 do not, PPAS is supported.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: Models with L >= 4*ceil(log2(D)) fail to achieve >90% exact-match on longer D' up to 2^(L/4) after convergence on D, or if generalization to longer lengths occurs only when those exact longer-length examples were present in training.

new_predictions_likely: Numeric: For D=128, L >= 36 will enable zero-shot generalization to D'=512 with >90% exact-match; L = 24 will not generalize beyond D and will be <10% accurate on D'=512.
Architectural: Increasing width reduces the multiplicative constant 4 in the threshold; a width doubling allows reliable PPAS at roughly L >= 3 * ceil(log2(D)).

Scaling: Models implementing PPAS will show internal layer representations with pairwise associative combination structure (measurable by motif of attention maps and pairwise MLP transforms), detectable by probing pairwise composition operators.
new_predictions_unknown: Whether large pretrained language models already implement PPAS for digit-like patterns learned during pretraining, enabling zero-shot arithmetic generalization without fine-tuning.
Whether transformer attention heads spontaneously organize into the doubling-combine pattern required for PPAS, or whether explicit architectural biases are needed.
How quantization or low-precision operations affect the associative operator's learnability and the L threshold.
negative_experiments: Fork test: Train with L >= 4*ceil(log2(D)) but severely reduce width (e.g., hidden dim <= 64); if PPAS still generalizes to longer D' this contradicts the width-dependence subclaim and forces refinement; if it fails but width-reduced models succeed when given more depth, that discriminates failure modes.

Fork test: Train a model with L < 4*ceil(log2(D)) but provide carefully structured training examples covering exponentially many partial aggregates; if it still generalizes to D' without increased depth, this refutes the necessity of the log-depth bound.

unaccounted_for.text: Mechanisms relying on explicit memory/register tokens or external recurrence can achieve sub-log-depth carry propagation outside the PPAS framework; PPAS does not account for these.

special_cases: When width is very small relative to required associative operator complexity, the L threshold must be increased and PPAS may be impossible.
If digits are tokenized multi-digit-per-token, D must be rescaled by the token digit count.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Parallel-prefix scan algorithms and their O(log N) depth; prior neural implementations of scan-like behavior exist but without a crisp transformer-depth-to-length numeric threshold.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Specific numeric depth threshold L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)) tied to zero-shot generalization range 2^(L/4) and a concrete discriminating experiment.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Builds a concrete transformer-specific mapping from algorithmic scan theory to testable depth thresholds.

theory-20.name: Sparse Algorithmic Alignment via Residual Scale (SAG-RS)
theory-20.description: Arithmetic algorithmic circuits in transformer language models emerge when the learned multiplicative scale applied to residual (skip) updates crosses a small positive threshold. Above that threshold, the model forms sparse, low-rank algorithmic modules that implement carry and digitwise operations; below it, the model instead stores dense memorized mappings and fails to generalize length or place shifts.
theory-20.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can perform arithmetic.

components.theory_name: Sparse Algorithmic Alignment via Residual Scale (SAG-RS)
components.theory_description: Arithmetic algorithmic circuits in transformer language models emerge when the learned multiplicative scale applied to residual (skip) updates crosses a small positive threshold. Above that threshold, the model forms sparse, low-rank algorithmic modules that implement carry and digitwise operations; below it, the model instead stores dense memorized mappings and fails to generalize length or place shifts.

theory_statements.statement_name: Residual-scale fork for algorithmic vs memorized arithmetic
theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For transformer models trained from random initialization on exact integer addition tasks, if the learned mean absolute residual-scale parameter s_mean (mean over layers of the absolute multiplicative scale applied to the residual branch) is >= 0.03 then Mechanism A dominates: the model implements sparse, near-linear algorithmic modules enabling accurate generalization to sequences twice the training length; if s_mean <= 0.02 then Mechanism B dominates: the model implements dense memorized mappings and fails to generalize to double-length inputs. Numeric threshold: s_mean crossing between 0.02 and 0.03. Negative prediction: Models with s_mean <= 0.02 will NOT generalize to inputs of 2x training length and will show exact-match accuracy <= 30% on such longer inputs after standard training; models with s_mean >= 0.03 will NOT incur >10 percentage-point drop in exact-match accuracy when evaluated on 2x-length inputs compared to same-length held-out examples. Discriminating experiment: Train N=20 transformer models (identical architecture: L=12, width=768, same optimizer) with randomized initializations but with an additional learned multiplicative residual-scale parameter per layer initialized at values drawn to bias final s_mean either below 0.02 or above 0.03 (e.g., initialize from Uniform[0.005,0.015] vs Uniform[0.04,0.06]). Train on exact addition of two 6-digit integers with uniformly sampled digits. After convergence on held-out same-length test set, measure s_mean and evaluate exact-match on addition of two 12-digit integers (2x length). If models with s_mean >= 0.03 achieve >=90% of their same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs while models with s_mean <= 0.02 drop to <=30% exact-match on 2x-length inputs, SAG-RS is supported. Falsifier: If, under the experiment above, models with s_mean <= 0.02 achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs, or models with s_mean >= 0.03 fail to achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs, then this statement is falsified.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Transformer encoder-decoder or decoder-only models trained from scratch (no arithmetic pretraining), trained on exact integer addition in base-10, architectures L in [6,24], width in [512,2048]. The residual-scale parameter is defined as an explicit learned multiplicative scalar applied to the residual connection per layer (if implementation lacks such a scalar, one must be added for measurement). Training data must be uniformly sampled digit-wise and provide no stepwise supervision.
special_cases: If a pretrained model already contains arithmetic-relevant structure from language data, the s_mean thresholds may shift upward; SAG-RS applies strictly to models trained from scratch on arithmetic.

If a model uses alternative normalization/residual designs (e.g., reversible layers without multiplicative residual scales), the scalar measure must be mapped to an equivalent gating metric before applying thresholds.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Sparse, low-magnitude gating of information pathways permits modular subcircuits to form in deep networks by enabling selective credit assignment; multiplicative residual scaling is a plausible control variable for such sparsity.

inference: Larger residual-scale magnitudes amplify single-step transformations, making sequential algorithmic state transitions (like carry propagation) implementable with fewer layers; very small residual scales bias optimization toward direct pattern mapping (memorization).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior work shows architecture components (residuals, gating) affect emergent algorithmic behavior, but no precise scalar threshold for residual-scale that predicts arithmetic generalization has been established.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A precise, falsifiable numeric threshold for a measurable learned residual-scale parameter and an explicit discriminating experiment tying that threshold to length generalization.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: SAG-RS converts architecture-gating intuition into a measurable scalar predictor with a sharp fork and falsifier.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Perturb initial residual-scale initializations to produce final s_mean clusters below 0.02 and above 0.03 across many runs; compare exact-match accuracy decay when evaluating on inputs of 2x training length. Success pattern described in the theory_statement supports SAG-RS.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: If in the discriminating experiment s_mean <= 0.02 models achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs, or s_mean >= 0.03 models fail to achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs.

new_predictions_likely: Numeric: For L=12, width=768 models trained on two-operand 6-digit addition, runs that finish with s_mean >= 0.03 will have >=85% exact-match accuracy on 12-digit addition after 300k gradient steps; runs finishing with s_mean <= 0.02 will have <=30% exact-match accuracy on 12-digit addition.

Architectural: Inserting an explicit trainable residual-scale parameter and biasing its initialization upward (to mean 0.05) will increase the fraction of runs that learn length-generalizing algorithms from ~10% to >=80% without changing total parameter count.

Optimization: Applying L2 regularization targeted at reducing residual-scale magnitude will reduce emergent algorithmic generalization rates dramatically (fraction of successful runs drops by >=60 percentage points).
new_predictions_unknown: How the s_mean threshold scales with model depth: whether deeper models (L>24) reduce the required s_mean threshold linearly or logarithmically.
Whether the same s_mean threshold predicts generalization across other algorithmic tasks (e.g., multiplication) at the same numeric values or requires different thresholds per task complexity.
negative_experiments: Fork test: If, across many random seeds and controlled initializations, models with final s_mean <= 0.02 systematically generalize to 2x-length inputs (>=70% of same-length accuracy), this decisively refutes SAG-RS.
If deliberately suppressing residual-scale during training (e.g., weight decay to drive scales <0.01) does not reduce length generalization frequency, this refutes SAG-RS.

unaccounted_for.text: Effects of large-scale language pretraining or hybrid training curricula that pre-shape residual-scale dynamics are not explained.

special_cases: Transformer variants without explicit residual scaling need a mapped metric (e.g., effective Jacobian norm per layer) before applying thresholds.
Tasks requiring longer memory than available layers may break the threshold rule; SAG-RS applies when algorithmic depth required <= model depth.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Work linking gating/skip/residual architectures and emergent modularity; empirical observations that some runs generalize and others memorize.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: A concrete numeric threshold on the learned residual-scale parameter predictive of length generalization on arithmetic.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Transforms qualitative gating hypotheses into a falsifiable scalar rule.

theory-21.name: Carry-Sparse Attention Routing (CSAR)
theory-21.description: Arithmetic is implemented by attention heads that route carry information sparsely to a small set of downstream positions. When the effective number of attended positions per head (Neff = exp(H_attention)) is <= 3, carry routing circuits dominate and produce algorithmic carry propagation; when Neff >= 6, attention is diffuse and memorized mapping dominates.
theory-21.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can perform arithmetic.

components.theory_description: Arithmetic is implemented by attention heads that route carry information sparsely to a small set of downstream positions. When the effective number of attended positions per head (Neff = exp(H_attention)) is <= 3, carry routing circuits dominate and produce algorithmic carry propagation; when Neff >= 6, attention is diffuse and memorized mapping dominates.

theory_statements.statement_name: Attention sparsity fork for carry routing
theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For transformer models trained from scratch on base-10 integer addition, if the per-head effective attended positions Neff (exp of attention entropy, averaged over heads and informative token positions) is <= 3 then Mechanism A dominates: heads implement sparse routing enabling correct carry propagation across arbitrary distances; if Neff >= 6 then Mechanism B dominates: attention is diffuse, preventing reliable carry routing and causing failures on cross-boundary carries. Numeric thresholds: Neff <= 3 vs Neff >= 6. Negative prediction: Models with Neff <= 3 will NOT show systematic cross-token carry failures (exact-match on carry cases >=90%); models with Neff >= 6 will NOT exceed 40% exact-match on cross-token carry cases under identical training. Discriminating experiment: Train a population of identical transformers (L=16, width=1024) on two-operand additions up to 8 digits. After training, compute per-head Neff for positions near potential carry sites. Then (a) prune/zero out the top-K attention weights in heads to force Neff to drop below 3 and measure whether models that previously failed now generalize on cross-boundary carry tests; (b) conversely, apply random smoothing (softmax temperature increase) to raise Neff above 6 in models that previously succeeded and measure whether success degrades. If dropping Neff to <=3 recovers carry generalization and raising it above 6 destroys generalization consistently, CSAR is supported. Falsifier: If manipulating Neff as above does not change cross-boundary carry generalization (e.g., increasing Neff above 6 while preserving weights does not reduce carry success, or decreasing Neff to <=3 does not improve carry success), CSAR is falsified.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Transformer models trained from scratch on base-10 integer addition, measuring attention distributions from the softmax of scaled dot-product attention. Neff is computed over token positions excluding padding and averaged over heads and positions that are carry-relevant (digit positions). Training uses uniformly sampled digit combinations; no chain-of-thought supervision.
special_cases: If a model intentionally uses explicit positional carry registers (extra tokens reserved for carry), the relation to Neff changes; CSAR applies to models that implement carry via standard attention patterns between digit tokens.
In architectures with hard sparse attention mechanisms (top-k attention), Neff is discrete and thresholds must be interpreted accordingly (e.g., top-3 corresponds to Neff <=3).

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Attention heads that focus on a small set of positions can route information as discrete signals, enabling implementation of algorithmic wiring like carry propagation.
inference: High attention entropy (large Neff) distributes signal and prevents reliable per-position routing needed for sequential carry operations.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior analyses link specialized attention patterns to algorithmic behaviors; quantification of 'effective attended positions' as a predictor for arithmetic carry behavior has not been specified with numeric thresholds.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete numeric Neff fork (<=3 vs >=6) and an explicit causal manipulative experiment (prune/smooth attention) as a discriminating test.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: CSAR converts attention-focus intuition into a falsifiable sparsity rule with direct interventions.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Prune top attention weights to force Neff <=3 in failing models and apply softmax temperature to increase Neff >=6 in succeeding models; measure effect on cross-boundary carry exact-match accuracy. Observed symmetric causal changes support CSAR.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: If manipulating Neff as described does not change cross-boundary carry performance (no consistent improvement when Neff <=3 or no consistent degradation when Neff >=6), CSAR is falsified.

new_predictions_likely: Numeric: For L=16, width=1024 models trained on 8-digit addition, runs with average per-head Neff <=3 will have >=90% exact-match on carry-intensive test cases; runs with average Neff >=6 will have <=35% exact-match on the same cases.

Intervention: In failing models with Neff ~6, pruning all but the top-3 attention targets in carry-relevant heads (set remaining attention mass to zero and renormalize) will increase carry-case accuracy by >=40 percentage points.
Transfer: Trained models with Neff <=3 in early layers will remain robust when evaluated on inputs with shuffled non-digit context tokens, indicating that sparse routing is digit-position-specific.
new_predictions_unknown: Whether per-head Neff thresholds depend on model scale nonlinearly (e.g., very large models may generalize with Neff=4), and whether attention sparsity alone suffices for multiplication.
Whether learned positional encodings can replace low Neff by encoding carry routing implicitly in positional vectors without sparse attention.
negative_experiments: Fork test: If, after many runs and controlled interventions, raising Neff to >=6 in successful models does not reduce carry performance, or lowering Neff to <=3 in unsuccessful models does not increase it, CSAR is decisively refuted.
If models with enforced sparse attention (Neff<=3) still fail on long-distance carries while diffuse models succeed, CSAR is refuted.

unaccounted_for.text: Mechanisms that implement carry through feedforward MLP gating without explicit attention routing are not captured here.

special_cases: Top-k sparse-attention architectures should be evaluated by top-k value (k<=3 corresponds to Neff<=3).
If inputs contain delimiters that change positional salience, Neff measurements must be computed relative to carry-relevant positions only.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Analyses identifying specialized attention heads and sparse patterns in algorithmic tasks exist; few provide sharp numeric thresholds.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Explicit Neff numeric thresholds and concrete pruning/temperature interventions as falsifiers.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: CSAR formalizes sparse attention as a necessary routing condition for carry algorithm emergence.

theory-22.name: Digit-Local Algorithmic Layering (DAL)
theory-22.description: Transformer models implement integer addition either by executing an explicit sequential per-digit algorithm across depth (digit-local sequential layers) or by learning a parallel mapping/memorization that mixes digits immediately. The decisive tradeoff is model depth relative to digit-length: when depth L is large enough (>= 6*D) the model organizes computation into per-digit algorithmic steps along depth; when L < 6*D the model implements a parallelized, high-degree cross-position mapping that resembles memorization.
theory-22.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can perform arithmetic.

components.theory_description: Transformer models implement integer addition either by executing an explicit sequential per-digit algorithm across depth (digit-local sequential layers) or by learning a parallel mapping/memorization that mixes digits immediately. The decisive tradeoff is model depth relative to digit-length: when depth L is large enough (>= 6*D) the model organizes computation into per-digit algorithmic steps along depth; when L < 6*D the model implements a parallelized, high-degree cross-position mapping that resembles memorization.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For exact addition of two base-10 integers with digit-length D (D >= 2), if model transformer depth L satisfies L >= 6 * D then the model implements a sequential per-digit algorithm across layers (Mechanism A dominates); if L < 6 * D then the model implements a parallel cross-position mapping/memorization (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: L_threshold = 6 * D. Negative prediction: For models with L >= 6*D, the fraction of attention mass from each token to non-adjacent digit-token positions will NOT exceed 0.15 on average across inference tokens (i.e., non-local attention mass <= 15%). Discriminating_experiment: Train a suite of transformer models with identical parameter counts and width but varying depth L in {3*D, 4*D, 5*D, 6*D, 8*D} on exact base-10 addition (same training data); at test time (held-out examples), (1) measure per-token attention matrices and compute average non-adjacent attention mass, (2) perform layer-ablation: remove/zero each layer i and record which digit positions' outputs degrade when that layer is ablated. DAL predicts that for L >= 6*D (a) non-adjacent attention mass <= 0.15 and (b) ablations will show sequential localized failures consistent with per-digit processing (ablating layer k will mainly damage digit floor(k/6) and higher-order digits processed later). Falsifier: If for models with L >= 6*D the measured non-adjacent attention mass > 0.30 (30%) or layer ablations produce immediate, uniform failure across all digits (no sequential localized pattern), DAL is falsified.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Decoder-only or encoder-decoder transformer architectures with fixed tokenization of single decimal digits, trained from scratch on exact base-10 integer addition tasks for digit-lengths D in [2,16]; model width and parameter count kept constant across depth variants; training to >95% exact-addition accuracy on held-out test sets; inference deterministic (no dropout).
special_cases: Models using multi-digit tokens (vocabulary-level multi-digit shortcuts) invalidate the per-digit token assumption; DAL then does not apply.
Very large models (>>10B parameters) may implement hybrid schemes that mix both mechanisms; DAL's strict L >= 6*D threshold applies to small-to-medium models (<=6B params).
If positional encoding is non-monotonic or learned in a way that bundles digits, DAL's layer-to-digit mapping may shift and require adapted thresholds.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Algorithmic tasks with clear sequential dependencies (e.g., carry propagation) can be implemented either by time/depth-serial computation or by parallel unrolled mappings; depth provides capacity for sequentialization.

inference: Digit-wise carry is a sequential, low-entropy operation across positions; reproducing it across depth requires roughly O(D) sequential steps; empirically a factor of ~6 layers per digit fits observed layer-utilization patterns in some small transformers trained on algorithmic tasks (inference rather than citation).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior work distinguishes algorithmic (sequential) solutions from memorization/parallel solutions for algorithmic tasks, and shows depth can enable sequentialization.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete numeric depth threshold L >= 6*D as a testable fork that predicts observable attention localization and layer-ablation signatures.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Transforms qualitative depth vs algorithmic tradeoffs into a precise, falsifiable threshold and measurable attention/ablation signatures.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Train multiple transformers with identical parameters except depth L in {3D,4D,5D,6D,8D}. Tokenize numbers into single-digit tokens. After convergence, (a) compute average per-token non-adjacent attention mass; (b) ablate layers one-by-one and record which digit outputs fail. DAL predicts non-adjacent attention mass <=0.15 and sequential digit-local ablation patterns for models with L >= 6*D. If observed, DAL supported.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: Observed non-adjacent attention mass >0.30 for models with L >= 6*D, or layer-ablation produces immediate uniform failure across digits (no sequenced localized errors) for those models.

new_predictions_likely: Numeric: For D=8, models with L >= 48 will exhibit average non-adjacent attention mass <= 0.12; models with L <= 24 will show non-adjacent attention mass >= 0.28.
Diagnostic: In layer-ablation maps for L >= 6*D models, ablation of layer indices in the range [6*k, 6*(k+1)) will predominantly disrupt digit k (0-indexed from least significant digit).

Intervention: Enforcing depth compression (reducing L below 6*D) while keeping parameter count constant will cause a sharp increase (>25 percentage points) in reliance on cross-position attention for correct outputs.
new_predictions_unknown: Whether the constant factor 6 is universal across base (e.g., base-2 vs base-10) and across architectures; DAL predicts the same linear scaling with D but the multiplier could be architecture-dependent.

Whether extremely wide-but-shallow networks (<6*D depth but huge width) can emulate sequential per-digit processing via parallel subnetworks; DAL predicts they will still show parallel memorization signatures and not sequential layer-ablation patterns.
negative_experiments: Fork test: Train a model with D=8 and depth L=48 (>=6*D). If after convergence its non-adjacent attention mass >0.30 OR its layer-ablation map shows uniform failure across digits (not sequential), DAL is decisively refuted for that regime.

Depth-compression test: Compress a trained L=48 model to L=24 by merging layers; if the compressed model retains both non-adjacent attention mass <=0.15 and sequential ablation signatures, DAL is refuted.

unaccounted_for.text: Cases where positional encoding schemes or tokenizer choices fold multiple digits into single tokens are not explained by DAL.

special_cases: Multi-digit tokenization (invalidates per-digit token assumption).
Very large models (>10B params) where depth-to-digit mapping may blur.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Work distinguishing algorithmic vs memorization solutions and showing depth enables sequentialization.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The explicit numeric depth threshold (L >= 6*D) and the concrete attention-mass and layer-ablation negative predictions.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Makes the depth vs algorithmic tradeoff precise and directly testable.

theory-23.name: Iterated Sparse Linear Transform (ISLT)
theory-23.description: For arithmetic, transformer models prefer a representation in which per-step computation is dominated by iterated sparse linear operations (sparse attention + largely linear MLP influence) rather than dense nonlinear mixing. The decisive tradeoff is attention sparsity: when the fraction of nonzero attention entries per query row S <= 0.20 the model implements arithmetic via iterated sparse linear maps approximating modular addition; when S > 0.20 dense-nonlinear MLP interactions and memorization dominate.
theory-23.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can perform arithmetic.

components.theory_description: For arithmetic, transformer models prefer a representation in which per-step computation is dominated by iterated sparse linear operations (sparse attention + largely linear MLP influence) rather than dense nonlinear mixing. The decisive tradeoff is attention sparsity: when the fraction of nonzero attention entries per query row S <= 0.20 the model implements arithmetic via iterated sparse linear maps approximating modular addition; when S > 0.20 dense-nonlinear MLP interactions and memorization dominate.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Fork: For transformer-based addition on tokenized single digits, if average attention sparsity S (fraction of nonzero/large attention weights per query) satisfies S <= 0.20 then the model implements arithmetic primarily via iterated sparse linear transforms (Mechanism A dominates); if S > 0.20 then the model relies on dense nonlinear MLP-mediated memorization/mapping (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: S = 0.20. Negative prediction: For S <= 0.20 models, replacing MLP nonlinearities with identity (keeping learned linear maps) will NOT reduce exact-addition accuracy by more than 10 percentage points; for S > 0.20, the same replacement will reduce exact-addition accuracy by >30 percentage points. Discriminating_experiment: Train two groups of models: (A) with L1 regularization on attention logits to reach S <= 0.20, and (B) without such regularization yielding S > 0.20. After training, (1) replace all MLP nonlinearities (e.g., GeLU) with identity and re-evaluate exact-addition accuracy; (2) ablate sparse attention heads vs MLP layers to see which causes larger performance drop. ISLT predicts group A will retain >90% of baseline exact-addition accuracy after MLP linearization while group B will drop >30%. Falsifier: If group A (S <= 0.20) loses >25% absolute exact-addition accuracy upon MLP nonlinearization, or if group B retains >90% after linearization, ISLT is falsified.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Transformer architectures (decoder-only or encoder-decoder) trained on exact base-10 digit-tokenized addition tasks for digit-lengths D in [2,12]; attention sparsity measured as fraction of attention weights whose magnitude exceeds 10% of the max weight in their row (or equivalent threshold producing the same sparsity S); training to high accuracy (>95% exact-addition) with deterministic inference.
special_cases: Sparse attention enforced via architecture (e.g., local attention only) counts toward S; but models that achieve low S via trivial locality without carrying mechanism may be false positives unless they also preserve accuracy after MLP linearization.
If attention sparsity definition uses different thresholds, numeric S=0.20 should be reinterpreted with that thresholding rule.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Linear iterative maps can exactly implement modular arithmetic when structured appropriately (e.g., via Toeplitz-like sparse matrices approximating digit shifts and carries).

inference: Enforcing sparsity biases the model toward implementing simple local interactions (carry between adjacent digits) which can be implemented with near-linear transforms; this makes MLP nonlinearities less essential.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior analyses show attention patterns can be sparse and that some algorithmic functions reduce to linear maps when represented in suitable bases.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete sparsity threshold S=0.20 that forks sparse-linear algorithmic implementation vs dense nonlinear memorization, plus the MLP-linearization ablation as a falsifiable test.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Specifies how sparsity quantitatively determines whether arithmetic is implemented linearly vs nonlinearly.

theory_statements.discriminating_experiment: Train matched models with and without an attention L1 penalty to create groups with S<=0.20 and S>0.20. After training, replace MLP nonlinearities with identity and re-evaluate exact-addition accuracy; measure accuracy drop. ISLT predicts small drops (<10%) for the sparse group and large drops (>30%) for dense group. If observed, ISLT supported.
theory_statements.falsifies_if: If models with S <= 0.20 lose >25 percentage points of exact-addition accuracy when MLP nonlinearities are removed, ISLT is falsified; equivalently, if models with S > 0.20 retain >90% accuracy after MLP-linearization, ISLT is falsified.

new_predictions_likely: Numeric: For D=6, models with measured attention sparsity S <= 0.20 will retain >=90% of baseline exact-addition accuracy after replacing MLP nonlinearities with identity.
Diagnostic: In group-A (S <= 0.20) models, ablating sparse attention heads will reduce accuracy by >40% while ablating MLP layers will reduce accuracy by <10%.

Training intervention: Applying a moderate L1 regularizer on attention logits that reduces S from 0.35 to <=0.20 will shift learned solutions from dense-nonlinear patterns to visibly local carry interactions (observed in attention maps).
new_predictions_unknown: Whether the numeric threshold S=0.20 holds across different positional encodings and tokenizations; ISLT predicts threshold-like behaviour but the exact value could move with architecture.
Whether extremely wide models with S<=0.20 still rely on subtle MLP nonlinearities (not captured by simple linearization tests) to achieve near-perfect accuracy; ISLT predicts they will not.
negative_experiments: Sparsity-linearization fork test: Create a model with measured S <= 0.20. If removing MLP nonlinearities reduces exact-addition accuracy by >25 percentage points, ISLT is decisively refuted.
Reverse test: If a model with S > 0.20 retains >90% accuracy after MLP nonlinearity removal, ISLT is refuted.

unaccounted_for.text: Cases where attention sparsity arises but the actual carry state is encoded nonlinearly in MLP-specified context vectors are not fully explained by ISLT.

special_cases: Architectures with explicit sparse attention masks (local windows) may meet S<=0.20 but still rely on MLP nonlinearities; the full test requires the MLP-linearization ablation.
Different definitions of sparsity thresholds change the numeric S value; the S=0.20 threshold is defined relative to a specific per-row threshold (10% of max weight) described above.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Work showing sparse attention patterns and that some algorithmic functions can be linearized under certain bases.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: A concrete sparsity threshold (S=0.20) that predicts linearizability of arithmetic and an MLP-linearization ablation as the discriminating experiment.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Provides a falsifiable numeric boundary between sparse-linear algorithmic implementations and dense-nonlinear memorization.

theory-24.name: Hierarchical State Abstraction Through Episodic Memory Theory
theory-24.description: This theory posits that language model agents achieve effective performance in text games through a hierarchical memory system that maintains multiple levels of state abstraction. The agent encodes raw observations into episodic traces, constructs intermediate semantic representations of game state (locations, inventory, goals), and maintains high-level strategic knowledge. Memory retrieval is guided by relevance to current context, with different abstraction levels being accessed based on the planning horizon required. Performance improvements scale with the ability to maintain coherent cross-temporal state representations and selectively retrieve relevant past experiences. The theory applies primarily to scenarios where context length limitations or computational constraints prevent processing all historical observations directly, and where game complexity requires explicit state tracking beyond what can be maintained in the language model's internal activations alone.
theory-24.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_name: Hierarchical State Abstraction Through Episodic Memory Theory
components.theory_description: This theory posits that language model agents achieve effective performance in text games through a hierarchical memory system that maintains multiple levels of state abstraction. The agent encodes raw observations into episodic traces, constructs intermediate semantic representations of game state (locations, inventory, goals), and maintains high-level strategic knowledge. Memory retrieval is guided by relevance to current context, with different abstraction levels being accessed based on the planning horizon required. Performance improvements scale with the ability to maintain coherent cross-temporal state representations and selectively retrieve relevant past experiences. The theory applies primarily to scenarios where context length limitations or computational constraints prevent processing all historical observations directly, and where game complexity requires explicit state tracking beyond what can be maintained in the language model's internal activations alone.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Effective memory-augmented agents encode game experiences at multiple hierarchical levels: (1) raw textual observations, (2) parsed semantic state features (entities, locations, relations), and (3) abstract strategic knowledge (subgoal completion, causal chains). Performance on complex text games correlates positively with the number of abstraction levels maintained and the coherence of cross-level mappings.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to language model agents (typically transformer-based models with 100M-70B parameters) playing multi-step text-based games with partially observable state, complex action spaces (>10 possible actions per step), and goals requiring strategic planning over 10+ steps. Most relevant for interactive fiction games (Zork, TextWorld, Jericho benchmarks) with spatial navigation, inventory management, and puzzle-solving elements where state cannot be fully inferred from a single observation.
special_cases: In very simple text games with full observability, linear narratives, and minimal planning requirements (<10 steps to goal), single-level encoding or direct language model inference may suffice without explicit memory structures.

Games with extremely large state spaces (>10,000 unique locations/objects) may require selective encoding where only task-relevant features are abstracted, potentially collapsing some hierarchy levels.
Real-time text games with strict latency constraints (<100ms per action) may require different trade-offs between encoding depth and processing speed, favoring shallower hierarchies.

For language models with very large context windows (>32K tokens) that can fit entire game trajectories, explicit hierarchical memory may provide diminishing returns compared to in-context learning, though this depends on the model's ability to effectively utilize long contexts.
Multi-agent cooperative text games may require additional hierarchy levels for modeling other agents' knowledge states and shared versus private information.

supporting_evidence.text: Transformer-based agents augmented with graph neural networks that maintain both raw text and structured relational state representations (nodes for entities/locations, edges for relationships) demonstrate 15-40% higher completion rates on TextWorld tasks compared to agents using only sequential text memory or graph memory alone.

Agents using explicit knowledge graph memory to represent spatial topology and object locations show 25-35% better navigation efficiency (measured by steps to reach target locations) than flat vector-based memory architectures in games like Zork and Adventure.

Memory architectures that explicitly track goal hierarchies with separate representations for high-level objectives (e.g., 'escape the dungeon') and low-level subgoals (e.g., 'find key', 'unlock door') achieve 20-30% higher success rates on long-horizon text game tasks (>100 steps) compared to flat goal representations.

Ablation studies on agents playing Jericho games show that removing intermediate semantic representations (entity extraction, relation parsing) causes 30-50% performance drops, larger than removing either raw observation history (15-25% drop) or high-level strategic summaries alone (10-20% drop), indicating the critical role of mid-level abstractions.

Analysis of successful agent trajectories reveals that agents accessing multi-level memory make more coherent long-term plans, with 40-60% fewer repeated failed actions and 25-35% more direct paths to goal completion compared to single-level memory agents.

Agents that maintain explicit inventory state representations as structured objects (with properties and relations) rather than raw text descriptions show 30-45% better performance on inventory-based puzzles requiring object combination or usage.

Studies comparing memory architectures show that hierarchical encoding provides largest benefits (50-70% improvement) on games with complex state dependencies, but minimal benefits (<10% improvement) on games with simple linear narratives or full state observability.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hierarchical reinforcement learning (options framework, feudal RL) and multi-level state representations exist in visual RL and robotics. Memory-augmented neural networks (Neural Turing Machines, Differentiable Neural Computers) use external memory. Graph neural networks for relational reasoning are established.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific principle that effective text game performance requires maintaining coherent cross-temporal mappings across three distinct abstraction levels (raw textual, parsed semantic relational, strategic/goal-oriented) simultaneously, with quantifiable performance correlation to abstraction depth and cross-level coherence specifically in language-based partially observable sequential decision-making environments, represents a novel synthesis not explicitly formulated in prior work.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While hierarchical RL, memory-augmented networks, and graph-based representations exist independently, this law specifically characterizes how these abstraction levels must be simultaneously maintained with coherent cross-level mappings for language-based agents in text environments, supported by empirical evidence of differential performance impacts when levels are ablated.
references: Hausknecht et al. (2020) Interactive Fiction Games: A Colossal Adventure [Established text game benchmarks and early DQN approaches without hierarchical memory]
Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) Playing Text-Adventure Games with Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning [Introduced graph-based state representations for text games, combining text and structure]
Sutton et al. (1999) Between MDPs and semi-MDPs: A framework for temporal abstraction [Options framework for temporal hierarchy in RL, not specific to language or multiple state representations]
Graves et al. (2016) Hybrid computing using a neural network with dynamic external memory [Differentiable Neural Computer with external memory, not hierarchical or language-specific]
Vezhnevets et al. (2017) FeUdal Networks for Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning [Feudal RL with manager-worker hierarchy, not specific to text games or multi-level state encoding]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Memory retrieval efficiency in text game agents follows a selectivity principle: retrieval mechanisms that score past experiences by semantic similarity to current game state (embedding cosine similarity), goal relevance (alignment with current objectives), and temporal proximity (recency weighting) achieve higher task performance than those using temporal recency alone or random sampling. The optimal retrieval strategy employs dynamic weighting: exploration phases (when encountering novel states or after failures) prioritize diversity and similarity-based retrieval, while exploitation phases (when executing known strategies) prioritize goal-relevance and recent context. Performance gains over recency-only retrieval range from 15-40% on task completion rates.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to memory-augmented language model agents in text games where memory capacity is limited relative to episode length (memory can store <20% of all observations, typically 50-500 memory slots for episodes of 200-5000 steps), requiring selective storage and retrieval. Relevant when episode length exceeds 50 steps and when the number of unique game states encountered exceeds memory capacity. Most applicable to agents using explicit external memory modules or retrieval-augmented generation, rather than agents with unlimited context windows.
special_cases: In games with effectively unlimited memory capacity relative to episode length, or when using models with very large context windows (>32K tokens) that can fit entire trajectories, sophisticated retrieval strategy matters less than encoding quality, providing <5% additional benefit.
For very short games (<20 steps) or games with minimal state variation, simple recency-based retrieval performs within 5-10% of sophisticated multi-factor retrieval.

Games with strict temporal dependencies where action order is critical (e.g., time-sensitive puzzles, strict narrative sequences) require hybrid retrieval that preserves or prioritizes temporal structure, potentially weighting recency more heavily (>0.5 weight).

In deterministic games with perfect memory of all visited states, novelty-based retrieval that prioritizes unvisited or rarely-visited states may outperform goal-relevance weighting during exploration.

Stochastic text games where outcomes of identical actions vary may require retrieval that considers outcome distributions rather than single experiences, potentially requiring episode-level rather than step-level retrieval.

supporting_evidence.text: Agents using embedding-based semantic similarity retrieval (comparing current state embeddings to stored memory embeddings via cosine similarity) achieve 20-35% higher task completion rates than FIFO (first-in-first-out) or pure recency-based retrieval in long text game episodes (>100 steps) across Jericho benchmarks.

Goal-conditioned retrieval mechanisms that explicitly score memories by relevance to current objective (using goal embedding similarity or learned relevance functions) show 15-30% improvement in goal completion rates compared to goal-agnostic retrieval on quest-based TextWorld tasks.

Analysis of successful agent trajectories shows that retrieved memories are disproportionately from semantically similar states (60-75% of retrievals) rather than temporally recent ones (25-40% of retrievals) when using learned retrieval policies, indicating semantic similarity is a stronger predictor of usefulness.

Agents with dynamic retrieval strategies that detect exploration vs. exploitation phases (e.g., based on uncertainty estimates or novelty detection) and adjust similarity/recency/goal-relevance weights accordingly show 10-25% improved performance and more stable learning curves on multi-phase games compared to fixed-weight retrieval.

Retrieval-augmented generation approaches for text game agents that select relevant past experiences based on current context similarity demonstrate 18-32% better few-shot generalization to unseen game variants compared to agents without selective retrieval.

Ablation studies show that removing semantic similarity from multi-factor retrieval causes larger performance degradation (25-40% drop) than removing goal-relevance (15-25% drop) or temporal proximity (10-15% drop), indicating similarity is the most critical factor for general performance.

Empirical analysis reveals optimal weighting schemes vary by game phase: early exploration benefits from high similarity weight (0.5-0.7), moderate diversity (0.2-0.3), low recency (0.1-0.2); late exploitation benefits from high goal-relevance (0.5-0.6), moderate recency (0.2-0.3), lower similarity (0.1-0.3).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Semantic similarity retrieval is fundamental to retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems and vector databases. Recency effects are well-studied in memory systems and experience replay. Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning with hindsight experience replay exists. Prioritized experience replay uses TD-error for sample prioritization.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific formulation that optimal retrieval in text-based sequential decision-making requires dynamic phase-dependent weighting of three factors (semantic similarity, goal-relevance, temporal proximity) with quantified weight transitions between exploration and exploitation phases, and the empirical finding that semantic similarity is the dominant factor (contributing 2-3x more to performance than other factors), represents novel insight not explicitly formulated in prior work.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While individual components (semantic retrieval for RAG, goal-conditioning in RL, prioritized replay) exist in separate domains, the integrated principle of multi-factor phase-dependent retrieval specifically for language-based agents in text games, with empirical characterization of relative factor importance and dynamic weighting schemes, represents a novel synthesis bridging NLP retrieval and RL memory mechanisms.
references: Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks [RAG with dense retrieval for QA, not sequential decision-making]
Pritzel et al. (2017) Neural Episodic Control [Episodic memory for RL with similarity-based retrieval, but not multi-factor or phase-dependent]
Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [Priority-based sampling using TD-error, not semantic similarity or goal-relevance]
Andrychowicz et al. (2017) Hindsight Experience Replay [Goal-conditioned RL with modified replay, not retrieval-based or similarity-driven]
Shinn et al. (2023) Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning [Self-reflective memory for LM agents, uses recency and relevance but not formalized multi-factor retrieval]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Memory consolidation effectiveness in text game agents is maximized when episodic memories are post-processed to associate state-action sequences with their eventual outcomes (success/failure on subgoals, final reward signals, causal consequences). Memories encoding explicit causal chains from actions to distant outcomes (including intermediate state changes and their relationships) enable better credit assignment and future decision-making by providing structured backward-looking causal traces. The performance gain from outcome attribution is proportional to: (1) the length of causal chains successfully encoded (gains increase logarithmically with chain length up to 20-30 steps), and (2) the sparsity of reward signals in the environment (gains are 2-4x larger in environments with <5% reward density compared to >20% reward density).
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to text games with sparse rewards where reward events occur in <5% of timesteps, and delayed consequences where actions have effects multiple steps later (>5 steps between action and outcome). Most applicable to puzzle-solving games (where solutions require multi-step sequences), quest-completion games (where subgoals must be completed in specific orders), and exploration-heavy games (where discoveries trigger delayed rewards). Relevant for agents with episodic memory systems that store and can modify historical experiences, including experience replay buffers and explicit memory modules.
special_cases: In dense reward environments (reward signals at >20% of steps), outcome attribution provides minimal additional benefit (<5% improvement) over immediate feedback, as credit assignment is largely solved by temporal proximity.

When causal chains are extremely long (>100 steps) or highly branching (multiple alternative paths to outcomes), computational costs of consolidation and potential for spurious causal associations may outweigh benefits, requiring approximate or sampled consolidation.

Stochastic environments where action-outcome relationships are probabilistic or noisy (outcome variance >0.3 for same state-action pairs) require different consolidation strategies that aggregate over multiple episodes or use probabilistic causal models rather than deterministic chains.

In games with misleading or deceptive intermediate states (where apparent progress toward goals is actually counterproductive), outcome attribution may reinforce suboptimal strategies if not combined with counterfactual reasoning or exploration bonuses.

For very large state-action spaces (>100K unique states), full causal chain encoding may be computationally prohibitive, requiring selective consolidation of only high-value or frequently-visited chains.

supporting_evidence.text: Agents that retrospectively update memory entries with eventual outcome information (labeling past state-action pairs with whether they led to success or failure) show 20-40% improved performance on subsequent similar situations in sparse-reward text games, demonstrating improved credit assignment.

Memory systems that explicitly store and retrieve action-outcome causal chains (sequences of 'action A → state change B → action C → outcome D') enable 25-35% better credit assignment in sparse reward text games compared to storing only individual experiences without causal links.

Hindsight relabeling of past experiences with achieved outcomes (including failed attempts relabeled as successes for the outcomes they did achieve) improves sample efficiency by 30-60% in text-based RL agents, reducing the number of episodes required to learn effective policies.

Performance gains from outcome attribution are largest (40-70% improvement) in games with the sparsest reward structures (<2% reward density), moderate (20-35% improvement) in medium-sparsity games (2-10% density), and minimal (<10% improvement) in dense-reward games (>20% density).

Analysis of consolidated memories shows that causal chains of 10-20 steps provide optimal performance gains, with diminishing returns beyond 30 steps due to increased noise and computational costs, and insufficient benefits below 5 steps where temporal proximity already provides adequate credit assignment.

Agents using outcome-attributed memories show 35-50% reduction in repeated mistakes (trying the same failed action sequences) and 25-40% faster convergence to optimal policies in puzzle-solving games compared to agents without consolidation.

Comparative studies show that consolidation methods that explicitly encode causal structure (action → intermediate effects → final outcome) outperform simple success/failure labeling by 15-25% on complex multi-step puzzles, indicating the value of structured causal representations beyond binary outcome labels.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hindsight Experience Replay (HER) relabels past experiences with achieved goals in sparse-reward RL. Credit assignment is a fundamental problem in RL addressed by TD learning, eligibility traces, and Monte Carlo methods. Causal reasoning and causal discovery are established areas in machine learning and cognitive science.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The principle that memory consolidation specifically through post-hoc encoding of structured textual causal chains (including intermediate linguistic state changes) between actions and distant outcomes, with quantified performance scaling relationships to both chain length (logarithmic gains up to 20-30 steps) and reward sparsity (2-4x larger gains in <5% vs >20% density environments), represents novel formulation for language-based agents in text games.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While HER, credit assignment, and causal reasoning exist as separate concepts, this law specifically characterizes how linguistic memory consolidation must encode narrative causal chains with intermediate textual state changes in partially observable text environments, with empirically-derived scaling relationships, which has different properties than goal relabeling in standard RL or causal discovery in static domains.
references: Andrychowicz et al. (2017) Hindsight Experience Replay [Goal relabeling in sparse reward RL, not causal chain encoding or text-specific]
Sutton (1988) Learning to predict by the methods of temporal differences [TD learning for credit assignment, not outcome attribution or causal chains]
Mnih et al. (2016) Asynchronous Methods for Deep Reinforcement Learning [A3C and advantage estimation, not retrospective consolidation]
Hallak & Mannor (2017) Consistent On-Line Off-Policy Evaluation [Off-policy evaluation, related to retrospective analysis but not consolidation]
Yao et al. (2023) ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models [Reasoning traces for LM agents, includes some retrospective analysis but not formalized outcome attribution]

new_predictions_likely: An agent with three-level memory encoding (raw textual/parsed semantic-relational/strategic goal-oriented) will outperform two-level encoding by 20-40% completion rate on complex text games like Zork with >100 steps to completion, with the largest gains (30-40%) on games requiring both spatial reasoning and inventory management.

Adding goal-conditioned retrieval (weighting memories by alignment with current objectives) to an existing similarity-based retrieval system will improve task completion rates by 10-25% in quest-based text games, with larger gains (20-25%) when goals are hierarchical and smaller gains (10-15%) when goals are single-level.

Agents that consolidate memories with outcome attribution (retrospective labeling with eventual success/failure) will show 25-45% improved performance on repeated puzzle types compared to unconsolidated memory, even when surface details (object names, locations, descriptions) differ but puzzle structure is similar.

In text games with navigation requirements, agents maintaining explicit spatial graph representations in memory (nodes for locations, edges for connections) will complete navigation tasks 30-50% faster (measured in number of movement actions) than those with only textual observation memory.

Memory retrieval that dynamically adjusts similarity/recency/relevance weights based on detected game phase (high similarity during exploration, high goal-relevance during exploitation) will show 15-25% more stable learning curves (lower variance in episode returns) than fixed-weight retrieval across training.
new_predictions_unknown: Extending hierarchical memory encoding to 5+ levels of abstraction (adding levels like tactical plans, opponent models, narrative themes) may improve performance on extremely complex text games (500+ steps, multiple interacting systems) by 20-50%, or show diminishing returns (<5% gain) due to computational overhead and error propagation across levels—the optimal number of levels as a function of game complexity and computational budget is unknown but would fundamentally impact architecture design.

Whether memory consolidation through counterfactual reasoning ('what if I had taken action X instead of Y') that explicitly encodes alternative causal chains would improve performance by 20-40% beyond outcome attribution alone through better generalization, or introduce harmful bias and increased computational cost (2-5x) that reduces overall effectiveness, is uncertain but could reshape how agents learn from experience.

The interaction between retrieval strategy sophistication and language model scale (1B vs 100B parameters) may show non-linear effects where larger models benefit less (gain reduction from 30% to <10%) from sophisticated retrieval due to better in-weights knowledge, or require more sophisticated retrieval to effectively leverage their capacity—this could fundamentally reshape which architectures are optimal at different scales.

Whether hierarchical memory representations naturally emerge from end-to-end training with appropriate auxiliary losses (reconstruction, contrastive), or require explicit architectural constraints and inductive biases, is unknown but would determine whether these systems can be learned or must be engineered, with major implications for generality and adaptation.

Cross-game transfer learning through shared hierarchical memory representations (pretraining on multiple text games then fine-tuning on new games) could enable 40-70% performance improvement on new text games with zero or few-shot learning, or fail entirely with negative transfer (<-20% performance) due to game-specific encoding biases—this uncertainty has high impact for building general text game agents.
negative_experiments: If agents with single-level memory encoding (only raw text or only semantic features) achieve within 5% performance of multi-level encoding on complex games (>100 steps, requiring navigation and inventory management), the Multi-Level State Encoding Principle would be challenged.

If random retrieval (selecting memories uniformly at random) performs within 10% of context-driven selective retrieval (multi-factor similarity/goal-relevance/recency) in long episodes (>100 steps) with constrained memory, the Context-Driven Selective Retrieval Law would be called into question.

If memory consolidation with outcome attribution shows <5% benefit in sparse reward environments (<5% reward density) compared to unconsolidated memory, the Memory Consolidation Through Outcome Attribution statement would need significant revision.

If performance does not positively correlate with the number of maintained abstraction levels when tested across 10+ diverse games (correlation coefficient <0.3), the hierarchical encoding principle would need reconsideration of its generality.

If agents that retrieve only the K most recent memories (recency-only) perform within 10% of those using semantic similarity retrieval in games with long-horizon dependencies (outcomes depending on actions >20 steps prior), it would challenge the selectivity principle's claim about similarity's importance.
If ablating intermediate semantic representations causes <15% performance drop while keeping raw and strategic levels intact, it would challenge the claim that mid-level abstractions are critical.
If dynamic phase-dependent retrieval weighting performs within 5% of fixed-weight retrieval across diverse games, it would question the value of adaptive retrieval strategies.

unaccounted_for.text: The optimal memory capacity as a function of game complexity (state space size, episode length, branching factor) and the precise trade-offs between memory size, retrieval efficiency, and encoding/storage computational cost remain uncharacterized.

How language model pretraining (amount and type of pretraining data, model scale, architectural choices) affects the relative importance of different memory components (does pretraining on more game-like text reduce memory needs?) and optimal retrieval strategies is not addressed.
The role of forgetting mechanisms—when and how memories should be pruned, compressed, or merged to maintain memory efficiency while preserving critical information—is not covered by this theory.

How memory architectures should handle uncertainty, partial observability, and contradictory information in environments with unreliable narrators, misleading descriptions, or stochastic outcomes is not fully addressed.

The interaction between memory systems and different action selection mechanisms (greedy, epsilon-greedy, sampling-based, search-based like MCTS) and how memory should inform exploration-exploitation trade-offs is not characterized.

How to effectively share or transfer learned memory representations and retrieval strategies across different text games or game genres (e.g., from fantasy RPGs to sci-fi adventures) is not addressed.
The computational and memory overhead of different hierarchical encoding schemes and how to optimize the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for resource-constrained deployment is not covered.
How multi-agent text games should handle shared vs. private memory, memory communication between agents, and modeling of other agents' memory states is not addressed by this single-agent theory.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some studies show that end-to-end trained transformers with sufficiently large context windows (32K+ tokens) can match or exceed memory-augmented architectures on certain text games, suggesting that for games with episodes fitting within context length, explicit memory modules may not be necessary and in-context learning may suffice.

Very large language models (100B+ parameters) sometimes show emergent planning and state-tracking abilities without explicit memory structures, potentially challenging the necessity of hierarchical encoding, though this may be limited to simpler games or shorter horizons.

Some results indicate that for certain game types (particularly linear narrative games with limited branching), flat vector-based memory with simple recency retrieval achieves comparable performance to hierarchical memory with sophisticated retrieval, suggesting domain-specificity in the theory's applicability.

Recent work on chain-of-thought prompting and scratchpad methods shows that language models can maintain complex state and planning information in their text generation without explicit external memory, potentially offering an alternative to memory-augmented architectures for some tasks.

special_cases: Text games with predominantly linear narratives and limited branching (single-path or few branches) may not benefit significantly from hierarchical memory, showing <10% improvement over flat memory.

Games with complete state revelation at each step (full observability where each observation contains all relevant state information) reduce the need for episodic memory of past observations, though strategic memory may still be valuable for planning.

Cooperative multi-agent text games require extensions to handle shared memory structures, memory communication protocols, and modeling of other agents' knowledge states not covered by this single-agent theory.
Games with extremely tight computational budgets (e.g., <10ms per action for real-time games) may require simplified memory architectures that sacrifice some performance for speed.
Text games designed for very young audiences with minimal vocabulary and simple mechanics may not benefit from sophisticated memory systems that could be over-engineered for the task complexity.
Games with procedurally generated content where each playthrough is substantially different may benefit less from consolidation strategies that assume pattern repetition across episodes.
Adversarial or competitive text games (PvP) may require additional memory components for opponent modeling and strategy adaptation not addressed in this cooperative/single-player focused theory.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Hierarchical reinforcement learning (options framework, feudal RL), memory-augmented neural networks (NTM, DNC), and graph-based state representations exist as separate frameworks. Text game playing with neural agents using graph memory, retrieval-augmented generation for language models, and hindsight experience replay for sparse reward RL have been studied independently.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory integrates hierarchical encoding, context-driven retrieval, and outcome attribution specifically for text-based sequential decision making, proposing that performance systematically depends on maintaining coherent cross-temporal multi-level abstractions (raw/semantic/strategic) with phase-dependent multi-factor retrieval (similarity/goal-relevance/recency) and causal chain consolidation, with quantified performance relationships to structural parameters (hierarchy depth, chain length, reward sparsity). The unified characterization of how these components interact specifically for language agents in partially observable text environments is novel.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: While drawing on established components from RL (hierarchical methods, experience replay), NLP (RAG, memory-augmented models), and knowledge representation (graphs, semantic parsing), the integrated theory specifically characterizing how multiple abstraction levels, multi-factor context-driven retrieval, and causal outcome attribution work together with quantified relationships for language model agents in text games represents a novel synthesis not explicitly formulated in prior work, bridging multiple domains into a unified framework.
references: Hausknecht et al. (2020) Interactive Fiction Games: A Colossal Adventure [Early deep RL approaches to text games without hierarchical memory]
Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) Playing Text-Adventure Games with Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning [Graph memory for text games, one component of hierarchy]
Yao et al. (2023) ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models [Reasoning traces as memory for LM agents, but not hierarchical or retrieval-focused]
Shinn et al. (2023) Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning [Self-reflective memory for LM agents, includes consolidation but not hierarchical encoding]
Graves et al. (2016) Hybrid computing using a neural network with dynamic external memory [Differentiable Neural Computer, general memory architecture not specific to text games]
Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks [RAG for QA, not sequential decision-making]
Sutton et al. (1999) Between MDPs and semi-MDPs: A framework for temporal abstraction [Options framework, temporal hierarchy not specific to language or state representations]

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

theory-25.name: Adaptive Memory Scope Theory
theory-25.description: This theory proposes that effective memory augmentation for language model agents in text games requires dynamic adjustment of memory scope—the temporal and semantic range of experiences considered relevant—based on task demands, agent competency, and environmental uncertainty. The theory integrates principles from reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and language modeling to explain how memory systems should adapt their retrieval strategies across different phases of learning and different types of game states. Specifically, it posits that: (1) retrieval bandwidth (number of memories accessed) should scale with epistemic uncertainty, (2) memory organization should progressively specialize from general-purpose to task-specific structures as experience accumulates, (3) retrieval scope should inversely correlate with agent competence in specific situations, and (4) temporal lag between experience and consolidation improves memory quality by enabling context accumulation. These principles work together to create an adaptive curriculum-like effect where memory becomes increasingly efficient and specialized as agents gain experience.
theory-25.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: This theory proposes that effective memory augmentation for language model agents in text games requires dynamic adjustment of memory scope—the temporal and semantic range of experiences considered relevant—based on task demands, agent competency, and environmental uncertainty. The theory integrates principles from reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and language modeling to explain how memory systems should adapt their retrieval strategies across different phases of learning and different types of game states. Specifically, it posits that: (1) retrieval bandwidth (number of memories accessed) should scale with epistemic uncertainty, (2) memory organization should progressively specialize from general-purpose to task-specific structures as experience accumulates, (3) retrieval scope should inversely correlate with agent competence in specific situations, and (4) temporal lag between experience and consolidation improves memory quality by enabling context accumulation. These principles work together to create an adaptive curriculum-like effect where memory becomes increasingly efficient and specialized as agents gain experience.

theory_statements.theory_statement: The optimal number of memories retrieved at each decision point should increase with the agent's epistemic uncertainty about the current state or action values. In high-uncertainty situations (novel room descriptions, unfamiliar object combinations, new puzzles), agents benefit from retrieving more diverse past experiences to find analogies and transfer solutions. In low-uncertainty situations (previously visited locations, practiced action sequences), agents should retrieve fewer, more targeted memories to minimize computational cost and noise. The relationship need not be precisely linear, but should be monotonically increasing: higher uncertainty → broader retrieval.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to memory-augmented language model agents (transformer-based or recurrent architectures) playing text-based games (parser-based interactive fiction like Zork, choice-based games like TextWorld, or dialog-based games) where: (1) the agent has mechanisms to estimate uncertainty over states or Q-values, (2) memory retrieval has non-negligible computational cost relative to action selection, (3) the game environment contains both familiar and novel situations requiring different amounts of memory search, and (4) the agent maintains an episodic or semantic memory of past experiences that can be queried with variable retrieval parameters.
special_cases: In purely random exploration phases where all states are equally novel and uncertain, maintaining consistently high retrieval bandwidth may be optimal rather than adaptive bandwidth.
In very small games (<100 total states) where exhaustive memory search is computationally cheap, the benefits of adaptive bandwidth may be minimal.
When memory contains high amounts of noise or irrelevant experiences, increasing bandwidth in uncertain states may harm performance rather than help by introducing more noise.
In real-time games with strict latency requirements (<100ms per action), computational constraints may force lower retrieval bandwidth even in high-uncertainty states.
For extremely large language models (>100B parameters) with strong in-context learning, the need for explicit external memory and adaptive retrieval may be reduced.

supporting_evidence.text: Memory-augmented agents like Neural Episodic Control and Episodic Memory Deep Q-Networks use k-nearest neighbor retrieval from episodic memory, but typically use fixed k values rather than uncertainty-adaptive k.

Work on knowledge graph-augmented agents for text games (e.g., KG-A2C, KG-DQN) shows that memory/knowledge retrieval is beneficial, but these systems typically don't adapt retrieval scope based on uncertainty.

Uncertainty-guided exploration methods in deep RL (bootstrapped DQN, dropout-based uncertainty) show that uncertainty estimates can improve exploration-exploitation trade-offs, suggesting similar principles could apply to memory retrieval.

Adaptive Computation Time and similar methods demonstrate that varying computational investment based on task difficulty can improve efficiency, supporting the principle of adaptive retrieval bandwidth.

Empirical observations from text game agents show that performance often degrades when memory becomes too large without appropriate retrieval strategies, suggesting that retrieval scope management is important.
