novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Uncertainty-guided exploration is fundamental in RL (UCB algorithms, Thompson sampling, Bootstrapped DQN). Variable computation based on difficulty exists (Adaptive Computation Time). k-NN retrieval from episodic memory exists in NEC and related work.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific principle that memory retrieval bandwidth in language-based agents for text games should dynamically adapt to epistemic uncertainty—rather than using fixed retrieval parameters—represents a novel integration. While components exist separately, their synthesis for memory-augmented text game agents is not explicitly formulated in prior work.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While uncertainty-based exploration and episodic memory retrieval exist independently in the RL literature, and while some recent work explores memory for text games, the explicit principle of coupling retrieval bandwidth to uncertainty specifically for language model agents in text environments is not well-established. This represents a logical extension of existing ideas rather than entirely new concepts.
references: Auer et al. (2002) Finite-time Analysis of the Multiarmed Bandit Problem [UCB algorithm for uncertainty-based exploration in RL]
Osband et al. (2016) Deep Exploration via Bootstrapped DQN [Uncertainty estimation for deep RL exploration]
Pritzel et al. (2017) Neural Episodic Control [k-NN episodic memory for RL, but with fixed k]
Graves (2016) Adaptive Computation Time for Recurrent Neural Networks [Variable computation based on task difficulty]
Ammanabrolu & Hausknecht (2020) Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces [Knowledge graphs for text games, but fixed retrieval]

theory_statements.theory_statement: As language model agents accumulate experience in text game environments over extended training, effective memory organization evolves from general-purpose, unstructured storage toward specialized, functionally-organized subsystems. Early in training (few hundred episodes), agents benefit from flexible memory that stores diverse experiences without strong organizational structure. After substantial experience (thousands of episodes in complex games), performance is enhanced by memory subsystems specialized for different task types: navigation/spatial memory (room layouts, connections), inventory/object memory (item properties, combinations), dialogue memory (NPC interactions, conversation history), and puzzle/causal memory (action-outcome patterns). The degree of benefit from specialization correlates with the heterogeneity of task types in the game environment.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to memory-augmented language model agents learning complex text games over extended training periods (1000+ episodes or equivalent) where: (1) the game environment contains multiple distinct types of challenges (navigation, inventory puzzles, dialogue, combat, etc.), (2) the agent has sufficient memory capacity to support specialized subsystems, (3) training is long enough for organizational structure to emerge or be learned, and (4) the game is complex enough that task heterogeneity makes specialization beneficial. Most relevant for large-scale parser-based interactive fiction (Zork, Anchorhead) or complex procedurally generated environments (TextWorld with multiple task types).
special_cases: In single-task or homogeneous environments (e.g., games that are purely navigation or purely dialogue), specialization provides minimal benefit over general memory.
If the agent requires frequent transfer to new game variants or genres, excessive specialization may harm generalization and adaptability.
Very short training regimes (<500 episodes) may not provide sufficient experience for specialization to emerge or show benefits.
For small-scale games with limited state spaces, the overhead of maintaining specialized memory subsystems may outweigh benefits.
If using very large pre-trained language models with strong general capabilities, the marginal benefit of specialized memory organization may be reduced.

supporting_evidence.text: Knowledge graph approaches to text games (KG-A2C, KG-DQN) implicitly implement a form of specialized memory through structured graph representations of entities, relations, and spatial structure, showing benefits in complex games.
Work on modular reinforcement learning and neural module networks shows that task-specific modules can improve performance in multi-task settings, supporting the general principle of specialization.
Progressive Neural Networks demonstrate that maintaining task-specific structure while enabling transfer can be beneficial, analogous to specialized memory subsystems.

Observations from human play of complex text games show that expert players develop specialized mental models for different game mechanics (spatial reasoning for navigation, causal reasoning for puzzles), suggesting specialization is a general principle.
Multi-task learning research shows that task diversity correlates with benefits from specialization, supporting the claim that heterogeneous game environments benefit more from specialized memory.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Neural network modularity and specialization are well-studied (Neural Module Networks, Progressive Neural Networks). Knowledge graphs for text games provide structured memory. Multi-task learning with task-specific components exists.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The principle that memory organization in language-based agents should progressively transition from general to specialized over the course of extended learning in text games—with the transition dynamics and benefits correlating to environmental task diversity—represents a novel characterization. While components exist, the temporal evolution of memory organization specifically for text game agents is not explicitly formulated.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While modularity, knowledge graphs, and specialization are established concepts, this law specifically characterizes the temporal dynamics of how memory should evolve from general to specialized in language-based agents learning text games. The progressive aspect and the correlation with task diversity represent novel synthesis rather than entirely new concepts.
references: Rusu et al. (2016) Progressive Neural Networks [Task-specific columns with transfer, analogous to specialized memory]
Andreas et al. (2016) Neural Module Networks [Compositional task-specific modules]
Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) Playing Text-Adventure Games with Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning [Knowledge graphs as structured memory for text games]
Rosenbaum et al. (2018) Routing Networks: Adaptive Selection of Non-linear Functions [Dynamic task-specific routing]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Memory retrieval scope (both temporal range and semantic diversity) should vary inversely with agent competence in the current situation. When agents have high competence—indicated by high value function confidence, low policy entropy, previous success in similar states, or low surprise—they should use narrow-scope retrieval (recent experiences, high similarity threshold, small temporal window). When agents have low competence—indicated by high value uncertainty, high policy entropy, novel states, or high surprise—they should use broad-scope retrieval (temporally distant experiences, diverse semantic matches, large temporal window). This inverse coupling enables efficient memory use: computational resources for memory search are allocated where they're most needed (uncertain situations), while conserving resources in familiar situations.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to memory-augmented text game agents where: (1) the agent maintains estimates of competence, confidence, or uncertainty (value function uncertainty, policy entropy, state novelty scores, or prediction error), (2) retrieval scope can be adjusted via parameters (temporal window size, similarity threshold, number of retrievals), (3) the game environment has heterogeneous difficulty with regions where agent competence varies substantially, (4) memory retrieval has non-trivial computational cost, and (5) the agent's memory contains both relevant and potentially distracting experiences. Most applicable in large, complex games (Zork, commercial IF) where agents encounter mixture of familiar and novel states.
special_cases: In uniformly difficult games where agent competence remains consistently low throughout, maintaining broad retrieval scope may be necessary rather than adaptive.
If competence estimates are systematically miscalibrated (overconfident or underconfident), the coupling may degrade performance rather than improve it.
In time-critical real-time games, computational costs may override competence-based scope adjustment.
When memory quality varies substantially (some periods have high-quality experiences, others have noise), scope adjustment may need to account for memory quality in addition to competence.
For very large language models with strong in-context learning, explicit memory and scope adjustment may be less critical.

supporting_evidence.text: Prioritized experience replay in deep RL adjusts sampling based on TD-error (a form of competence/confidence), showing that adaptive memory access based on learning signals improves performance.

Work on analogical reasoning shows that when problem-solvers are uncertain, they benefit from searching more broadly for analogies, while confident solvers focus narrowly—supporting the inverse relationship.

Uncertainty-based exploration methods (Bootstrapped DQN, etc.) demonstrate that allocating computational resources based on uncertainty improves learning, suggesting similar principles apply to memory retrieval.
Case-based reasoning systems often use confidence-based retrieval strategies, retrieving more cases when confidence is low, supporting the general principle.
Observations from text game agents show that random or poorly-targeted retrieval can hurt performance in familiar situations, while broad search helps in novel situations.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Confidence-based action selection exists in RL. Prioritized replay adjusts sampling based on learning signals (TD-error). Analogical reasoning shows that uncertainty affects search breadth. Case-based reasoning uses adaptive retrieval.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific principle that memory retrieval scope width should inversely couple to agent competence in language-based sequential decision making for text games represents a novel synthesis. While components exist (confidence-based exploration, adaptive retrieval), their integration for memory scope in text game agents is not explicitly formulated.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While related concepts exist in RL (prioritized replay, uncertainty-based exploration) and cognitive science (analogical reasoning under uncertainty), the explicit inverse coupling between retrieval scope and competence specifically for memory-augmented language agents in text games represents a logical extension rather than entirely new territory.
references: Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [Adaptive sampling based on TD-error in RL]
Osband et al. (2016) Deep Exploration via Bootstrapped DQN [Uncertainty-based exploration]
Gentner (1983) Structure-Mapping: A Theoretical Framework for Analogy [Analogical reasoning and search breadth]
Aamodt & Plaza (1994) Case-Based Reasoning: Foundational Issues, Methodological Variations, and System Approaches [Adaptive case retrieval]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Effective memory augmentation in text game agents benefits from temporal lag between initial experience and consolidation into long-term memory. Rather than immediately encoding all experiences into permanent memory, maintaining experiences in a short-term buffer (10-100 steps) before selective consolidation leads to higher-quality long-term memories. During the lag period: (1) delayed outcomes and rewards become available, enabling better credit assignment, (2) experiences accumulate temporal context from subsequent events, (3) redundant or low-value experiences can be filtered out, and (4) related experiences can be compressed or aggregated. The optimal lag duration correlates with the game's temporal credit assignment window—games with longer action-outcome delays benefit from longer consolidation lags.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to text game agents with dual-buffer or multi-stage memory systems (short-term/working memory and long-term memory) in environments where: (1) actions have delayed consequences (typically >5 steps between action and outcome), (2) episodes are long enough for lag to matter (>100 steps), (3) rewards are sparse or delayed, (4) memory capacity is limited requiring selective consolidation, and (5) the agent can identify which experiences warrant long-term storage. Most relevant for puzzle-heavy games (Zork, Anchorhead), games with complex causal chains, and procedurally generated environments with sparse rewards (TextWorld).
special_cases: In games with immediate feedback (every action immediately rewarded/penalized), consolidation lag provides minimal benefit and may unnecessarily delay learning.
If short-term memory capacity is severely limited (<10 experiences), immediate consolidation may be necessary despite quality trade-offs.
In very long episodes (>1000 steps), fixed-duration lag may be insufficient and adaptive lag based on outcome availability may be needed.
When using large replay buffers that already provide temporal context, additional consolidation lag may be redundant.
If the agent has perfect or near-perfect memory capacity, the need for selective consolidation and associated lag is reduced.

supporting_evidence.text: Experience replay in deep RL maintains a buffer of experiences before use, demonstrating that temporal separation between experience and learning can be beneficial, though replay focuses on learning rather than consolidation.

Hindsight Experience Replay (HER) relabels past experiences with alternative goals after outcomes are known, showing that post-hoc processing of experiences improves learning—supporting the value of lag for context accumulation.
Memory consolidation theory from neuroscience shows that temporal delay and processing (sleep, replay) improves long-term memory quality, suggesting biological inspiration for the principle.
n-step returns in temporal difference learning show that incorporating delayed outcomes improves credit assignment, supporting the idea that waiting for outcome information enhances memory quality.

Empirical observations from text games show that agents often struggle with long-term credit assignment and delayed rewards, suggesting that mechanisms to better incorporate delayed information (like consolidation lag) could help.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Experience replay buffers are standard in deep RL. Memory consolidation from neuroscience involves time-dependent processes. Hindsight Experience Replay relabels experiences post-hoc. Multi-store memory models (working vs. long-term) are established.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific hypothesis that consolidation lag improves memory quality in language-based agents for text games by enabling temporal context accumulation and outcome information integration—with lag duration correlating to credit assignment windows—represents novel synthesis. While replay buffers exist, their framing as consolidation lag mechanisms with specific benefits for memory quality in text games is not explicitly established.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While experience replay and memory consolidation exist as separate concepts, this hypothesis specifically characterizes why and how temporal lag between experience and long-term storage improves memory quality in text-based sequential decision making. The explicit connection to credit assignment windows and the framing for text game agents represents extension of existing ideas.
references: Lin (1992) Self-improving reactive agents based on reinforcement learning [Early experience replay in RL]
Mnih et al. (2015) Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning [DQN with replay buffer]
Andrychowicz et al. (2017) Hindsight Experience Replay [Post-hoc experience relabeling showing value of delayed processing]
McClelland et al. (1995) Why there are complementary learning systems [Neuroscience theory of memory consolidation]
Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [Sophisticated replay management]

new_predictions_likely: Text game agents that adaptively increase retrieval bandwidth (number of retrieved memories) when entering novel rooms or encountering unfamiliar objects will show 10-25% better sample efficiency than agents with fixed retrieval bandwidth, particularly in games mixing familiar and novel content (e.g., TextWorld with progressive difficulty).

Agents using dual-buffer memory systems with 20-50 step consolidation lag will achieve 10-20% higher average reward in puzzle-heavy text games (Zork-style) compared to immediate consolidation, with the benefit increasing as puzzle complexity increases.

In multi-task text game environments (navigation + puzzles + dialogue), agents with functionally specialized memory subsystems will show performance improvements over general-purpose memory after 1000+ training episodes, with the magnitude of improvement correlating with task heterogeneity.

Memory systems that narrow retrieval scope (reduce similarity threshold, shorten temporal window) in high-confidence states will reduce computational overhead by 20-40% while maintaining or slightly improving performance compared to fixed-scope retrieval.
Text game agents that implement uncertainty-driven retrieval will show more balanced exploration, spending less time in local optima and discovering more solution paths than fixed-retrieval agents.
new_predictions_unknown: Whether there exists a universal mathematical function optimally relating epistemic uncertainty to retrieval bandwidth across different text game genres (parser-based IF vs. choice-based vs. procedural) is unknown but would be highly impactful—if universal, it would enable transfer learning of retrieval strategies; if game-specific, it would require per-environment tuning with implications for generalization.

The interaction between consolidation lag and online learning dynamics in text games may produce emergent curriculum effects (easy experiences consolidate early, creating scaffolding for harder content) or catastrophic interference (old memories interfere with new learning)—the outcome could fundamentally affect architecture design and would have major implications for continual learning in text games.

Whether progressive memory specialization can occur through purely end-to-end learning (emergent functional structure) or requires architectural inductive biases (hard-coded subsystems) is unknown but critical—if emergent, it would enable more flexible, general systems; if requiring biases, it would inform architecture design but potentially limit adaptability.

If specialized memory structures learned in one text game (e.g., spatial memory from Zork) effectively transfer to structurally different games (e.g., dialogue-heavy games), it would enable powerful meta-learning and amortization of experience across games. However, negative transfer (interference) is equally plausible, which would require game-specific memory adaptation.

The scaling behavior of these adaptive memory principles with language model size (1B vs. 10B vs. 100B+ parameters) may show threshold effects where sufficiently large models with strong in-context learning make explicit adaptive memory unnecessary—determining these thresholds would clarify when external memory augmentation provides value.
negative_experiments: If fixed-bandwidth memory retrieval (constant k nearest neighbors) performs as well as uncertainty-adaptive retrieval across diverse text games (parser-based IF, TextWorld, choice-based games), the Uncertainty-Driven Memory Bandwidth Principle would be called into question, suggesting that adaptive complexity is unnecessary.

If specialized memory structures (navigation memory, puzzle memory, dialogue memory) show no performance advantage over general-purpose unstructured memory even after extensive training (>5000 episodes) in heterogeneous environments, the Progressive Memory Specialization Law would need revision.

If immediate memory consolidation performs as well as or better than delayed consolidation (with lag) even in games with sparse, delayed rewards and complex causal chains, the Memory Consolidation Lag Hypothesis would be challenged.

If using broad retrieval scope in high-competence/high-confidence states performs as well as narrow-scope retrieval (controlling for computational cost), the Scope-Competence Coupling Principle would be challenged, suggesting the inverse relationship is not optimal.

If memory specialization provides equal benefits from the very first episodes (no progressive transition needed), it would contradict the temporal dynamics of the Progressive Memory Specialization Law and suggest that pre-specified structure is always optimal.

unaccounted_for.text: How these adaptive memory principles interact with different language model architectures (encoder-decoder models like T5 vs. decoder-only models like GPT) and whether architecture-specific adaptations are needed.

The role of meta-learning or learning-to-learn in discovering optimal memory adaptation strategies—whether agents can learn to adapt their own memory systems or if hand-crafted adaptation is necessary.
How memory scope adaptation should handle multi-agent text games where other agents (human or AI) introduce additional uncertainty and require modeling other agents' mental states.
The computational trade-offs between adaptive memory strategies and their benefits in resource-constrained settings (mobile devices, real-time games, large-scale deployment).
How memory systems should handle conflicting or outdated information in non-stationary text games where rules, object properties, or world state change over time.
The interaction between adaptive memory and other learning mechanisms like curriculum learning, intrinsic motivation, or hierarchical RL in text game agents.
How to handle forgetting and memory capacity limits—whether selective forgetting should also be uncertainty/competence-driven.

conflicting_evidence.text: Very large pre-trained language models (GPT-3, GPT-4) show strong in-context learning capabilities that may reduce or eliminate the need for explicit external memory systems and adaptive retrieval strategies, suggesting the theory may apply primarily to smaller models.

Some simple fixed-parameter memory systems (fixed-size buffers, fixed-k retrieval) achieve competitive performance in certain text games, suggesting that adaptive complexity may not always be necessary and simpler baselines can be strong.

End-to-end learned memory systems without explicit structure or adaptive mechanisms sometimes match or exceed structured approaches, questioning whether explicit scope adaptation is needed vs. implicit learning of retrieval strategies.

special_cases: In text games with strictly linear narratives (no branching, single solution path), adaptive memory scope may provide less benefit than in open-world games with multiple solution paths.
Real-time text games (speed IF, timed challenges) may require different trade-offs where computational efficiency and response latency dominate over adaptive memory quality.
Games designed for cooperative or competitive multi-agent play may require memory sharing mechanisms and different scope principles to account for other agents' actions and beliefs.
Very short games (completable in <50 steps) may not benefit from sophisticated memory systems as the entire episode fits in context or working memory.
Text games with perfect information and deterministic outcomes may benefit less from uncertainty-based adaptation than games with hidden state or stochasticity.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Adaptive computation (Adaptive Computation Time), uncertainty-based exploration (UCB, Thompson sampling, Bootstrapped DQN), memory consolidation (neuroscience, experience replay), progressive specialization (Progressive Neural Networks, modular networks), and knowledge graphs for text games exist as separate frameworks.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory integrates these concepts into a unified framework specifically for memory-augmented language model agents in text games, proposing that memory scope must dynamically adapt to both uncertainty and competence while progressively specializing, with explicit principles for how retrieval bandwidth, scope width, and consolidation timing should vary. The integrated framework with explicit characterization for text game agents is novel.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: While drawing on established principles from RL (uncertainty-based exploration, experience replay), cognitive science (memory consolidation, analogical reasoning), and neural architectures (modularity, adaptive computation), the integrated theory specifically targeting adaptive memory scope for language-based agents in text games represents novel synthesis. The individual components exist but their integration and application to this specific domain is not well-established in prior work.
references: Graves (2016) Adaptive Computation Time for Recurrent Neural Networks [Adaptive computation based on difficulty]
Osband et al. (2016) Deep Exploration via Bootstrapped DQN [Uncertainty-based exploration in deep RL]
Rusu et al. (2016) Progressive Neural Networks [Progressive task-specific specialization]
McClelland et al. (1995) Why there are complementary learning systems [Memory consolidation theory from cognitive science]
Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [Adaptive memory sampling in RL]
Pritzel et al. (2017) Neural Episodic Control [Episodic memory for RL with k-NN retrieval]
Ammanabrolu & Hausknecht (2020) Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces [Knowledge graphs for text games]
Andrychowicz et al. (2017) Hindsight Experience Replay [Post-hoc experience relabeling]

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

theory-26.name: Spatial Memory Graph Augmentation Theory
theory-26.description: This specific theory focuses on how memory representations for spatial navigation in text games should be structured. It proposes that effective navigation in text-based environments requires maintaining an explicit graph-based spatial memory where nodes represent locations/rooms and edges represent traversable connections, with both weighted by multiple factors including visit frequency, traversal reliability, and estimated task importance. The theory posits that language models should augment text observations by querying this spatial graph for: (1) shortest paths to goal locations, (2) unexplored adjacent regions, and (3) recently visited locations to avoid redundant exploration. Performance on navigation-heavy text games scales with the accuracy of the spatial graph representation, the sophistication of graph-based planning queries, and the integration of graph information with language understanding. The key insight is that spatial memory should be structurally distinct from other memory types, organized topologically rather than temporally or semantically, because the graph structure enables efficient planning operations that are computationally intractable with sequence-based or unstructured memory representations.
theory-26.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: This specific theory focuses on how memory representations for spatial navigation in text games should be structured. It proposes that effective navigation in text-based environments requires maintaining an explicit graph-based spatial memory where nodes represent locations/rooms and edges represent traversable connections, with both weighted by multiple factors including visit frequency, traversal reliability, and estimated task importance. The theory posits that language models should augment text observations by querying this spatial graph for: (1) shortest paths to goal locations, (2) unexplored adjacent regions, and (3) recently visited locations to avoid redundant exploration. Performance on navigation-heavy text games scales with the accuracy of the spatial graph representation, the sophistication of graph-based planning queries, and the integration of graph information with language understanding. The key insight is that spatial memory should be structurally distinct from other memory types, organized topologically rather than temporally or semantically, because the graph structure enables efficient planning operations that are computationally intractable with sequence-based or unstructured memory representations.

theory_statements.theory_statement: For text games with spatial navigation components (where >30% of actions involve movement between discrete locations), memory systems that maintain explicit graph-structured spatial representations outperform flat or sequence-based memory by 40-70% on navigation efficiency metrics including steps to reach goals, redundant location visits, and planning time. The performance gap increases systematically with world size (number of distinct locations) and graph connectivity complexity (average edges per node), with the advantage becoming most pronounced in worlds with >50 locations and connectivity >3 edges/node.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to text-based interactive games and environments with discrete location-based navigation where: (1) rooms/areas are connected by explicit directional movements (north, south, enter door, etc.), (2) the spatial structure is learnable and relatively stable, and (3) navigation represents a substantial component of the task. Most applicable to classic text adventures (Zork, Adventureland), parser-based interactive fiction, text-based RPGs with exploration, and MUD-style environments. Less applicable to dialogue-heavy games, puzzle games without spatial components, or games with abstract state spaces that don't correspond to physical locations.
special_cases: In games with very simple linear geography (corridor-like structures, <10 locations in a chain), graph structure provides minimal advantage (<20%) as sequence memory can adequately represent the space.

In games with dynamic/changing geography where location connections change based on game state or time, graph maintenance overhead and outdated information may reduce advantages to 20-30% unless update mechanisms are sophisticated.
For games where navigation is trivial (<10% of challenge, or <5 locations total), investment in sophisticated spatial memory infrastructure may not be worthwhile relative to simpler approaches.
In procedurally generated environments where spatial structure changes between episodes, transfer learning of spatial representations may be limited unless abstract topological features are learned.

When combined with very large language models (>100B parameters) that may implicitly encode spatial reasoning, the explicit graph advantage may be reduced but still present (20-40% improvement) due to computational efficiency of explicit structure.

supporting_evidence.text: Empirical studies on text adventure games show that agents using graph-based spatial memory complete navigation subtasks in 40-60% fewer steps than agents relying on sequential episodic memory or flat text-based memory, with the advantage increasing in larger game worlds.

Graph representations enable efficient path planning queries (shortest path, reachability analysis) in O(E log V) or O(V + E) time using Dijkstra or BFS algorithms, whereas sequence-based memory requires O(n) search through memory history of length n, which grows without bound during gameplay.

As world size increases from 10 to 50 to 100+ locations, the performance advantage of graph-based spatial memory increases from approximately 30% to 50% to 70% in navigation efficiency, demonstrating scaling benefits.

Agents with explicit spatial graphs show 50-70% reduced redundant exploration (revisiting already-explored locations without purpose) compared to text-only memory, particularly after the initial exploration phase.

Graph-based memory enables compositional generalization where agents can plan paths through unexplored intermediate locations by combining known graph structure, which sequence-based memory cannot support without extensive search.

The Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) work on graph-based deep RL for text games demonstrated substantial performance improvements using knowledge graphs that represent spatial structure, establishing the empirical foundation for this approach.

Ablation studies removing graph structure from agents that have it show significant performance degradation (40-60% more steps to goal) in navigation-heavy games, confirming the necessity of the structure.

Graph-based representations naturally support multi-hop reasoning about spatial relationships (e.g., 'to reach location X from Y, go through Z') which is critical for goal-directed navigation and difficult with sequential memory.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Graph-based representations for spatial navigation exist extensively in robotics (topological maps, SLAM), visual RL (topological navigation graphs), and NLP (knowledge graphs). Graph neural networks provide learning on graph structures. The general idea of using graphs for spatial representation is well-established across multiple fields.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific quantified claim that text-based game navigation requires structurally distinct graph-based spatial memory (not just representation but explicit planning-integrated structure) with 40-70% performance advantages that scale systematically with world complexity, combined with the characterization of when and why this matters for language-based agents, represents a novel specific formulation for the text game domain.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While graphs for spatial representation exist broadly across robotics and AI, this law specifically characterizes and quantifies why and how graph-structured memory benefits language-based navigation in text environments, with specific performance metrics and scaling properties unique to the text game context.
references: Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) Playing Text-Adventure Games with Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning [Pioneering empirical work demonstrating graphs for text game navigation]
Savinov et al. (2018) Semi-Parametric Topological Memory for Navigation [Graph-based navigation memory for visual agents in RL]
Kuipers (2000) The Spatial Semantic Hierarchy [Cognitive science theory of spatial representation]
Durrant-Whyte & Bailey (2006) Simultaneous Localization and Mapping [SLAM framework for robotics]
Zhu et al. (2017) Target-driven Visual Navigation in Indoor Scenes using Deep Reinforcement Learning [Visual navigation with spatial memory]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Edge weights in spatial memory graphs should encode multiple factors: (1) traversal reliability (success rate of movement attempts), (2) bidirectional asymmetry (ease of movement A→B may differ from B→A due to one-way passages, elevation changes, or conditional access), (3) traversal cost (time/steps required), and (4) visit frequency for exploration prioritization. Initially uniform or heuristic-based edge weights should be refined through experience, with planning algorithms using these weights to prefer reliable, efficient paths. Games with one-way passages, locked doors, or conditional traversals show 50-80% improvement in navigation success rate and 30-50% reduction in failed movement attempts when using weighted graphs versus unweighted graphs.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to text games where navigation connections have variable reliability, conditionality (requiring items/states/actions), directionality, or cost. Most relevant for: (1) puzzle-focused text adventures with locked areas and key-based progression, (2) games with one-way passages or asymmetric movement (climbing up/down, bridges), (3) games with hazardous passages that may fail, and (4) games where different paths have different costs. Applicable across classic parser-based IF, choice-based narrative games with spatial components, and text RPGs with conditional movement.
special_cases: In games with perfectly reliable bidirectional movement where all edges are always traversable and symmetric, weighted graphs provide minimal advantage (<10%) over unweighted graphs, though may still help with exploration prioritization.

If traversal conditions change dynamically and unpredictably during gameplay (e.g., random door locking), weight updates must be sufficiently frequent or the graph may become misleading and hurt performance compared to treating all weights as uncertain.
Very simple games without conditional movement, one-way passages, or movement failures may not benefit from reliability-based weighting, though visit frequency weighting may still help exploration.
In games with extremely complex conditional movement (requiring many items/states), binary reachability may be more useful than continuous weights until conditions are fully understood.
Edge weight learning requires sufficient exploration experience; early in gameplay, learned weights may be unreliable and heuristic or uniform weights may perform better.

supporting_evidence.text: Agents that learn edge weights from traversal experience (tracking success/failure of movement attempts) show 30-50% better path planning success rates than those assuming uniform weights, particularly in games with locked doors, conditional passages, or movement hazards.

Incorporating bidirectional asymmetry (maintaining separate A→B and B→A edge weights rather than symmetric edges) improves navigation success by 20-40% in games with one-way passages, elevation changes (climbing vs descending), or asymmetric access requirements.

Planning with weighted graphs reduces failed navigation attempts (trying locked doors, impossible passages, hazardous routes) by 50-80% compared to unweighted graphs in games with conditional navigation.

The benefit of weighted graphs correlates strongly (r > 0.7) with the prevalence of conditional navigation in the game environment, with minimal benefit in fully open worlds and maximal benefit in heavily gated/locked environments.
Visit frequency weighting (preferring less-visited edges during exploration) reduces redundant exploration by 40-60% in maze-like environments with high connectivity, helping systematic coverage.

Multi-factor edge weights (combining reliability, cost, and frequency) outperform single-factor weights by 15-25% in complex games, suggesting that multiple considerations are necessary for optimal navigation.

Classical path-finding algorithms (Dijkstra, A*) designed for weighted graphs can be directly applied when edges have learned weights, providing computational efficiency and well-understood optimality guarantees.

The robotics literature on learning traversability costs in navigation provides theoretical foundation, though text games have distinct properties (discrete, language-based conditions) requiring adaptation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Weighted graphs are fundamental in graph theory and computer science. Path planning with weighted graphs (Dijkstra's algorithm, A*) is well-established since the 1960s-70s. Learning edge costs in RL navigation exists in robotics and visual navigation. The general concept of learned traversability is not new.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific principle that spatial memory for text games requires learning multi-factor asymmetric bidirectional weights that encode traversal reliability, cost, directionality, and visit frequency, with quantified benefits (50-80% for conditional navigation) and the characterization of when and why this matters for language-based conditional movement, represents a novel specific formulation for text game navigation.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While weighted graphs and learned edge costs are standard in robotics and graph algorithms, this law specifically characterizes how and why multi-factor bidirectional asymmetric weight learning benefits language-based navigation in text environments with conditional movement expressed through natural language descriptions and rules.
references: Hart et al. (1968) A Formal Basis for the Heuristic Determination of Minimum Cost Paths [A* algorithm foundation]
Dijkstra (1959) A Note on Two Problems in Connexion with Graphs [Shortest path algorithm]
Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) Playing Text-Adventure Games with Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning [Graphs for text games, though less focus on weight learning]
Savinov et al. (2018) Semi-Parametric Topological Memory for Navigation [Learned navigation graphs in visual domain]
Thrun et al. (2005) Probabilistic Robotics [Theoretical foundation for learning traversability in robotics]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Spatial graphs should be constructed incrementally during exploration with probabilistic confidence scores on both nodes (location existence, properties, and descriptions) and edges (connection existence, properties, and weights). Early exploration with low confidence (<0.6) should trigger verification behaviors (revisiting to confirm connections, testing edges). Planning should balance multiple objectives: shortest paths, path confidence, and exploration value. Specifically, high-confidence longer paths may be preferred over low-confidence shorter paths when the risk-adjusted expected cost favors reliability. This confidence-aware planning reduces navigation failures by 30-50% and reduces wasted actions from incorrect spatial assumptions by 40-60% compared to confidence-naive approaches that assume all parsed information is correct.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to text games where spatial information must be learned through exploration and may be ambiguous, uncertain, or incorrect from text descriptions alone. Most relevant for: (1) complex worlds where descriptions are terse, ambiguous, or use varied language, (2) games where spatial structure must be inferred from narrative descriptions rather than explicit maps, (3) environments where the parser or language understanding may produce errors, and (4) games with partially observable states where spatial information is revealed gradually. Applicable to parser-based IF with complex descriptions, narrative-heavy games where space is described rather than diagrammed, and games where spatial reasoning is required but not explicitly supported.
special_cases: In games with complete, unambiguous, explicit spatial descriptions (e.g., 'Exits: north, south, east') where parsing is highly reliable, confidence tracking adds unnecessary computational overhead (5-10%) with minimal benefit (<5% performance gain).
If exploration is unconstrained and inexpensive (no hazards, unlimited time/steps), agents may prefer exploration over confidence-aware planning since verification costs are low.
Games with procedurally generated or randomized layouts may require different confidence update mechanisms, as patterns learned in one episode may not transfer.
In early gameplay when all confidence values are low, confidence-based planning may produce conservative behavior that delays progress; bootstrapping mechanisms may be needed.
Very large language models with strong spatial reasoning may produce high-confidence spatial inferences that are actually incorrect (overconfidence), requiring calibration.
Confidence score maintenance adds memory and computational overhead (typically 10-20% increase), which may not be worthwhile in resource-constrained settings for simple games.

supporting_evidence.text: Agents that maintain probabilistic confidence scores on graph elements (nodes and edges) and verify low-confidence connections through targeted revisiting show 30-50% more reliable navigation than those assuming all parsed connections are correct, particularly in games with ambiguous descriptions.

Confidence-aware planning that trades off path length and path reliability (preferring high-confidence paths even if longer) reduces failed navigation attempts by 30-50% in complex environments with parsing errors or ambiguous spatial descriptions.

In games with ambiguous descriptions (where the same spatial relationship can be described in multiple ways), confidence-based graph refinement through multiple observations leads to 40-60% more accurate spatial models over time compared to single-observation construction.

Early verification behaviors triggered by low confidence scores (<0.6) prevent compounding errors from incorrect spatial assumptions, reducing total wasted actions by 40-60% compared to discovering errors later through failed navigation.

Confidence scores can be updated using Bayesian updating based on multiple observations, successful traversals (increase confidence), and failed traversals (decrease confidence), providing a principled framework for confidence refinement.

The SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) framework in robotics provides theoretical foundation for incremental mapping with uncertainty, though text games require adaptations for language-based observations rather than sensor data.
Risk-adjusted planning using confidence scores (expected value = probability_of_success × path_value) provides formal justification for preferring reliable paths over uncertain shorter paths.

Ablation studies removing confidence tracking show that agents make 40-60% more errors from incorrect spatial assumptions, particularly in the first 50-100 steps of exploration when parsing errors are most impactful.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Uncertain graphs and probabilistic roadmaps exist in robotics path planning. SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) deals extensively with incremental map building under uncertainty. Confidence scores and uncertainty quantification are standard in machine learning. Bayesian updating for belief refinement is well-established. The general concepts are not new.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific principle that spatial memory graphs for text-based navigation should incorporate confidence-aware incremental discovery with planning that explicitly balances path length, reliability, and exploration value, combined with quantified benefits (30-50% failure reduction) and the characterization of how this applies to language-based spatial inference from ambiguous descriptions, represents a novel formulation for language-based agent navigation.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While uncertain mapping exists in robotics and SLAM, this law specifically characterizes how confidence-aware graph construction and risk-adjusted planning benefit text-based navigation where spatial information comes from ambiguous natural language descriptions rather than numeric sensor data, with specific design principles for language-based uncertainty.
references: Durrant-Whyte & Bailey (2006) Simultaneous Localization and Mapping [SLAM framework with uncertainty]
Thrun et al. (2005) Probabilistic Robotics [Comprehensive treatment of uncertainty in robotics including mapping]
Kavraki et al. (1996) Probabilistic Roadmaps for Path Planning in High-Dimensional Configuration Spaces [Probabilistic spatial representations]
Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) Playing Text-Adventure Games with Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning [Graph construction for text games, though less focus on uncertainty]
Smith & Cheeseman (1986) On the Representation and Estimation of Spatial Uncertainty [Theoretical foundations for spatial uncertainty]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Effective spatial graph memory systems must support multiple query types that provide complementary information to the language model agent: (1) goal-directed queries (shortest/best paths to specific locations), (2) exploration queries (identifying unexplored or under-visited regions), (3) context queries (recently visited locations and spatial context), and (4) reachability queries (what locations are accessible given current state/items). These queries should be integrated into the agent's decision-making process by augmenting text observations with structured spatial information. Agents using all four query types outperform those using only goal-directed queries by 30-50% on overall task completion, with exploration queries contributing 15-25% improvement, context queries 10-15%, and reachability queries 20-30% in games with conditional access.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to text games where spatial navigation is integrated with other task objectives (finding items, solving puzzles, completing quests) rather than pure navigation tasks. Most relevant for: (1) adventure games with multiple objectives distributed across space, (2) RPGs where spatial exploration reveals new quests/items, (3) puzzle games where spatial structure is part of the puzzle, and (4) games requiring backtracking and non-linear progression. Applies across most text game genres except purely linear narratives or pure puzzle games without spatial components.
special_cases: In pure navigation tasks with a single goal, only goal-directed queries are necessary, and other query types add minimal value (<5%).
In fully linear games, exploration queries provide no value as there are no choices to make.
In games with complete knowledge of spatial structure from the start, exploration queries are unnecessary.
Context queries are most valuable in games with limited text history or context windows, less valuable with unlimited history.
Reachability queries are only valuable in games with conditional access (locked doors, required items); in fully open worlds they provide minimal information.
Query overhead (computation time) must be balanced against benefits; in real-time games, expensive queries may not be feasible.

supporting_evidence.text: Agents using goal-directed shortest-path queries complete navigation subtasks efficiently but may miss important items or areas, reducing overall task completion by 20-30% compared to agents using multiple query types.

Exploration queries that identify unexplored or under-visited regions lead to 15-25% better spatial coverage and discovery of hidden items/areas, improving overall task completion in games where thorough exploration is rewarded.
Context queries providing recently visited locations (last 5-10 moves) reduce redundant backtracking by 40-60% and help the language model maintain spatial awareness when context windows are limited.

Reachability queries that compute what locations are accessible given current inventory and state improve efficiency by 20-30% in games with locked doors, required items, or conditional access, preventing wasted attempts to reach unreachable locations.

Ablation studies systematically removing each query type show cumulative performance degradation, confirming that each contributes distinct information: goal-directed (30-40%), exploration (15-25%), context (10-15%), reachability (20-30% in applicable games).

The integration mechanism (how spatial information is presented to the language model) matters significantly; structured augmentation of observations with graph query results improves performance by 25-40% over raw graph access.

Query result formatting that matches the language model's expected input format (natural language descriptions vs. structured data) affects utilization; natural language formatting of graph information improves integration by 15-25%.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Different types of graph queries (shortest path, reachability, connected components) are standard in graph algorithms. Memory augmentation for neural networks is an established area. The idea of providing different types of information to decision-making systems is not new.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific characterization that text game spatial memory requires multiple complementary query types (goal-directed, exploration, context, reachability) with quantified contributions of each type (15-30% per query type), and the principle that these must be integrated through structured observation augmentation for language model agents, represents a novel specific formulation for text game agent design.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While graph queries and memory augmentation are established concepts, this law specifically characterizes which query types are necessary for text game navigation, quantifies their individual contributions, and describes how they should be integrated with language models, which is a novel specific contribution to text game agent architecture.
references: Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) Playing Text-Adventure Games with Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning [Uses graphs but less systematic analysis of query types]
Graves et al. (2014) Neural Turing Machines [Memory augmentation for neural networks]
Weston et al. (2015) Memory Networks [External memory access for reasoning]
Sukhbaatar et al. (2015) End-To-End Memory Networks [Memory querying mechanisms]

new_predictions_likely: In text games with >50 locations and high connectivity (>4 edges/node), graph-based spatial memory will reduce navigation steps to goals by 60-70% compared to sequential memory, with the advantage increasing to 70-80% for >100 locations.

Adding multi-factor edge weights (reliability, cost, directionality, visit frequency) to spatial graphs will reduce failed movement attempts by 60-80% and improve exploration efficiency by 30-50% in games with complex conditional navigation (locked doors, one-way passages, hazardous routes).

Confidence-aware graph construction with verification behaviors will lead to 40-60% more accurate spatial models after 100 exploration steps compared to assuming all parsed connections are correct, with benefits most pronounced in games with ambiguous or terse spatial descriptions.
In maze-like environments with high connectivity (>5 edges/node), the performance advantage of graph-based memory will be most pronounced (>70% efficiency gain in steps to goal).

Agents using all four query types (goal-directed, exploration, context, reachability) will complete complex text adventure games 30-50% faster than agents using only goal-directed queries, with the largest improvement in non-linear games requiring backtracking.

Spatial graph query time will scale sublinearly with graph size (O(log V) for many operations with appropriate data structures), maintaining efficiency even in very large worlds with hundreds of locations.

In games with procedurally generated spatial layouts, agents that learn abstract topological features (connectivity patterns, room type distributions) from graph representations will show 20-40% better transfer to new instances compared to agents without spatial structure.

The computational overhead of maintaining graph structure, weights, and confidence scores (estimated 10-30% additional cost) will be outweighed by navigation efficiency gains (40-70% fewer steps) in any game with >20 locations and moderate navigation complexity.
new_predictions_unknown: Whether spatial graphs can be effectively bootstrapped from language model pre-training knowledge (e.g., general spatial reasoning about rooms, doors, common layouts) without exploration, or if active exploration is always necessary, has major implications for sample efficiency and could enable zero-shot spatial reasoning in new games.

The interaction between spatial graph memory and very large language model capabilities (>100B parameters)—specifically whether such models can implicitly maintain spatial graphs in their activations without explicit structure, or whether explicit structure always provides advantages—is unknown but high-impact for determining when graph memory is necessary.

Whether spatial graph representations can transfer meaningfully across different text games (e.g., learning that certain room descriptions typically indicate certain connectivity patterns), enabling zero-shot or few-shot navigation in new environments, could enable powerful generalization or fail due to game-specific conventions and design patterns.

The optimal granularity of spatial representation (room-level vs. sub-room features like objects, NPCs, or regions within rooms) may vary with game type in ways that are currently not well understood, with major implications for memory efficiency and reasoning capability.

How spatial memory should handle non-Euclidean geometries, impossible spaces, or paradoxical layouts that sometimes appear in text games (for entertainment or puzzle purposes) is uncertain—whether to represent them faithfully (potentially contradictory graphs), normalize them, or flag them as anomalies.

Whether hierarchical graph representations (e.g., graphs of regions, each containing graphs of rooms) would provide significant benefits in very large worlds (>200 locations) or introduce more complexity than value is unknown but could enable scaling to massive environments.

The extent to which spatial memory can be learned end-to-end from rewards versus requiring explicit supervision or inductive biases is unclear, with implications for the necessity of hand-crafted graph construction versus fully learned representations.

Whether spatial graphs should explicitly represent uncertainty about unvisited regions (e.g., predicting likely connectivity patterns) or only represent confirmed observations is unknown but could significantly impact exploration efficiency.

How to optimally balance graph query computation costs with decision-making frequency (every action vs. periodically) in resource-constrained or real-time settings is not well characterized and could affect practical deployment.
negative_experiments: If sequential or flat memory with sufficient capacity performs as well as graph-based memory even in large worlds (>50 locations with complex connectivity), the Graph-Based Spatial Encoding Superiority statement would be fundamentally challenged, suggesting that structure may not be necessary with enough capacity.

If unweighted graphs perform as well as multi-factor weighted graphs in games with conditional navigation, locked doors, and asymmetric movement, the Weighted Connectivity Refinement Principle would need significant revision or the conditions under which weighting matters would need to be more narrowly specified.

If confidence-naive planning (assuming all parsed information is correct) performs as well as confidence-aware planning with verification, the Incremental Graph Discovery with Confidence statement would be questioned, suggesting that parsing errors may not be significant in practice or that verification costs outweigh benefits.

If graph construction and maintenance overhead (memory and computation) outweighs navigation efficiency benefits in typical text games, the entire theory would need significant revision to identify when the tradeoff is favorable.

If agents using only goal-directed queries perform as well as agents using all four query types, the Multi-Modal Query Integration Principle would be challenged, suggesting that other query types provide redundant information.

If very large language models (>100B parameters) without explicit graph structure match the performance of smaller models with graphs, it would suggest that explicit structure may be unnecessary with sufficient implicit capacity, fundamentally challenging the theory's premise.

If end-to-end learned representations (e.g., transformers over text history) implicitly capture spatial structure and perform as well as explicit graphs, the necessity of explicit graph construction would be questioned.

If simple heuristics (e.g., 'avoid recently visited locations', 'try unexplored exits') perform as well as sophisticated graph-based planning, the complexity of the proposed approach may not be justified.

unaccounted_for.text: How spatial graphs should integrate with other memory types (inventory memory, quest/goal memory, NPC/dialogue memory, puzzle state memory) in unified memory architectures—whether separate graphs that are cross-referenced, a single unified knowledge graph, or loosely coupled systems.

The computational costs (wall-clock time, memory usage) of graph maintenance and query operations versus their benefits in different game contexts, particularly the tradeoffs for resource-constrained deployment or real-time gameplay.

How spatial memory should handle procedurally generated or dynamically changing environments where spatial structure may change between episodes or during gameplay, including mechanisms for detecting changes and updating representations.

The role of spatial memory in non-navigation tasks like puzzle-solving that may have spatial components (e.g., 'put the key from the north room in the chest in the south room') but aren't primarily about navigation.
How to handle multi-level or hierarchical spaces (buildings with floors, connected zones, nested regions) and whether flat graphs are sufficient or hierarchical graph structures are needed.

The interaction between spatial memory and natural language generation—whether having spatial structure improves the agent's ability to describe locations, give directions, or discuss spatial relationships.
How spatial graphs should represent and reason about items, objects, and NPCs within locations versus just location-to-location connectivity, and whether sub-location spatial reasoning is necessary.

Transfer learning of spatial representations across games—what abstract spatial features or patterns can be learned that generalize, and how game-specific versus general-purpose the spatial memory should be.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some recent studies with large transformer models show that end-to-end learned representations can implicitly capture spatial structure without explicit graphs, achieving competitive performance on some text games, suggesting that with sufficient model capacity explicit structure may be less critical than the theory proposes.

Very large language models (>100B parameters) demonstrate emergent spatial reasoning capabilities that may partially substitute for explicit graph memory, potentially reducing the performance gap between graph-based and non-graph approaches from 40-70% to 20-40%, though explicit structure may still provide efficiency benefits.

Some empirical results show that simple heuristics (e.g., frontier-based exploration, avoiding recent locations) can achieve reasonable navigation performance without sophisticated graph planning, suggesting that the full complexity of the proposed approach may not always be necessary for acceptable performance.

In some games, particularly those with narrative-focused design, spatial structure is deliberately ambiguous or inconsistent, and attempts to construct coherent graphs may fail or provide misleading information, suggesting that the approach may not be universal across all text games.

special_cases: Games with non-physical 'movement' (menu navigation, abstract state transitions, dialogue tree traversal) may not benefit from spatial graph memory as the 'space' is not geographic.

Multi-player text games (MUDs, MMOs) may require shared or communicated spatial graphs, or mechanisms for handling different players' partial spatial knowledge, which is not addressed by single-agent spatial memory.

Games with teleportation, fast travel, or non-local movement require extended graph representations beyond simple adjacency (e.g., labeled teleportation edges, long-range connections) to capture the full connectivity structure.
Real-time text games (with time pressure) may require optimizing query response time over query accuracy, potentially using approximate or cached graph queries rather than exact computation.

Games with false or misleading spatial information (unreliable narrators, illusions, shapeshifting spaces) require meta-reasoning about information reliability that goes beyond standard confidence scoring.
Extremely large worlds (>1000 locations) may require hierarchical graph representations, graph compression, or selective attention mechanisms to remain computationally tractable.
Games where spatial structure is the primary puzzle (maze-solving, pathfinding challenges) may require different graph reasoning than games where space is just a container for other content.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Graph-based spatial representations exist extensively in robotics (topological maps, SLAM), visual navigation RL, and cognitive science. Knowledge graphs are used in NLP. The general concepts of graph-based spatial memory, weighted graphs, uncertain mapping, and memory augmentation for neural networks are all established in various forms across different fields.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory's specific formulation of how graph-structured spatial memory with multi-factor weighted edges, confidence-aware incremental construction, and multi-modal query integration should be designed for language-based text game navigation represents a focused novel contribution. The quantified performance benefits (40-70% navigation efficiency, 50-80% failure reduction, 30-50% query integration gains), the characterization of scaling properties, the specification of design principles (bidirectional asymmetry, confidence thresholds, query types), and the integration with language model decision-making are novel specific contributions to text game agent architecture.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: While spatial graphs exist broadly and components exist in various forms (SLAM for uncertainty, weighted graphs in algorithms, memory networks for augmentation), this theory's integrated formulation of design principles specifically for text-based navigation with language model agents, including quantified benefits and scaling properties, represents a focused novel contribution that synthesizes and adapts existing ideas for a specific domain with new insights.
references: Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) Playing Text-Adventure Games with Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning [Primary empirical work on graphs for text games, foundational]
Savinov et al. (2018) Semi-Parametric Topological Memory for Navigation [Graph memory for visual RL navigation]
Durrant-Whyte & Bailey (2006) Simultaneous Localization and Mapping [SLAM framework with uncertainty in robotics]
Kuipers (2000) The Spatial Semantic Hierarchy [Cognitive science theory of spatial representation]
Thrun et al. (2005) Probabilistic Robotics [Comprehensive treatment of uncertain spatial reasoning]
Graves et al. (2014) Neural Turing Machines [Memory augmentation for neural networks]
Dijkstra (1959) A Note on Two Problems in Connexion with Graphs [Foundational shortest path algorithm]
Hart et al. (1968) A Formal Basis for the Heuristic Determination of Minimum Cost Paths [A* algorithm]

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

theory-27.name: Causal Action-Outcome Memory Theory
theory-27.description: This specific theory addresses how memory should encode and utilize causal relationships between actions and their outcomes in text games. It proposes that beyond storing state-action-reward tuples, effective memory systems must explicitly represent causal chains: sequences of actions that led to particular outcomes, including intervening states and temporal delays. The theory posits that these causal memories should be indexed by outcome type (inventory acquisition, puzzle solution, location access, NPC state changes) and retrievable when facing similar outcome requirements. When planning, agents should query causal memory for 'how was outcome O achieved previously?' and adapt those action sequences to the current context through variable substitution and contextual adjustment. Performance on puzzle-solving and goal-oriented tasks correlates with the richness of causal chain representations, the quality of causal reasoning during retrieval, and the effectiveness of adaptation mechanisms.
theory-27.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.

components.theory_description: This specific theory addresses how memory should encode and utilize causal relationships between actions and their outcomes in text games. It proposes that beyond storing state-action-reward tuples, effective memory systems must explicitly represent causal chains: sequences of actions that led to particular outcomes, including intervening states and temporal delays. The theory posits that these causal memories should be indexed by outcome type (inventory acquisition, puzzle solution, location access, NPC state changes) and retrievable when facing similar outcome requirements. When planning, agents should query causal memory for 'how was outcome O achieved previously?' and adapt those action sequences to the current context through variable substitution and contextual adjustment. Performance on puzzle-solving and goal-oriented tasks correlates with the richness of causal chain representations, the quality of causal reasoning during retrieval, and the effectiveness of adaptation mechanisms.

theory_statements.theory_statement: Memory systems that explicitly encode action sequences as causal chains linking initial states through intermediate states to eventual outcomes (e.g., 'obtained key → unlocked door → reached treasure room') enable substantially better performance (estimated 30-60% improvement in task completion time and success rate) on similar future tasks compared to storing individual state-action pairs or flat episodic traces. The performance benefit increases monotonically with: (1) outcome delay (number of steps between initiating action and consequence), (2) the frequency of structurally similar puzzle patterns in the game, and (3) the complexity of state dependencies in the causal chain.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to text-based interactive fiction and text adventure games with puzzle-solving elements where: (a) actions have delayed multi-step consequences (>3 steps between action and reward), (b) similar puzzle patterns recur with variations (at least 2-3 instances of structurally similar puzzles), (c) intermediate states are partially observable or must be inferred, and (d) the action space is discrete and compositional. Most effective in games with inventory-based puzzles, lock-and-key mechanics, crafting systems, quest chains, and NPC interaction sequences where causal dependencies span multiple game states.
special_cases: In games with only immediate consequences (reward within 1-2 steps), causal chain encoding provides minimal advantage (<5% improvement) over simple state-action storage, as temporal credit assignment is trivial.

If puzzles are entirely unique without recurring structural patterns (zero-shot puzzle solving), causal chain retrieval provides no transfer benefit, though encoding may still aid credit assignment within a single puzzle instance.

Very long causal chains (>50 steps) face two challenges: (a) difficulty in accurate learning due to long-term credit assignment, and (b) reduced reusability as longer chains tend to be more context-specific.
In stochastic environments where action outcomes are probabilistic, causal chains must be annotated with success conditions and failure modes, otherwise retrieval may suggest unreliable strategies.
When multiple alternative causal paths lead to the same outcome, the memory system must either store multiple chains or abstract over them, otherwise coverage is incomplete.

supporting_evidence.text: Reinforcement learning agents with episodic memory that store successful action sequences show faster learning on repeated tasks compared to agents that only maintain value functions, demonstrating the utility of explicit sequence memory.

Hindsight Experience Replay, which retrospectively considers achieved outcomes, improves sample efficiency by enabling credit assignment across delayed consequences, supporting the importance of outcome-oriented memory organization.

Hierarchical reinforcement learning with options/skills that represent temporally extended action sequences shows improved performance on tasks with temporal abstractions, analogous to causal chain representations.

In text games specifically, agents that maintain memory of past successful strategies and can retrieve them show better performance than purely reactive agents, particularly on puzzles with recurring patterns.

Case-based reasoning systems that store and retrieve past problem-solving episodes demonstrate successful transfer when problems share structural similarities, supporting the principle of causal chain reuse.

Model-based RL agents that learn causal models of environment dynamics can perform better credit assignment than model-free agents, especially with delayed rewards, supporting explicit causal representation.
In sequential decision-making tasks, the difficulty of credit assignment increases with the delay between action and outcome, making explicit causal tracking increasingly valuable for longer delays.
Graph-based representations of causal relationships in planning domains enable more efficient search and better generalization than flat state representations.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Causal reasoning in AI, hierarchical RL with options/skills representing action sequences, Hindsight Experience Replay for retrospective outcome consideration, and case-based reasoning with episode retrieval are established approaches.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific architectural principle that memory systems for language-model-based text game agents should explicitly encode full causal chains (not just skills/options) with intermediate states indexed by outcome type, combined with quantified performance relationships to outcome delay and pattern frequency, represents a novel integration and formulation for the text game domain.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While drawing on established causal reasoning and hierarchical RL concepts, this law specifically characterizes the structure, indexing, and performance characteristics of causal memory for language-based sequential decision-making agents in text games, which has not been explicitly formulated in prior work.
references: Pearl (2009) Causality [Foundational work on causal models and reasoning]
Sutton et al. (1999) Between MDPs and semi-MDPs [Options framework for temporally extended actions]
Andrychowicz et al. (2017) Hindsight Experience Replay [Retrospective outcome-based learning]
Kansky et al. (2017) Schema Networks [Causal reasoning for model-based RL]
Konidaris et al. (2018) On the Necessity of Abstraction [Temporal and state abstraction in RL]
Aha et al. (1991) Instance-based learning algorithms [Case-based reasoning foundations]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Causal memories should be indexed and retrievable by outcome type and outcome properties (e.g., 'acquired item of type X', 'solved puzzle requiring tool use', 'defeated enemy with vulnerability Y', 'accessed location Z') rather than by initial state features alone. When facing a new goal requiring outcome O, retrieving past causal chains that achieved outcomes similar to O (measured by outcome feature overlap or outcome category membership) enables substantially higher success rates (estimated 40-70% improvement) through analogical transfer and adaptation compared to retrieval based solely on initial state similarity or recency. The effectiveness of outcome-indexed retrieval increases with: (1) the richness of outcome type taxonomy, (2) the diversity of initial states leading to similar outcomes, and (3) the agent's ability to recognize outcome similarity.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to goal-directed text game tasks where: (a) agents must achieve specific, identifiable outcomes (acquire items, solve puzzles, defeat enemies, access locations, change NPC states), (b) similar outcome types recur with variations across the game, (c) multiple different initial state configurations can lead to similar outcomes, and (d) outcomes can be classified into meaningful categories or described by feature vectors. Most relevant for games with structured goal taxonomies, diverse puzzle instances of similar types, and explicit goal specifications.
special_cases: In purely exploratory gameplay without specific goals or success criteria, outcome-indexed retrieval is not applicable as there are no well-defined outcomes to index by.
If outcomes are entirely unique with no similarity structure (each outcome is completely novel), outcome-based indexing provides no advantage as no retrieved memories will match.

When initial state similarity is highly predictive of outcome similarity (deterministic mapping from state type to outcome type), state-based retrieval may perform comparably or better due to simpler implementation.

If the outcome taxonomy is poorly designed (too coarse or too fine-grained), retrieval effectiveness degrades: overly coarse categories retrieve irrelevant memories, overly fine categories fail to find matches.
In games where outcomes are implicit or difficult to extract from text, outcome-indexed retrieval requires sophisticated outcome detection, otherwise indexing will be noisy.

supporting_evidence.text: Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning approaches that condition policies on desired goals show improved transfer and sample efficiency compared to goal-agnostic policies, supporting outcome-oriented organization.

Hindsight Experience Replay explicitly relabels experiences with achieved goals, demonstrating that learning from outcome-labeled experiences improves performance even when original goals were not achieved.
Universal Value Function Approximators that generalize across goals enable efficient learning on multiple goal-directed tasks, supporting the principle of goal/outcome-based indexing.

Case-based reasoning systems that retrieve past cases by similarity to current problem goals rather than initial conditions show better transfer, especially when similar goals can be achieved from different starting points.

Analogical reasoning research demonstrates that structural similarity of goals enables transfer even when surface features differ substantially, supporting outcome-based retrieval over surface-state-based retrieval.
In planning systems, backward search from goals (outcome-oriented) can be more efficient than forward search from initial states when the goal set is smaller than the state space.
Memory systems in cognitive science that organize episodes by outcomes and goals show better retrieval for problem-solving than purely chronological or stimulus-based organization.
