novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Goal-conditioned RL, Hindsight Experience Replay with goal relabeling, Universal Value Function Approximators, case-based reasoning with goal-based retrieval, and analogical reasoning are established frameworks.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific law that causal memory in text game agents should be indexed by outcome type taxonomies (not just goal vectors) for retrieval, with quantified transfer benefits (40-70%) and explicit consideration of outcome feature overlap and category membership, represents a novel specific formulation for language-based interactive agents.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While goal-conditioned approaches exist, this law specifically characterizes how outcome-indexed memory organization with taxonomic structure benefits text game agents, including quantified transfer advantages and design considerations for outcome categorization.
references: Andrychowicz et al. (2017) Hindsight Experience Replay [Goal relabeling and outcome-conditioned learning]
Schaul et al. (2015) Universal Value Function Approximators [Goal-conditioned value functions]
Kolodner (1992) An introduction to case-based reasoning [Case retrieval by goals and outcomes]
Gentner (1983) Structure-Mapping [Analogical transfer based on structural goal similarity]
Eysenck & Keane (2015) Cognitive Psychology [Human memory organization by goals]
Nau et al. (2004) SHOP2: An HTN planning system [Goal-oriented hierarchical planning]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Stored causal chains should be abstracted from specific entities and actions to general patterns at multiple levels of granularity (e.g., 'obtain tool → use tool on obstacle → access new area' rather than 'get rusty key → unlock old door → enter tower', or more abstractly 'obtain enabling resource → apply resource to barrier → access restricted region'). This hierarchical abstraction enables transfer across superficially different but structurally similar puzzles. Performance on novel puzzle variants improves substantially (estimated 50-80% increase in success rate) with appropriately abstracted causal memory compared to concrete instance storage. The optimal abstraction level balances generality (enabling broad transfer) with specificity (preserving causally-relevant details), and may vary by puzzle domain. Multiple abstraction levels should be maintained simultaneously to enable both fine-grained and coarse-grained matching.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to text games with: (a) recurring puzzle structures across different thematic contexts (e.g., various lock-and-key puzzles, trading chains, combat sequences with different entities), (b) compositional action and object spaces where entities can be categorized by properties and affordances, (c) multiple similar-structured but differently-themed challenges, and (d) sufficient puzzle diversity to benefit from generalization (at least 5-10 puzzle instances per structural type). Most relevant for games with systematic design patterns, entity type hierarchies, and procedural puzzle generation.
special_cases: In games where specific entity properties are critical to solutions (e.g., a puzzle requiring specifically 'cold' items, not just any tool), over-abstraction that loses these details will fail. The abstraction must preserve causally-relevant properties.
If puzzles are truly unique without structural similarities (each puzzle uses completely novel mechanics), abstraction provides no transfer benefit as no structural patterns recur.
Very simple games with limited puzzle diversity may not have sufficient instances per pattern to benefit from abstraction, as the overhead of learning abstractions exceeds benefits.
When entity types are ambiguous or not clearly defined in the game world, automatic abstraction may create incorrect generalizations that group causally distinct cases.
In games with very large type hierarchies, determining the appropriate abstraction level becomes complex and may require meta-learning or explicit specification.
Abstractions that are too general may match too many irrelevant cases, while abstractions that are too specific may match too few cases, both degrading retrieval precision.

supporting_evidence.text: Transfer learning research shows that abstract representations that capture task structure enable better generalization to new task instances than instance-specific representations.
Schema learning in cognitive science demonstrates that humans extract abstract patterns from specific experiences and apply them to new situations with similar structure.
Analogical reasoning research shows that structural abstraction (removing surface details while preserving relational structure) is key to successful analogical transfer.
Hierarchical reinforcement learning with skills at multiple levels of abstraction shows better transfer than single-level skill representations.
In planning, abstract problem representations enable more efficient search and better transfer to related problems by factoring out irrelevant details.
Object-oriented and type-based representations in programming and knowledge representation enable reuse through abstraction, analogous to abstracted causal chains.
Relational learning approaches that learn patterns over entity types rather than specific entities show superior generalization in domains with compositional structure.
Multi-level abstraction hierarchies in cognitive architectures like Soar enable flexible matching at different granularities depending on problem requirements.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Abstraction in planning, schema learning, analogical transfer with structural abstraction, hierarchical RL with multi-level skills, and relational learning over types are established concepts.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific principle that causal chains in text game memory should be abstracted at multiple hierarchical levels from entities to structural patterns, with quantified transfer benefits (50-80%) and explicit consideration of abstraction level optimization and multi-granularity matching, represents a novel formulation for language-based agents.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While abstraction is well-studied, this law specifically characterizes how and at what granularities causal chain abstraction should be implemented for text game agents, with quantified advantages on puzzle transfer and architectural prescriptions for multi-level abstraction.
references: Geffner & Bonet (2013) A Concise Introduction to Models and Methods for Automated Planning [Planning with abstraction hierarchies]
Gentner (1983) Structure-Mapping [Analogical abstraction and structural transfer]
Konidaris et al. (2018) On the Necessity of Abstraction [State and temporal abstraction in RL]
Sutton et al. (1999) Between MDPs and semi-MDPs [Temporal abstraction via options]
Laird et al. (1987) Soar: An architecture for general intelligence [Multi-level abstraction in cognitive architecture]
Džeroski et al. (2001) Relational reinforcement learning [Learning abstract patterns over entity types]
Rumelhart & Norman (1978) Accretion, tuning, and restructuring [Schema abstraction in learning]

theory_statements.theory_statement: Causal memory systems must be integrated with other memory types (spatial/location memory, dialogue/NPC state memory, inventory memory, game rule knowledge) through explicit cross-references and consistency constraints. When retrieving causal chains, the system should verify that prerequisite conditions (spatial accessibility, inventory requirements, NPC preconditions) are satisfied in the current context before applying the chain. Performance degrades by 30-50% if causal chains are applied without contextual verification, leading to invalid action sequences. The integration should be bidirectional: causal chains inform what other memory to retrieve, and other memory types constrain which causal chains are applicable.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to complex text games with: (a) multiple interacting game systems (spatial navigation, inventory, NPC dialogue, puzzle-solving), (b) prerequisite conditions for action applicability (location constraints, inventory requirements, NPC state dependencies), (c) rich contextual state that affects action outcomes, and (d) need for multi-step reasoning across different knowledge types. Most relevant for large-scale interactive fiction, RPG-style text games, and complex adventure games.
special_cases: In simple single-system games (e.g., pure puzzle games without spatial navigation or inventory), integration with other memory types is unnecessary as causal chains are self-contained.
If prerequisite conditions are always explicitly stated in the current observation, verification may be redundant as invalid actions will be obviously inapplicable.
When causal chains are only stored at high abstraction levels, they may not contain sufficient detail for fine-grained consistency checking with other memory systems.
In games with weak coupling between systems (changes in one system rarely affect others), integration overhead may exceed benefits.

supporting_evidence.text: Cognitive architectures like Soar and ACT-R demonstrate that multiple memory systems with different properties (procedural, declarative, episodic) must interact for complex behavior.
Planning systems that check action preconditions and maintain consistency between different knowledge types (spatial, object properties, agent capabilities) produce more reliable plans.
In text games, agents that maintain world models including spatial layout, inventory, and object locations outperform agents that only track action-reward pairs.
Embodied AI agents that integrate multiple modalities (vision, language, action) through explicit cross-modal grounding show better performance than unimodal agents.
Database systems with referential integrity constraints that maintain consistency across related tables provide analogous examples of multi-system integration.
Retrieval failures in case-based reasoning often occur when retrieved cases are applied in contexts where their preconditions are not satisfied, supporting the need for contextual verification.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Multi-memory-system integration in cognitive architectures, precondition checking in planning, world model maintenance in text game agents, and cross-modal grounding in embodied AI are established concepts.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific principle that causal memory must be bidirectionally integrated with other memory types through cross-references and consistency constraints, with quantified performance degradation (30-50%) from lack of integration, represents a novel specific formulation for text game causal memory systems.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While memory integration is recognized in cognitive architectures, this law specifically characterizes how causal memory should interact with other memory types in text game agents, with explicit architectural prescriptions and quantified integration importance.
references: Laird et al. (1987) Soar: An architecture for general intelligence [Multi-memory system integration]
Anderson (2007) How Can the Human Mind Occur in the Physical Universe? [ACT-R memory systems]
Ghallab et al. (2004) PDDL - The Planning Domain Definition Language [Precondition checking in planning]
Côté et al. (2018) TextWorld: A learning environment for text-based games [World model maintenance in text games]
Shridhar et al. (2020) ALFRED: A Benchmark for Interpreting Grounded Instructions for Everyday Tasks [Multi-modal integration in embodied AI]

new_predictions_likely: Agents with explicit causal chain encoding will show larger performance advantages (closer to 60% improvement) on games with longer causal dependencies (>10 steps) compared to games with shorter dependencies (3-5 steps, closer to 30% improvement).
Outcome-indexed retrieval will provide greater benefits in games with high initial state diversity but recurring outcome patterns (e.g., procedurally generated puzzles with fixed solution types).

Abstracted causal memory will enable transfer across thematically different games within the same genre (e.g., different fantasy adventure games with similar puzzle mechanics) with 40-60% success rate on transferred patterns.
The number of successfully abstracted and reused causal patterns will correlate positively with overall game completion rates (Pearson r > 0.6).
Agents that maintain multiple abstraction levels will adapt more quickly to new puzzles by trying progressively more specific matches until finding the appropriate abstraction level.
Integration of causal memory with spatial memory will reduce invalid action attempts by 50-70% compared to causal-only memory.
Fine-tuning the abstraction level (through meta-learning or manual specification) for each game will improve transfer performance by an additional 15-25% beyond fixed abstraction.
new_predictions_unknown: Whether causal chains can be effectively learned through pure self-supervised learning from game transcripts (without reward signals) or require reinforcement learning with outcome labels is unknown but would dramatically affect practical implementation feasibility and data requirements.

The interaction between causal memory and model-based planning—whether they provide redundant capabilities (both predict consequences) or complementary benefits (memory for retrieval, planning for novel situations)—could reshape architecture design toward hybrid or purely memory-based approaches.

Whether abstracted causal chain representations transfer across completely different game genres (e.g., from adventure games to mystery games to strategy games) or are genre-specific has major implications for building general game-playing agents versus specialized agents.

The optimal balance between storage cost (maintaining detailed causal chains), retrieval cost (searching large memory), and reasoning cost (adapting chains to new contexts) at scale (>1000 puzzles) is unknown and may favor different architectures than in small-scale settings.

How causal memory should handle stochastic or partially observable outcomes where the same action sequence sometimes succeeds and sometimes fails (e.g., probabilistic combat, hidden information puzzles) is uncertain—whether to store probability distributions, multiple outcome branches, or contextual modifiers.

Whether large language models' emergent causal reasoning capabilities can substitute for explicit causal memory structures or whether explicit structures still provide complementary benefits is unknown and has major implications for LLM-based agent design.

The extent to which counterfactual reasoning ('what if I had done X instead of Y at step 3?') over stored causal chains can improve future performance is unclear but could enable powerful credit assignment and exploration strategies.
negative_experiments: If simple flat state-action pair storage performs comparably to causal chain encoding on delayed-reward puzzle tasks (difference <10%), the Causal Chain Encoding Principle would be challenged and suggest the benefits don't justify the complexity.

If state-similarity-based retrieval performs as well as outcome-indexed retrieval on transfer tasks (difference <15%), the Outcome-Indexed Retrieval Law would need revision to identify when outcome indexing provides value.
If concrete instance storage enables as much transfer as abstracted patterns on puzzle variants (difference <20%), the Causal Abstraction and Generalization Principle would be questioned.
If applying causal chains without contextual verification doesn't significantly increase failure rates (<10% degradation), the Causal Memory Integration Principle would be challenged.

If the computational overhead of causal chain storage, retrieval, and reasoning outweighs performance benefits in terms of total game completion time or sample efficiency, the practical utility of the entire theory would be questioned.

If end-to-end trained neural agents without explicit causal memory structures match or exceed performance of causal-memory-augmented agents, it would suggest explicit causal structures are unnecessary.
If increasing abstraction level actually decreases transfer performance (negative correlation), it would challenge the fundamental assumption of the Causal Abstraction Principle.

unaccounted_for.text: How causal memory should handle contradictory experiences where similar actions lead to different outcomes in different contexts (e.g., a key that unlocks door A but not door B despite similar descriptions), requiring context discrimination.

The computational complexity and scalability of causal reasoning and retrieval when memory contains thousands of causal chains, and how to manage retrieval efficiency through indexing, pruning, or approximation.
How causal memory should be initialized or bootstrapped in new games where no prior experience exists, including potential transfer from out-of-domain experiences or pre-training.
The role of negative experiences (failed causal chains) in memory: whether to store them for avoidance, use them to identify preconditions that were missing, or discard them to save space.
How to automatically determine the appropriate abstraction level for each puzzle type without manual specification or extensive trial-and-error.
The interaction between causal memory and exploration strategy: whether causal memory should influence what actions to explore or only be used for exploitation.
How causal chains should represent and reason about temporal constraints (e.g., 'must complete step 2 within N turns of step 1') beyond just action ordering.
Whether and how causal memory should be updated or refined when later experiences contradict or refine earlier causal beliefs.

conflicting_evidence.text: Large language models (GPT-3, GPT-4) show emergent causal reasoning and planning capabilities in text games without explicit causal memory structures, suggesting that implicit representations in neural networks may be sufficient, potentially making explicit causal memory structures unnecessary overhead.

End-to-end deep reinforcement learning agents sometimes discover optimal solutions without explicit causal representation, suggesting that causal structure may emerge implicitly from experience rather than requiring explicit encoding, though this may require more samples.

Some research suggests that hierarchical abstraction in RL can be difficult to learn and may not always improve performance, potentially conflicting with the Causal Abstraction Principle's assumption that abstraction reliably benefits transfer.

In some cases, model-based RL (which learns general environment dynamics) outperforms episodic memory approaches, suggesting that forward models may be more effective than causal chain retrieval for some tasks.

special_cases: Games with purely reactive challenges (real-time combat, timing-based puzzles) may not benefit from causal chain memory focused on multi-step puzzle-solving, as these require different memory structures (e.g., pattern recognition, reflex-like responses).

Procedurally generated puzzles with no recurring patterns at all would limit the utility of outcome-indexed retrieval and abstraction, though causal encoding may still aid credit assignment within a single episode.
Games with full state observability and no hidden causal relationships (all consequences immediately visible) may not require sophisticated causal reasoning beyond simple model-based planning.

In games where dialogue and persuasion are primary mechanics rather than puzzle-solving, different memory organization (dialogue state tracking, NPC modeling) may be more important than causal chains.
Very short games (<100 actions total) may not have enough recurring patterns or complexity to justify the overhead of sophisticated causal memory structures.
Games with explicit instruction or tutorial systems that teach causal relationships may reduce the need for learning causal chains from experience.
In games designed with intentionally opaque or random mechanics (experimental or avant-garde interactive fiction), causal memory may not provide benefits as causal relationships may not be learnable.

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Causal reasoning in AI, case-based reasoning with episode retrieval, hierarchical reinforcement learning with options/skills for temporally extended actions, Hindsight Experience Replay for outcome-based learning, goal-conditioned RL, analogical transfer, and schema learning are established frameworks that address related problems.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory specifically integrates causal chain encoding with intermediate states, outcome-indexed retrieval with taxonomic organization, multi-level abstraction, and cross-memory-system integration into a unified framework for text game memory. The specific quantified relationships (performance improvements of 30-60%, 40-70%, 50-80%), architectural prescriptions for memory structure and retrieval, and explicit consideration of language-based interactive fiction characteristics represent novel contributions.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: While the theory draws heavily on established concepts from causal reasoning, hierarchical RL, case-based reasoning, and goal-conditioned learning, its specific formulation of how these principles should be integrated for language-model-based text game agents, with quantified benefits and explicit architectural prescriptions, represents a focused novel synthesis rather than mere application of existing work.

components.theory_query: Build a theory of how language models agents can be augmented with memory to play text games.
